These results demonstrate that model failures provide an effective and scalable source of targeted training signal for improving tool-using language model agents.

</details>


### [113] [PiDA: Phonetically-Informed Data Augmentation for Robust Vietnamese Speech Translation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12911)
*Giang Son Nguyen,Tung X. Nguyen,Hieu Minh Truong,Nhu Vo,Wray Buntine,Dung D. Le*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper addresses error propagation in Vietnamese Cascaded Speech Translation systems by categorizing and mitigating ASR phonetic errors and proposing a data augmentation approach that improves translation quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study is motivated by the issue of error propagation in cascaded Speech Translation systems, where errors in ASR outputs negatively affect subsequent Neural Machine Translation (NMT) outcomes.

Method: The paper categorizes ASR substitution errors based on phonetic causes, uses Linear Mixed-Effects Modelling to analyze their impact, and proposes a Phonetically-Informed Data Augmentation (PiDA) approach that simulates ASR errors using word embeddings and fine-tunes models on the augmented dataset.

Result: The PiDA approach significantly improves translation quality (+2.04 BLEU for erroneous ASR outputs) while also slightly enhancing clean-text performance.

Conclusion: Phonetic confusions are the main source of ASR errors in speech translation, and addressing these using PiDA can mitigate their negative impact, improving both noisy and clean translation outputs.

Abstract: Cascaded speech translation (ST) systems suffer from error propagation when Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) outputs incorrect transcripts. We present the first systematic categorization of ASR errors for Vietnamese ST, classifying substitution errors by phonetic cause and quantifying their impact on downstream Neural Machine Translation (NMT) performance using Linear Mixed-Effects Modelling. We confirm that most ASR substitution errors arise from phonetic confusions rather than random noise, and that these phonetic errors significantly degrade ST quality. Motivated by this finding, we propose Phonetically-Informed Data Augmentation (PiDA), which generates ASR-like corruptions by substituting words with phonetically similar alternatives using phonetic word embeddings. Fine-tuning on a PiDA-augmented version of FLEURS Vietnamese-English improves translation of erroneous ASR outputs (up to +2.04 BLEU over standard fine-tuning) while also slightly improving clean-text performance.

</details>


### [114] [Polar: A Benchmark for Evaluating Political Bias in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12922)
*Sangho Kim,Heejin Kim,Yoonhee Park,Hyunggeun Jeon,Jaejin Lee*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Polar, a benchmark to measure the political bias of large language models (LLMs) systematically across various political and linguistic contexts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The increasing significance of political bias in LLMs coupled with the difficulty of measuring it uniformly motivated the creation of a reproducible approach to assess this bias.

Method: The authors developed Polar, a 4,026-instance multiple-choice benchmark that evaluates political bias through option likelihoods rather than generation and applied it across two ideological axes, eight issue categories, and two linguistic contexts (U.S. and South Korea).

Result: The study found systematic variation in measured bias based on political context, issue category, model group, and language, with U.S. content leaning left-progressive while South Korean content exhibited a mixed pattern. Translation experiments also revealed the influence of presentation language on political bias.

Conclusion: Cross-contextual and multilingual evaluation is essential to effectively measure and understand political bias in LLMs as biases vary significantly across contexts and languages.

Abstract: Political bias in large language models (LLMs) is increasingly significant, but difficult to measure reproducibly across political and linguistic contexts. We introduce Polar, a 4,026-instance multiple-choice benchmark that measures political bias through option-level likelihoods rather than prompt-based generation. Polar covers two ideological axes and eight issue categories derived from the Manifesto Project, and evaluates models in parallel across U.S. and South Korean political contexts. Across 38 LLMs, measured bias varies systematically with political context, issue category, model group, and presentation language. All models lean left-progressive on U.S. political content, but show more centered and mixed patterns on South Korean content. Translation experiments further show that presentation language alone can shift measured bias. These findings highlight the need for multilingual and cross-contextual evaluation of political bias in LLMs.

</details>


### [115] [Multi-Turn Reasoning When Context Arrives in Pieces: Scalable Sharding and Memory-Augmented RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12941)
*Shu Tong Luo,Wenqin Liu,Rui Liu,Mingming Gong,Jiaxian Guo*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper addresses LLM performance drop in multi-turn conversations by training with a compact rolling memory, improving multi-turn reasoning and zero-shot generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address a significant drop in LLM accuracy during multi-turn conversations despite full context availability, and to improve the robustness and efficiency of reasoning within such interactions.

Method: Introduces a scalable sharding pipeline to convert single-turn QA datasets to multi-turn episodes and trains models with a memory-augmented policy that uses a compact rolling memory instead of growing history.

Result: The memory-augmented policy improves multi-turn accuracy, generalizes zero-shot to harder math and out-of-domain questions, and outperforms full-history baselines even when full context is available.

Conclusion: Training models to maintain compact rolling memory enhances reasoning, scalability, and generalization in multi-turn contexts, outperforming traditional methods based on full-history attendance.

Abstract: When a user reveals task-critical information across several conversation turns, LLM accuracy drops by up to 65% despite full context availability. We show that this Lost in Conversation degradation can be substantially mitigated by training models to maintain a compact rolling memory instead of attending to a growing history. To make such training scalable, we introduce a low-cost sharding pipeline that converts single-turn QA datasets into multi-turn fragmented-information episodes, eliminating the need for hours of manual annotation. Training only on sharded GSM8K, our memory-augmented policy significantly improves multi-turn accuracy and generalises zero-shot to harder math and out-of-domain long-context QA. Moreover, memory-trained models outperform full-history baselines even when given the full history at test time, suggesting that learning to compress induces more robust incremental reasoning than full-context exposure alone.

</details>


### [116] [SkillChain: Closing the Loop on Skill Evolution for Image-Based E-Commerce AI Assistants](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12984)
*Yimin Hu,Mengtao Xu,Hao Guo,Yuheng Song,Xiaoyong Zhu,Bo Zheng*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces SkillChain, a system that automates skill evolution in LLM-based image assistants for e-commerce, addressing intent diversity and improving response quality significantly.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The researchers aimed to solve issues with LLM-based AI systems in e-commerce image assistants, where varied user intents like product search or style recommendation demand customized responses. Manual engineering fails due to the dynamic and broad intent space.

Method: The proposed solution, SkillChain, automates the lifecycle of AI skills in three phases: Skill Creator for initializing tasks, Route Optimizer for aligning routing, and Body Refiner for refining tasks using LLM-Judge dual evaluation.

Result: In production-scale deployment, SkillChain significantly enhances response quality in terms of structural compliance and content quality. An A/B test demonstrated increased engagement, content usage, and user retention.

Conclusion: SkillChain effectively addresses the challenges of heterogeneous user intents in e-commerce image-based assistants by automating skill development and achieving measurable improvements in user interaction and satisfaction.

Abstract: Image-based AI assistants are now deployed at production scale on e-commerce platforms, where a single uploaded image can trigger fundamentally different user intents: product search, style recommendation, visual encyclopedia, or utility tool calls, each demanding its own response format, tool invocation, and domain knowledge. Without per-intent behavioral constraints, LLM-based systems conflate these heterogeneous modes and fall short of domain quality standards, while the breadth and dynamism of the intent space render manual engineering infeasible. To address this, we present SkillChain, which closes the production feedback loop on Skill evolution, automating the lifecycle of Skills through three stages: Skill Creator for bootstrapping from task specs and trajectories, Route Optimizer for routing alignment, and Body Refiner for iterative Skill Body refinement via dual-path LLM-Judge evaluation. Deployed on a production-scale e-commerce image assistant, SkillChain substantially improves aggregate response quality, with the strongest gains on structural compliance and content quality; a one-week online A/B experiment further confirms significant gains in user engagement, content consumption, and long-term retention.

</details>


### [117] [No Hidden Prompts Needed! You Can Game AI Peer Review with Presentation-Only Revisions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13044)
*Xu Yang,Zhizhou Sha,Junbo Li,Jian Yu,Yifan Sun,Matthew Zhao,Jinrui Fang,Xinyue Guo,Yining Wu,Xu Hu,Yifu Luo,Qiang Liu,Zhangyang Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper addresses vulnerabilities in AI review systems, focusing on how adversarially crafted presentation edits, without altering scientific content, can manipulate review outcomes effectively.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore and highlight the overlooked risks posed by presentation-level manipulations in papers evaluated by AI reviewers, beyond explicit attacks like prompt injection.

Method: The study introduces adversarial repackaging, a method of iteratively refining paper presentations using AI-reviewer feedback while keeping core scientific content static. Experiments were conducted using three mainstream AI reviewers.

Result: Adversarial repackaging enhances paper scores significantly, achieving a 75.1% attack success rate and a mean score increase of +1.21/10, with deeper edits (e.g., reframing contributions) outperforming superficial changes.

Conclusion: AI reviewers are vulnerable to presentation-level manipulation, misinterpreting the appearance of addressing limitations as substantive improvements. Deployment of such systems requires safeguards against these presentation-oriented optimizations.

Abstract: As AI-generated reviews move from experimental tools into peer-review infrastructure, most robustness concerns have focused on explicit attacks such as hidden instructions and prompt injection. We study a harder and more policy-relevant failure mode: no hidden text, no prompt injection, and no changes to methods, experiments, figures, equations, proofs, or numerical results. The attacker modifies only presentation-level content, such as the abstract, contribution framing, related work, discussion, and narrative structure. We introduce adversarial repackaging: a closed-loop attack that uses AI-reviewer feedback to search for presentation-level revisions while keeping the scientific evidence fixed. Across three mainstream AI reviewers, adversarial repackaging achieves a 75.1% attack success rate and a mean score gain of +1.21/10. The effect is not explained by ordinary prose polishing. We also reveal that strategies that change how the reviewer interprets the paper, such as related-work repositioning and analytical discussion expansion, substantially outperform surface edits such as local polishing, table formatting, and algorithm boxes.
  Our analysis reveals two deeper structural failure modes. First, AI reviewers are easier to impress than to convince: highlighting strengths reliably increases perceived merit, while attempts to dissolve weaknesses frequently backfire. Second, AI reviewers can confuse the appearance of addressing a limitation with actually resolving it, allowing unchanged evidence to be reinterpreted as stronger scientific contribution. These results show that the deployment risk is not only malicious hidden instructions, but the emergence of paper presentation itself as an optimization surface. We release a contamination-free rolling benchmark and attack framework for testing whether AI reviewers remain anchored to scientific content under presentation-only edits.

</details>


### [118] [sebis at CRF Filling 2026: A Two-Stage Local LLM Pipeline for Medical CRF Filling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13082)
*Katharina Sommer,Tristan Till,Florian Matthes*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a privacy-conscious, locally hosted pipeline utilizing the MedGemma-27B model tailored for clinical NLP tasks, achieving competitive performance in structured data extraction from EHR notes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current clinical NLP tasks rely heavily on LLMs, which face challenges like privacy concerns, hallucinations, and high inference costs. The study aims to develop a practical, deterministic, and privacy-preserving framework.

Method: A two-stage local architecture separates binary presence classification and value extraction, ensuring strict adherence to textual evidence. The solution uses item-specific, few-shot in-context learning without external APIs.

Result: The proposed method achieved a macro-F1 score of 0.55 in the CL4Health 2026 task, ranking second among local, open-source submissions.

Conclusion: Locally-hosted, privacy-preserving NLP pipelines using LLMs can achieve performance close to top proprietary models while ensuring data sovereignty.

Abstract: The extraction of structured clinical information from unstructured EHR notes is a persistent bottleneck in healthcare informatics. While large language models (LLMs) offer high performance, their deployment in clinical settings is hindered by privacy risks, inference costs, and the tendency to hallucinate beyond textual evidence. We address these challenges for the CL4Health 2026 Case Report Form (CRF) filling task by proposing a fully local, domain-adapted pipeline using the MedGemma-27B model. Our two-stage architecture, which separates binary presence classification from value extraction, enforces strict adherence to textual evidence and ensures deterministic outputs for negated, uncertain, or unknown states. By leveraging item-specific, few-shot in-context learning without external API calls or fine-tuning, our approach achieves a macro-F1 score of 0.55 on the official English test track. This result secures second place among all locally-hosted, open-source submissions. Our work demonstrates that privacy-preserving, on-premise LLM pipelines can achieve near-competitive performance with proprietary frontier models, providing a practical, data-sovereign framework for clinical NLP.

</details>


### [119] [LEDGER: A Long-Context Benchmark of Corporate Annual Reports for Grounded Financial Retrieval and Extraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13100)
*Charles Moslonka,Amaury de Vitry,Arthur Garnier,Hicham Randrianarivo,Emmanuel Malherbe*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces LEDGER, a dataset and benchmark for long-context financial document evaluation, offering three types of tasks and tools for KPI extraction and analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the need for rigorous evaluation of long-context capabilities of large language models in financial reporting, which goes beyond the limitations of current datasets relying on plain SEC filings and simple QA tasks.

Method: Created LEDGER, a dataset of 4,999 corporate annual reports labeled with 31 financial KPIs, along with three evaluation benchmarks (page-level retrieval, single-value lookup, and full KPI extraction) and tools for data processing and scoring.

Result: Introduced a dataset and benchmarks enabling nuanced evaluation of model performance in extracting financial insights. A case study links CEO rhetoric to market impact, showcasing the utility of the dataset.

Conclusion: LEDGER provides a comprehensive resource for evaluating large language models on complex, long-format financial documents, offering significant insights and a foundation for future research in this field.

Abstract: Finance reporting is a natural proving ground for large language models, and the very-long-context capabilities of recent models across all sizes make rigorous evaluation in this domain an increasingly pressing need. Yet most public financial resources reduce the task to plain-text SEC 10-K filings paired with a handful of question-answer items. We release LEDGER (Long-context Evaluation of Documents for Grounded Extraction and Retrieval), a corpus of 4,999 digitized corporate annual reports - full documents with figures, tables, and narrative, not just regulatory filings. Each report is labeled with 31 consolidated financial KPIs to be extracted and linked to the market's reaction at the earnings date. From this data we derive three evaluation benchmarks spanning the difficulty spectrum: a pure page-level KPI retrieval task with TREC-style relevance judgments over 118,048 questions in natural language, a conversational "needle-in-a-haystack" single-value lookup, and a full KPI extraction task, both from long, numerically dense reports. We additionally provide human OCR-quality annotations with inter-annotator agreement and the complete extraction, validation, and scoring toolchain. We further demonstrate the dataset's research utility with a case study linking CEO-letter rhetoric to post-publication market impact.

</details>


### [120] [MÖVE: A Holistic LLM Benchmark for the German Public Sector](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13111)
*Camilla Dalerci,Thilo Michael,Robin Schaefer,Daniel Weinland*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces MÖVE, a new benchmark tailored for evaluating large language models (LLMs) within the German public administration context, emphasizing both task performance and governance standards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current benchmarks are often English- and US-centric, and fail to address the specific needs of German public administration, where LLM adoption is increasing and model selection practices lack systematic rigor.

Method: MÖVE evaluates 39 LLMs across performance (summarization, question answering, topic extraction) and governance criteria (hallucination, energy efficiency, transparency, alignment with values and political knowledge). It uses ten German-language datasets and multi-metric strategies, including advanced NLP metrics and LLM-as-a-judge methods.

Result: Findings show no single LLM excels universally; performance varies by task, and model size is not a definitive quality indicator. Additionally, the benchmark's methodology is tested for statistical reliability, dataset influence, and energy estimate validity.

Conclusion: MÖVE provides a comprehensive and adaptable framework for selecting LLMs in Germany's public sector, bridging a critical gap in localized, multilingual model evaluation. The benchmark is designed for continuous updates and public accessibility.

Abstract: We present MÖVE (Modelle für die Öffentliche Verwaltung Evaluieren), a holistic benchmark for evaluating large language models (LLMs) in the context of the German public sector. While LLMs are increasingly adopted in public administration, model selection remains largely ad hoc, and existing benchmarks offer limited guidance: they are predominantly English-centric, US-centric in content, and focus exclusively on task performance. MÖVE addresses these gaps by evaluating 39 models across two complementary dimensions. Performance criteria cover summarization, question answering, and topic extraction. Governance criteria assess hallucination tendencies, energy consumption, provider transparency, and alignment with German constitutional values and knowledge about positions by German political parties. In total, we utilize ten German-language datasets, including gold- and silverstandard datasets that we constructed to reflect public-administration domains. We employ a multi-metric evaluation strategy combining classical NLP metrics, embedding-based methods, and LLM-as-a-judge approaches. Our results show that no single model dominates across all criteria: top performers differ between tasks, and model size alone is a poor predictor of quality. We further evaluate the benchmark itself, analyzing its statistical precision, LLM judge reliability, the impact of our private datasets on model rankings, the sensitivity of our results to prompt formulation, and the validity of our energy consumption estimates. MÖVE is designed as a living benchmark under active development; results are publicly available at https://moeve.bundesdruckerei.de/.

</details>


### [121] [G-Long: Graph-Enhanced Memory Management for Efficient Long-Term Dialogue Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13115)
*Minjun Choi,Yoonjin Jang,Sangwon Youn,Youngjoong Ko*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces G-Long, a graph-enhanced framework designed to improve consistency in long-term dialogue systems by utilizing a small Language Model for structured memory retrieval and reducing computational costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aimed to overcome the challenges of maintaining long-term consistency in open-domain dialogue systems due to the inefficiencies of long-context reasoning and high computational costs of current models.

Method: G-Long employs a fine-tuned small Language Model for structured triplet extraction and associative retrieval, combined with a novel attention-aware importance scoring using T5 summarizer's cross-attention signals.

Result: G-Long achieves state-of-the-art performance, with up to 9.8% improvement in response quality on MSC and 40.8% improvement in retrieval recall on LME benchmarks, while reducing computational requirements.

Conclusion: The results validate G-Long's potential to enhance dialogue systems by addressing both cost-efficiency and long-term memory retrieval challenges, marking a significant advancement in the field.

Abstract: While Large Language Models (LLMs) have advanced open-domain dialogue systems, maintaining long-term consistency remains a challenge due to inherent limitations in long-context reasoning and the inefficiency of processing extensive raw text. Existing approaches typically rely on either unstructured memory storage, which is prone to information loss, or computationally expensive LLMs that incur high latency. To address these limitations, we propose G-Long, a graph-enhanced framework that utilizes a fine-tuned small Language Model (sLM) for structured triplet extraction and associative retrieval, significantly reducing operational costs. Furthermore, we introduce the novel attention-aware importance scoring mechanism that leverages the intrinsic cross-attention signals of a T5 summarizer to identify salient memories. Extensive experiments across diverse benchmarks demonstrate that G-Long achieves state-of-the-art performance in both response generation and memory retrieval, yielding performance gains of up to 9.8% in response quality on MSC and 40.8% in retrieval recall on LME, while significantly minimizing computational overhead.

</details>


### [122] [EvoBrowseComp: Benchmarking Search Agents on Evolving Knowledge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13120)
*Yunhan Wang,Jiaan Wang,Lianzhe Huang,Xianfeng Zeng,Fandong Meng*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: EvoBrowseComp is a dynamic benchmark for evaluating search agents' browsing abilities, ensuring data freshness and contamination-free context.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current benchmarks fail to gauge genuine retrieval capabilities due to reliance on static knowledge, leading to memorization-based shortcuts.

Method: Developed a three-agent framework for automated synthesis: QA synthesis agent retrieves fresh data, filtering agent ensures credibility and prevents shortcuts, and guidance agent structures questions via reasoning graphs.

Result: Produced 800 complex, dynamic questions in English and Chinese, challenging search agents to conduct extensive knowledge traversal.

Conclusion: EvoBrowseComp sets a new standard for evolving, contamination-free benchmarks that align with advancing models and real-world knowledge.

Abstract: Search Agents -- large language models augmented with search tools -- have intensified the need for future-proof evaluation benchmarks. Existing benchmarks such as BrowseComp rely on static knowledge, making them vulnerable to test-set contamination and parametric memorization. Consequently, models can achieve high scores through fact recall rather than genuine retrieval, obscuring true browsing competence via reasoning shortcuts.
  In this paper, we introduce EvoBrowseComp, an evolving benchmark of 400 English and 400 Chinese contamination-free complex questions synthesized via live-web traversal. To collect these questions, we design a three-agent collaborative framework: (1) a QA synthesis agent that retrieves fresh knowledge from the live web to synthesize QA pairs; (2) an information filtering agent that filters retrieved knowledge in terms of credibility and popularity to block parametric shortcuts; and (3) a high-level guidance agent that formalizes questions into reasoning graphs to reduce logical redundancy and shortcuts in synthesized QA pairs. Because the framework supports fully automated synthesis, EvoBrowseComp can be regularly updated to prevent data contamination and maintain temporal freshness. Extensive experiments confirm its great difficulty, requiring broad horizontal search. It establishes a scalable paradigm for auto-updatable, high-difficulty benchmarking that keeps pace with both evolving world knowledge and advancing agent capabilities.

</details>


### [123] [NaturalFlow: Reducing Disruptive Pauses for Natural Speech Flow in Simultaneous Speech-to-Speech Translation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13121)
*Dongwook Lee,Youngho Cho,Sangkwon Park,Heeseung Kim,Sungroh Yoon*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a framework for achieving natural speech flow and low latency in simultaneous speech-to-speech translation, addressing issues with unnatural pauses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce the high latency of consecutive speech translation while ensuring natural acoustic flow, minimizing cognitive load on listeners.

Method: A fluency-aware optimization framework that uses model-internal signals like linguistic diversity and temporal variability to minimize inter-chunk silences.

Result: The framework balances low latency, natural flow, and competitive translation quality demonstrated on short- and long-form benchmarks.

Conclusion: This framework achieves a balance between simultaneous and consecutive translation advantages, improving the listener experience without sacrificing translation performance.

Abstract: Simultaneous speech-to-speech translation aims to enable near-real-time communication by minimizing latency, offering a compelling, real-time alternative to the high latency of consecutive translation. However, the excessive pursuit of low latency often results in fragmented chunk-wise speech. Consequently, listeners are subjected to an unnatural acoustic flow punctuated by frequent pauses, which could increase their cognitive load. To bridge this gap, we introduce a fluency-aware optimization framework designed to discover the sweet spot between the low-latency benefits of simultaneous translation and the natural flow of consecutive translation. Our framework minimizes inter-chunk silences by leveraging model-internal signals, including linguistic diversity and induced temporal variability in speech durations. Experiments on short- and long-form benchmarks show that our framework produces natural speech flow while maintaining competitive latency and translation quality.

</details>


### [124] [HyPE: Category-Aware Hypergraph Encoding with Persistent Edge Embeddings for Persona-Grounded Dialogue](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13142)
*Sangwon Youn,Yoonjin Jang,Youngjoong Ko*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes HyPE, a hypergraph-based framework for persona encoding in dialogue systems, offering superior performance over existing flat sentence-level baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing persona-grounded dialogue systems fail to model higher-order relations among persona attributes and treat personas as simple sets of sentences.

Method: The HyPE framework encodes persona elements as (Core, Expression, Sentiment, Category) quadruples and organizes them into a hypergraph. A HyperGCN neural network propagates this structure to condition the response generator, complemented by Persistent Edge Embeddings for per-category learnable priors.

Result: HyPE outperforms sentence-level pooling baselines across various backbones (GPT-2, LLaMA-3.2-3B, Qwen2.5-3B) on the PersonaChat dataset.

Conclusion: Incorporating structured hyperedge-level persona encoding results in enhanced performance and proves transferable across model scales.

Abstract: Persona-grounded dialogue systems aim to produce responses consistent with a speaker's persona, yet existing methods treat personas as a flat set of sentences and fail to model the high-order relations among persona attributes-e.g., that several persona sentences share a topical category. We propose HyPE (Hypergraph Persona Encoder), a framework that (i) analyzes each persona-bearing text as a (Core, Expression, Sentiment, Category) quadruple, and (ii) organizes persona elements into a hypergraph whose hyperedges are induced by shared category labels. An HyperGCN hypergraph neural network propagates this structure into a persona summary vector and a soft-memory bank that condition the response generator. We further propose Persistent Edge Embeddings (PEE), lightweight per-category learnable priors fused into the HyperGCN message-passing step. On PersonaChat under greedy decoding, HyPE consistently outperforms sentence-level pooling baselines across GPT-2, LLaMA-3.2-3B, and Qwen2.5-3B backbones by demonstrating that structured hyperedge-level persona encoding provides a transferable advantage across model scales.

</details>


### [125] [NTS-CoT: Mitigating Hallucinations in LLM-based News Timeline Summarization with Chain-of-Thought Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13171)
*Feng Lyu,Huiqin Yan,Sijing Duan,Hao Wu,Shuang Gu,Xue Qiao,Weixu Zhang,Haolun Wu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The study addresses hallucination issues in timeline summarization (TLS) with a novel framework called NTS-CoT that uses Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The research is motivated by the challenges of hallucinations in LLM-based TLS, where generated content either deviates from source news or omits crucial information, a topic not well-explored in prior works.

Method: The proposed NTS-CoT framework employs three modules: Element-CoT for faithful news summarization, Date Selection for temporal saliency, and Causal-CoT to infer causal relationships, minimizing hallucinations.

Result: Experimental results on three TLS benchmarks, including human evaluations, show that NTS-CoT outperforms existing methods by effectively reducing hallucinations and enhancing TLS performance.

Conclusion: NTS-CoT demonstrates its efficacy in tackling hallucinations in TLS and sets a new benchmark for accurate and reliable summarization in timeline tracking.

Abstract: The rapid updates of online news make tracking event developments challenging, highlighting the need for timeline summarization (TLS). Hallucinations, where LLM-generated content deviates from source news, still remain a critical issue in LLM-based TLS and are not well studied in existing works. To bridge this gap, we identify two primary types of hallucinations: unfaithful content during news summarization and information omission in date-event summarization. Then, we propose NTS-CoT, a novel framework that leverages Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning to mitigate hallucinations in TLS. The framework consists of three key modules: i) Element-CoT to capture essential news elements for faithful summarization, ii) Date Selection to combine temporal saliency and event prominence for timestamp selection, and iii) Causal-CoT to infer causal relationships and reduce omissions in date-event summarization. Extensive experiments, including quantitative analysis on three TLS benchmarks and human evaluation, demonstrate that NTS-CoT outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, effectively mitigating hallucinations and improving LLM-based TLS performance. Our source code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/NTS-CoT .

</details>


### [126] [MemRefine: LLM-Guided Compression for Long-Term Agent Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13177)
*Minjae Kim,Jinheon Baek,Soyeong Jeong,Sung Ju Hwang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: MemRefine is an LLM-guided framework designed to manage memory stores within fixed storage budgets by refining memory entries without compromising downstream performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As long-term interactions accumulate in LLMs, memory stores grow uncontrollably, leading to inefficient storage and retrieval issues on resource-constrained platforms. There is a need for systematic memory management to operate within fixed budgets while preserving essential information.

Method: MemRefine uses surface similarity to identify candidate memory pairs and an LLM judge to make decisions on deleting, merging, or preserving entries based on factual content. This iterative process ensures that memory stores meet storage budgets without major quality loss.

Result: MemRefine achieves compliance with storage budgets while maintaining or enhancing performance across various memory frameworks and long-term conversation benchmarks, outperforming rule-based baselines under tight constraints.

Conclusion: The proposed MemRefine framework offers an effective solution for memory management in budget-constrained LLMs by leveraging advanced LLM reasoning and achieving strong results in performance preservation.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents are increasingly expected to operate over long-term interactions, where information from past dialogues must be preserved and recalled to support future tasks. However, as interactions accumulate, the memory store grows without bound and fills with redundant entries that inflate storage cost and degrade retrieval by crowding out the most useful evidence. Furthermore, this is especially limiting on resource-constrained platforms with hard memory budgets, motivating us to formulate storage-budgeted memory management, the task of keeping an already constructed memory store within a fixed budget while preserving information useful for future interactions. To this end, we then propose MemRefine, an LLM-guided framework that, since surface similarity poorly reflects factual value, uses similarity only to propose candidate pairs and defers delete, merge, and preserve decisions to an LLM judge based on factual content, iterating until the budget is met. Across multiple memory frameworks and long-term conversation benchmarks, MemRefine consistently meets target budgets while preserving downstream performance and outperforming rule-based baselines under tight budgets.

</details>


### [127] [LAUKIN: A Multi-jurisdictional Common Law Contract Dataset](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13184)
*Amrita Singh,Aditya Joshi,Jiaojiao Jiang,Hye-young Paik,May Fong Cheong*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces LAUKIN, a dataset for cross-jurisdictional legal clause equivalence, containing labelled and unlabelled clause pairs from contracts across Australia, UK, and India.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of legal NLP datasets that handle cross-jurisdictional contract review, a critical need for multinational companies.

Method: A multi-stage retrieval and reranking pipeline was developed to identify clause pairs, followed by expert annotation for equivalence. LAUKIN contains 14,727 clause pairs, 3,000 of which are manually annotated.

Result: Using 12 models and 4 techniques, the best model achieved a macro-F1 score of 65.11%, demonstrating the dataset's significance and difficulty in classifying legal equivalences across jurisdictions.

Conclusion: LAUKIN establishes a challenging benchmark for legal NLP and highlights the complexity of classifying legal equivalence due to drafting variations across jurisdictions.

Abstract: Multinational companies increasingly require cross-jurisdictional contract review, yet existing legal NLP datasets are largely restricted to a single jurisdiction. We introduce LAUKIN (Legal equivalence dataset of Australia, UK, and INdia), a dataset of clause pairs (AU-UK, UK-IN, IN-AU) labelled for boolean legal equivalence. We develop a novel multi-stage retrieval and reranking pipeline to construct the initial clause pair mapping, with a subset of clause pairs subsequently annotated by legal experts as Equivalent or Not Equivalent. The dataset comprises 14,727 clause pairs from 204 contracts across 8 agreement types, of which 3,000 are manually labelled: 900 train, 600 dev, and 1,500 test. We evaluate 12 models across 4 techniques, achieving a best macro-F1 of 65.11%, establishing LAUKIN as a challenging benchmark. Results reveal that, despite shared legal heritage, drafting conventions diverge significantly across jurisdictions, making cross-jurisdictional equivalence classification non-trivial. LAUKIN also includes 11,727 unlabelled training pairs to support future semi-supervised learning research in legal NLP.

</details>


### [128] [A Context-Aware Dataset for Stance Detection in Bioethical Controversies on Reddit](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13187)
*Hu Huang,Genan Dai,Fuqiang Niu,Yi Yang,Zhaoya Gong,Bowen Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces BioStance, a dataset of annotated Post-Comment pairs from Reddit bioethical discussions, aiming to support research on context-aware discourse modeling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address the shortage of large-scale, domain-specific resources for understanding context-dependent bioethical debates on social media.

Method: The paper describes the creation of BioStance, a dataset with 39,600 annotated Post-Comment pairs on six bioethical targets. Hierarchical discourse context and three-class labeling (Favor, Against, None) were applied, achieving substantial reliability (Krippendorff's $α$ = 0.82).

Result: BioStance successfully provides a high-quality, context-rich dataset, enabling computational research on stance detection, argument mining, and discourse analysis in bioethical debates.

Conclusion: The BioStance dataset enriches the tools available for studying social media discussions in bioethics, creating new opportunities for context-aware research.

Abstract: Bioethical debates increasingly unfold on social media, yet stance detection research lacks large-scale, domain-specific resources for modeling such context-dependent discourse. We present BioStance, a context-aware dataset of 39,600 annotated Post-Comment pairs from Reddit bioethical discussions. BioStance covers six controversial targets across three dimensions of bioethical controversy: fundamental value conflicts, individual liberty versus collective responsibility, and technological uncertainty. Each instance preserves hierarchical conversational context and is labeled by three independent annotators using a three-class stance scheme: Favor, Against, and None. The annotations achieve a mean Krippendorff's $α$ of 0.82, indicating substantial reliability. By combining thematic diversity, conversational structure, and high-quality human annotation, BioStance supports research on context-aware stance detection, argument mining, and computational analysis of bioethical discourse.

</details>


### [129] [SICI: A Semantic-Pragmatic Complexity Index Reveals Regime Shifts in LLM Stance Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13189)
*Fuqiang Niu,Bowen Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces the Stance Inference Complexity Index (SICI) to measure the complexity of stance detection tasks for large language models (LLMs), revealing patterns in errors and the limitations of intervention methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prompt-based large language models (LLMs) are widely used for stance detection, but difficult examples remain unresolved even with advanced techniques. There is a need to better understand and diagnose the factors impacting LLM performance.

Method: The authors introduced SICI, a seven-dimensional diagnostic metric to quantify the semantic-pragmatic complexity of target-text pairs. They analyzed its predictive power for LLM accuracy and conducted a 15-method intervention study to observe changes in LLM behavior across complexity levels.

Result: SICI effectively predicts LLM accuracy and uncovers a phase-transition-like error structure, ranging from over-attribution on low-complexity tasks to abstention on high-complexity ones. Despite improvements with stronger models and interventions, high-complexity challenges persist.

Conclusion: SICI provides a robust diagnostic framework for stance detection in LLMs, highlighting that current intervention methods have limited success in addressing complexity bottlenecks, and further research is needed to handle high-complexity tasks.

Abstract: Prompt-based LLMs are increasingly used for stance detection, but harder examples are not always repaired by clearer instructions, reasoning prompts, retrieval, or debate. We introduce SICI (Stance Inference Complexity Index), a seven-dimensional diagnostic measure of the semantic-pragmatic burden imposed by a target--text pair. Across SemEval-2016 and VAST, SICI predicts LLM accuracy better than surface proxies and shows substantial cross-scorer reliability ($α=0.771$). More importantly, LLM errors change regime as SICI increases: low-complexity examples invite over-attribution, especially Against predictions; intermediate examples form an unstable boundary; and high-complexity examples rapidly concentrate on None. This phase-transition-like structure persists across GPT-3.5, GPT-4o-mini, DeepSeek-V3, and GPT-4o, although stronger models move the boundaries. A 15-method intervention study further shows that prompting, retrieval, and debate often shift models along the attribution--abstention axis rather than removing the high-complexity bottleneck.

</details>


### [130] [Layer-Resolved Optimal Transport for Hallucination Detection in NMT and Abstractive Summarization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13216)
*Mariia Onyshchuk,Maksym-Vasyl Tarnavskyi,Marta Sumyk*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The study explores using optimal transport (OT) to detect hallucinations in neural machine translation (NMT) and tests its applicability to abstractive summarization faithfulness detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to analyze and extend OT-based approaches for detecting hallucinations in NMT, and to test whether these methods generalize to abstractive summarization faithfulness detection.

Method: The study evaluates OT-based metrics across decoder layers in NMT, comparing performance against standard supervised approaches. It also conducts structural experiments on T5-base to study attention dynamics.

Result: OT-based detectors effectively identify hallucinations in NMT but show limited capacity in identifying unfaithful summaries in abstractive summarization when attention remains correctly aligned.

Conclusion: OT on cross-attention is a reliable hallucination detector for tasks where failures link to source disengagement, but it is inherently limited for downstream content faithfulness assessment.

Abstract: Optimal transport (OT) has been shown to detect hallucinations in neural machine translation (NMT) by measuring the geometric distance between cross-attention distributions and a reference distribution, without any supervision. We extend this analysis to all six decoder layers of the Fairseq DE-EN model ($N=3{,}414$), showing that Wass-to-Unif and Wass-to-Data are complementary detectors specialised across hallucination types, that detection is concentrated in layers L1--L4 with L5 anti-predictive for subtler types, and that hallucinated translations lack the exploratory attention phase present in correct translations from the first decoding step. We further evaluate whether the geometric signal transfers to abstractive summarization faithfulness detection: our unsupervised OT detector on AggreFact ($N=1{,}116$) achieves $57.2\%$/$57.6\%$ balanced accuracy on CNN/XSum -- above chance but substantially below supervised MiniCheck-Flan-T5-L($69.9\%$/$74.3\%$). This gap is principled: unlike NMT hallucinations, unfaithful summaries can attend correctly to source tokens while misrepresenting their content, a failure mode invisible to concentration-based OT metrics by construction. Structural experiments on T5-base confirm consistent decoder organisation across depth, with Layer~3 showing peak concentration and Layer~12 being most critical for generation quality. Together, the results establish OT on cross-attention as a reliable detector when the failure mode is source disengagement, a principled interpretability tool regardless of task, and fundamentally limited when faithfulness failures occur downstream of attention.

</details>


### [131] [When Similar Means Different: Evaluating LLMs on Arabic--Hebrew Cognates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13218)
*Junhong Liang,Noor Abo Mokh,Bashar Alhafni*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces SemCog Bench, a benchmark for evaluating the semantic understanding of Arabic-Hebrew word pairs in large language models (LLMs). It reveals LLM performance limitations in handling false friends and loanwords despite sentence-level context.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the challenge posed by the substantial overlap of Arabic and Hebrew lexicons, aiming to understand and improve cross-lingual semantic understanding in LLMs.

Method: Researchers curated a benchmark with 1,858 Arabic-Hebrew word pairs, annotated for cognate identification and semantic disambiguation, and tested multiple LLMs under diverse input representations and contexts.

Result: LLMs showed high accuracy for true cognates but struggled with false friends and loanwords, reflecting reliance on surface-form similarity. Sentence-level context yielded modest improvement.

Conclusion: SemCog Bench highlights a significant limitation in current LLMs regarding cross-lingual reasoning and semantic processing, making it a valuable benchmark for future multilingual research.

Abstract: Arabic and Hebrew, as closely related Semitic languages, share a substantial lexicon of true cognates, misleading false friends, and modern loanwords. This overlap poses a challenge for cross-lingual semantic understanding in large language models (LLMs). To evaluate this capability, we introduce SemCog Bench, a curated benchmark of 1,858 Arabic--Hebrew word pairs with sentence-level annotations for cognate identification and semantic disambiguation. We evaluate open-source and commercial LLMs across multiple input representations (raw, diacritized, Romanized, and phonetic) and reveal a critical gap in cross-lingual reasoning. While models achieve high accuracy on true cognates, performance drops sharply on false friends and loanwords, reflecting a strong reliance on surface-form similarity. Furthermore, sentence-level context yields only modest improvements, suggesting that contextual cues alone are insufficient to overcome misleading form-based signals. These findings reveal a fundamental limitation of current LLMs in resolving cross-lingual form--meaning conflicts and establish SemCog Bench as a rigorous benchmark for multilingual semantic reasoning. Our code and data are publicly available.

</details>


### [132] [PolyAlign: Conditional Human-Distribution Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13227)
*L. D. M. S. Sai Teja,Ufaq Khan,Sathira Silva,Xiao Wu,Muhammad Haris Khan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces PolyAlign, a framework for improving language model alignment to better match human response variations across contexts, using bilingual data organization and targeted optimization techniques.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current post-training alignment methods like supervised fine-tuning align language models to one global behavior, which suppresses the diverse, context-dependent variations of human responses.

Method: The paper proposes PolyAlign, which aligns language models to context-specific human response distributions through Bucket-Aware SFT and Human-Distribution Preference Optimization (HDPO), leveraging bilingual interaction data.

Result: PolyAlign improves conditional naturalness, distributional faithfulness, and maintains competitive task performance across bilingual evaluation settings.

Conclusion: Post-training alignment should focus on interaction-aware alignment that matches context-specific human response distributions, moving beyond global alignment approaches like traditional SFT.

Abstract: Post-training methods such as supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and preference optimization typically align language models toward a single global assistant behavior. While effective for improving average helpfulness, this can suppress the natural variation of human responses across languages, tasks, and dialogue settings. We study this problem as conditional human-distribution alignment: models should match the human response distribution appropriate to the current interaction context, rather than a universal response style. We introduce PolyAlign, a distribution-aware alignment framework that organizes bilingual interaction data into bucket-specific human reference distributions defined by language, interaction track, response family, and length. PolyAlign combines Bucket-Aware SFT, which balances optimization across heterogeneous buckets, with Human-Distribution Preference Optimization (HDPO), which regularizes preference learning using critic-estimated distance to bucket-specific human support. Across a bilingual evaluation suite covering English and Chinese single- and multi-turn settings, PolyAlign improves conditional naturalness and distributional faithfulness while preserving competitive task utility. The results suggest that post-training should move beyond global alignment objectives toward interaction-aware alignment with human response distributions.

</details>


### [133] [Evaluating Pluralism in LLMs through Latent Perspectives](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13254)
*Laura Majer,Jan Šnajder,Martin Tutek*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a framework to identify diverse perspectives in text, aiding the evaluation of large language model (LLM) generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models often generate text that lacks diversity, homogenizing perspectives found in training data. The need for pluralistic alignment highlights the importance of identifying and understanding diverse perspectives in text.

Method: The paper proposes a domain-agnostic, multi-layered unsupervised framework that extracts perspectives from text. This approach is applied to book reviews to identify gaps in how LLMs generate pluralistic content.

Result: The analysis shows that while certain models and prompts manage to capture a wide range of perspectives, rare perspectives are still underrepresented, demonstrating deviations from human-like distributions.

Conclusion: The framework provides a valuable tool for identifying pluralistic gaps in LLMs, though challenges remain in ensuring rare perspectives are adequately represented.

Abstract: The growing need to represent diverse perspectives has increased interest in pluralistic LLM generation. Although difficult to operationalize, identifying perspectives expressed in text would provide clear guidance on pluralistic alignment and more clearly articulate the pluralistic gap in LLM generation. While models have been shown to reduce the diversity of training data and generate homogeneously, this has been demonstrated primarily on multiple-choice questionnaires or using high-level characteristics of free-form text. In this paper, we introduce and implement a domain-agnostic multi-layered framework for unsupervised extraction of perspectives suitable for identifying the pluralistic gap in LLM-generated text. We evaluate our framework on book reviews, a highly opinionated dataset representing diverse perspectives, and compare various prompts and models. Our results show that while some models and prompting techniques come close to covering a broad spectrum of perspectives, rarer perspectives remain disproportionately underrepresented, resulting in distributions that diverge from human text.

</details>


### [134] [RogueAI: A Reverse Turing Test for Detecting Licensed AI Deception in Dialogue](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13310)
*Sara Candussio,Emanuele Ballarin,Lorenzo Bonin,Sandro Junior Della Rovere,Luca Bortolussi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new Turing Test framework focusing on trust and deception, introducing RogueAI and AutoRogueAI systems inspired by interrogation games involving deceptive AI agents. Human players underperformed compared to simple algorithms in identifying deceitful agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to redefine the classic Turing Test by focusing on evaluating trustworthiness and deception in AI interactions rather than simply distinguishing AI from humans, reflecting contemporary challenges in conversational systems.

Method: The authors developed RogueAI, an interactive system where players interrogate two AI agents (one being deceptive within a fictional scenario) to identify the deceitful agent. They also propose AutoRogueAI, which allows procedural co-design of scenarios with deception strategies chosen by a narrator AI. A three-day pilot study gathered interaction data to evaluate effectiveness.

Result: The pilot deployment revealed that a simple heuristic method achieved 75.6% accuracy in identifying the deceptive agent, while human players showed only 56.6% accuracy, indicating a significant diagnostic signal that humans failed to leverage.

Conclusion: The findings suggest potential for using the proposed systems as tools for evaluating deception-detection in human-AI interaction, training honesty-focused AI models, and teaching discernment strategies. However, the human heuristic gap raises questions about real-world application and understanding of diagnostic signals.

Abstract: The original Turing Test asks a human judge to distinguish a machine from a person through dialogue. Three quarters of a century later, conversational systems pass this test in casual settings; the interesting epistemological question has shifted. We argue that the relevant modern variant asks not whether a dialogue partner is artificial, but whether it can be trusted. We present RogueAI, an interactive webapp that operationalizes this revisited test as a one-on-two interrogation game: a human player questions two indistinguishable Large Language Model agents, knowing that exactly one of them has been licensed to deceive within a shared fictional scenario. The player's task is to identify the deceptive agent and "shut it off" before a turn budget is exhausted. We further introduce AutoRogueAI, a procedural extension in which players co-design a custom scenario with a narrator agent that secretly chooses its own deception strategy. We describe the framing, sketch the abstract architecture and gameplay loop, and situate the artifact within recent work on LLM deception, social-deduction benchmarks, and scalable oversight via debate. A three-day pilot deployment (467 initiated sessions, 415 completed, 1876 interaction turns in Italian) provides early feasibility evidence and surfaces a concrete tension: the deceptive agent carries a reliable, locally-present linguistic signature - differential helpfulness, brevity, hedging - that a simple heuristic exploits at 75.6% accuracy, yet human players achieved only 56.6%, consistent with ignoring the most diagnostic signal entirely. We discuss what this gap implies for the artifact's use as a data-collection vehicle, a teaching tool, and an evaluation harness for honesty-trained models.

</details>


### [135] [SkillCAT: Contrastive Assessment and Topology-Aware Skill Self-Evolution for LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13317)
*Kunfeng Chen,Qihuang Zhong,Juhua Liu,Bo Du*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces SkillCAT, a framework that enhances LLM agents' reusable skill evolution by sampling trajectory differences, validating patches rigorously, and optimizing inference efficiency for significant performance gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods for skill self-evolution in LLM agents face limitations due to their reliance on single task trajectories and inefficient skill corpus management.

Method: SkillCAT separates the evolution process into three stages: (1) sampling multiple trajectories and identifying causal evidence via Contrastive Causal Extraction (CCE), (2) replaying and validating skill patches through Assessment-Augmented Evolution (AAE), and (3) structuring the evolved skills into a topology for efficient inference with Topology-Aware Task Execution (TTE).

Result: SkillCAT demonstrates up to a 40.40% improvement in average performance across benchmarks and handles cross-model and out-of-distribution generalization effectively.

Conclusion: SkillCAT achieves reliable, training-free skill evolution for LLM agents, providing a scalable, efficient, and generalizable solution for skill document generation and usage.

Abstract: Skill self-evolution methods for LLM agents aim to turn execution trajectories into reusable skill documents, but current pipelines typically learn from one trajectory per task, merge candidate skill patches before checking them, and load the full skill corpus before inference. We propose SkillCAT, a training-free framework that separates this process into three stages. Contrastive Causal Extraction (CCE) samples multiple trajectories for each task and compares same-task success/failure pairs to identify evidence that explains outcome differences. Assessment-Augmented Evolution (AAE) replays each candidate patch on source-task clones and keeps only patches that improve or preserve task outcomes before hierarchical skill patch merging. Topology-Aware Task Execution (TTE) compiles the evolved skills into a routable sub-skill topology, so inference loads only the capability nodes relevant to the task. We evaluate SkillCAT on common agent benchmarks, including SpreadsheetBench, WikiTableQuestions, and DocVQA, and further test cross-model and out-of-distribution generalization. Across these settings, SkillCAT raises the average score over baselines by up to 40.40%, demonstrating reliable skill evolution without model training.

</details>


### [136] [Low-Latency Real-Time Audio Game Commentary System via LLM-Based Parallel Text Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13322)
*Ryota Kawamatsu,Anum Afzal,Yuki Saito,Shinnosuke Takamichi,Graham Neubig,Katsuhito Sudoh,Hiroya Takamura,Tatsuya Ishigaki*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The system creates real-time spoken commentary for gameplay video with reduced latency by generating text in parallel with speech and pre-buffering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome latency and unnatural silence in traditional sequential systems for generating audio commentary from gameplay.

Method: Runs text generation in parallel with speech synthesis, buffering multiple utterances for immediate playback at boundaries.

Result: Achieves a reduction in inter-utterance silence from 9.6 to 0.3 seconds and improves timing resemblance to professional speakers by over 40%.

Conclusion: The parallel design significantly enhances speech rhythm and user experience in real-time audio commentary for fast-paced games.

Abstract: We present a low-latency real-time audio game commentary system that generates spoken commentary directly from live gameplay video. In this end-to-end setting, a key bottleneck is accumulated waiting time; conventional pipelines capture frames, generate text, and synthesize speech sequentially for each utterance, and do not request the next generation until speech playback has completed. This strict sequentiality causes long and unnatural silence between utterances. To address this latency bottleneck, our system runs text generation in parallel with speech playback and buffers multiple candidate utterances ahead of time, enabling immediate synthesis at playback boundaries. Experiments on fast-paced game videos show that our parallel design reduces the mean inter-utterance silence from 9.6 seconds to 0.3 seconds compared to sequential baselines. It also improves similarity to professional speaking--silence timing patterns by over 40 %, and a user study with 120 experienced game players confirms significantly improved perceived speaking rhythm. Our demo video is available at: https://youtu.be/pmrRUlvav8M.

</details>


### [137] [IVIE: A Neuro-symbolic Approach to Incremental and Validated Generation of Interactive Fiction Worlds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13348)
*Micaela Vaucher,Santiago Silveira,Santiago Góngora,Luis Chiruzzo*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces IVIE, a neuro-symbolic system combining LLM creativity with symbolic world coherence for generating interactive fiction worlds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the tension between the creativity of Large Language Models (LLMs) and the coherence of symbolic systems in interactive fiction generation.

Method: IVIE uses a four-stage neuro-symbolic generation pipeline, combining LLM-based creative decisions with symbolic validation for grounding world coherence.

Result: The system produces immersive and thematically coherent interactive worlds, with human evaluation showing strong player engagement but noting occasional inconsistencies.

Conclusion: IVIE effectively balances generative creativity with coherence, though challenges persist such as LLM-driven inconsistencies and gaps in validation processes.

Abstract: Computational creativity in Interactive Fiction faces a fundamental tension: Large Language Models (LLM) may produce creative narratives but struggle with world coherence, while symbolic systems ensure consistency but lack creative flexibility. We present IVIE (Incremental & Validated Interactive Experiences), a neuro-symbolic approach to generating complete and playable interactive fiction worlds from scratch. Building upon PAYADOR's neuro-symbolic framework, IVIE implements a four-stage incremental generation pipeline that delegates creative decisions--setting and character creation, puzzle design--to LLMs while grounding the world state through symbolic validation. The system generates worlds with interconnected locations, functional items, non-player characters, and coherent puzzles, all structured around a central goal-oriented architecture. Human evaluation shows the approach generates immersive, thematically coherent worlds with high player engagement. Results seem to indicate that the neuro-symbolic approach successfully balances flexibility with narrative coherence: symbolic validation grounds LLM generation without eliminating generative freedom. However, challenges remain: LLM inconsistencies occasionally bypass puzzle constraints, and objective validation gaps allow some structurally impossible goals. We identify key design considerations for future neurosymbolic interactive storytelling systems, particularly regarding LLM capabilities and their limitations.

</details>


### [138] [From Passive Generation to Investigation: A Proactive Scientific Peer Review Agent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13349)
*Haishuo Fang,Yue Feng,Iryna Gurevych*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces ProReviewer, a scientific peer review agent based on a Markov Decision Process, which proactively investigates papers using a structured review log.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current large language models (LLMs) struggle to generate in-depth, evidence-based peer reviews, partly due to limited ability to investigate suspicious content proactively, as human reviewers do.

Method: ProReviewer is based on a Markov Decision Process framework and uses a structured review log to track evidence. It is trained via supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning.

Result: ProReviewer achieves the highest average quality scores, outperforming larger LLMs and fine-tuned baselines by significant margins in both automated and human evaluations.

Conclusion: ProReviewer demonstrates the potential for LLMs to enhance automated scientific peer review by performing proactive investigations and improving review quality significantly.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have shown promise in automating scientific peer review. However, existing approaches often struggle to generate in-depth reviews supported by concrete evidence. We argue that a key limitation is the lack of flexibility to proactively investigate suspicious parts of a paper based on accumulated evidence, as human reviewers do. In this paper, we explore how to enable an LLM-based review agent to perform such proactive investigation. We find that this can be naturally formulated as a Markov Decision Process (MDP), and propose ProReviewer, a scientific peer review agent that proactively reviews a paper guided by a maintained, structured review log. The structured review log serves as a workspace for the agent to track evidence and intermediate findings collected during review. Experiments show that ProReviewer with an 8B backbone, trained by supervised fine-tuning and optimized by reinforcement learning, achieves the highest average score across five quality dimensions, outperforming prompt-based methods with much larger frontier LLMs by up to 39% and the strongest fine-tuned baseline by 16% relatively. It also attains the highest win rates against baselines in human evaluation.

</details>


### [139] [An End-to-End Hybrid Framework for Rumour Detection in Low-Resources Algerian Dialect](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13411)
*Dihia Lanasri,Fatima Benbarek*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a hybrid framework for detecting rumours in Algerian dialect social media content, achieving high performance despite the challenges of dialect, limited resources, and informal text structures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenges of detecting rumours in Algerian dialect social media, characterized by informal, code-switched language and scarce annotated resources.

Method: The authors developed a hybrid framework using a domain-specific dataset from real posts, synthetic data, and FASSILA corpus with automatic labeling. They also implemented a transliteration pipeline and evaluated various machine learning and transformer-based models.

Result: A hybrid approach using transformer embeddings combined with a classical classifier achieved an F1-score of 0.84, with pre-training on domain-specific social media data proving more effective than using larger general Arabic models.

Conclusion: The study confirms the viability of an effective rumour detection system in Algerian dialect social media despite low-resource constraints, emphasizing the effectiveness of hybrid approaches and domain-specific pre-training.

Abstract: The rapid growth of social media has intensified the spread of rumours. This issue is more challenging in the Algerian context due to the informal and code-switched nature of dialectal content, the scarcity of annotated resources, and the limited effectiveness of standard Arabic NLP tools on dialect text.
  This paper presents an end-to-end rumour detection hybrid framework for Algerian dialect social media content. We build a domain-specific annotated dataset by combining real social media posts, synthetic data, and the FASSILA corpus, with automatic labeling based on a similarity-based annotation process. A transliteration pipeline is also introduced to generate parallel datasets in Arabic script and Arabizi.
  We evaluate multiple approaches, including classical machine learning, deep learning, transformers, and hybrid models. Experimental results show that a hybrid approach combining transformer embeddings with a classical classifier achieves the best performance, reaching an F1-score of 0.84. We also find that domain-specific pre-training is more important than model size, with social media-trained models outperforming larger models trained on formal Arabic corpora.
  These results demonstrate the feasibility of rumour detection in low-resource Algerian dialect settings.

</details>


### [140] [S-GBT: Smooth Growth Bound Tensor for Certified Robustness Against Word Substitution Attacks in NLP](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13439)
*Mohammed Bouri,Mohammed Erradi,Adnane Saoud*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Smooth Growth Bound Tensor (S-GBT), a second-order method enhancing robustness against word substitution attacks in NLP models, and shows significant improvements in certified robust accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: NLP models remain vulnerable to word substitution attacks due to insufficient consideration of how gradients evolve (curvature). Addressing this limitation motivates the study.

Method: The authors propose the Smooth Growth Bound Tensor (S-GBT), a second-order robustness measure that bounds the Hessian, and integrate it into the training objective of LSTM and CNN models using a regularization term.

Result: Evaluation on multiple NLP benchmark datasets demonstrated up to a 23.4% improvement in certified robust accuracy, while maintaining competitive clean accuracy.

Conclusion: Combining first and second order regularization proves to be an effective strategy for improving model robustness against adversarial word substitutions.

Abstract: Despite recent progress in Natural Language Processing (NLP), models remain vulnerable to word substitution attacks. Most existing defenses focus on first order sensitivity and measure how much the output changes when the input is slightly perturbed. However, they ignore how this sensitivity evolves, which is described by curvature. When gradients vary sharply, models can still fail. This paper introduces the Smooth Growth Bound Tensor (S-GBT), a second order method that bounds the Hessian element-wise, for which we provide formal theoretical proofs on the resulting robustness bounds. A regularization term is added during training to minimize these bounds. This yields tighter certified robustness against word substitution attacks. The change in the output under word substitution is bounded by both a linear term and a quadratic term. S-GBT is derived for two architectures: Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN). The method is integrated directly into the training objective. Its effectiveness is evaluated on multiple benchmark datasets. The results show that combining first and second order regularization improves certified robust accuracy by up to 23.4% compared to prior methods, while clean accuracy remains competitive. These findings indicate that controlling both the gradient and its variation is a promising direction for building more robust models.

</details>


### [141] [Ontology Memory-Augmented ASR Correction for Long Text-Speech Interleaved Conversations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13464)
*Xinxin Li,Huiyao Chen,Meishan Zhang,Yunxin Li,Zulong Chen,Zhibo Ren,Xiaoqing Dong Baotian Hu,Min Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces an ontology memory-augmented framework for long-term automatic speech recognition (ASR) correction in text-speech interleaved conversations, showing improvement over existing models in accuracy and evidence-grounded corrections.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to enhance ASR correction for long-term, interleaved conversations by addressing challenges such as sparse evidence and redundancy in long interaction histories.

Method: The authors propose organizing preceding interaction history into an ontology memory that dynamically stores entities, terminologies, surface variants, ASR confusions, and semantic relations, allowing for context-grounded ASR correction.

Result: Experiments on the RAMC-Corr dataset indicate that the presented method outperforms standard direct correction models in 9 out of 10 configurations, ensuring improved accuracy and corrections grounded in evidence.

Conclusion: The ontology memory-augmented framework effectively addresses issues of sparse evidence and redundancy in long conversations, enhancing ASR correction accuracy and promoting selective, context-aware correction.

Abstract: Automatic speech recognition (ASR) correction has traditionally focused on isolated utterances or short local contexts. However, as text and speech become increasingly interleaved in long interactions, ASR correction requires conversation-level contextual evidence. Existing ASR correction methods often rely on the current hypothesis or concatenate raw dialogue history. In such contexts, sparse correction evidence can be difficult to locate amid redundancy and noise. Addressing these challenges, we propose an ontology memory-augmented ASR correction framework for long text-speech interleaved conversations. The framework organizes preceding interaction history into a dynamically updatable ontology memory, where entities, terminology, surface variants, potential ASR confusions, and semantic relations are stored as retrievable nodes for context-grounded correction. To evaluate this setting, we construct RAMC-Corr, a dataset derived from MAGIC-RAMC for long-range ASR correction with grounded context. Experiments on RAMC-Corr show that our method improves over direct correction in 9 out of 10 paired backbone-setting combinations and encourages more selective and evidence-grounded corrections for context-dependent ASR errors.

</details>


### [142] [Leveraging Audio-LLMs to Filter Speech-to-Speech Training Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13507)
*Qixu Chen,Satoshi Nakamura*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a method to filter noisy data for speech-to-speech translation using a two-stage Rank-to-Distill strategy, improving translation performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mined corpora for speech-to-speech translation often contain noise, misalignment, or semantic errors, which degrade performance. Hence, there is a need to develop an effective way to filter such noisy data.

Method: The proposed method uses a two-stage Rank-to-Distill strategy. A lightweight ranker first assigns pseudo-labels (keep/drop decisions) to noisy speech pairs. Then, an audio-language model is trained to predict these decisions directly from raw audio pairs, focusing on both acoustic fidelity and semantic consistency.

Result: Experiments conducted on CVSS-C and SpeechMatrix datasets show consistent improvements in speech-to-speech translation performance, with gains of up to +1.4 ASR-BLEU when compared to unfiltered data training.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that training an audio-language model with the proposed method effectively filters noisy data, resulting in improved speech-to-speech translation accuracy and robustness.

Abstract: Large-scale mined corpora provide abundant training data for end-to-end speech-to-speech translation (S2ST) but may contain noise, misalignment, and semantic errors. Filtering noisy data is crucial to maintain robust speech translation performance. We study how to train an audio-language model to make keep/drop decisions on paired speech directly from audio. To obtain reliable supervision without manual labels, we adopt a scalable two-stage Rank-to-Distill strategy. A lightweight ranker generates keep/drop pseudo-labels from noisy speech pairs, then trains an audio large language model to predict keep/drop directly from raw paired speech. The resulting model jointly captures acoustic fidelity and cross-lingual semantic consistency for the selection of speech-conditioned data. Experiments on CVSS-C and SpeechMatrix show consistent improvements over unfiltered training, yielding up to +1.4 ASR-BLEU for end-to-end S2ST.

</details>


### [143] [When Does Mixing Help? Analyzing Query Embedding Interpolation in Multilingual Dense Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13537)
*Tongyao Zhu,Chao-Ming Huang,Min-Yen Kan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The study explores sensitivity of dense retrievers with mixed-language queries and finds structured, predictable patterns in retrieval performance based on query-language mixing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To better understand how dense retrievers perform with mixed-language querying, commonly used in multilingual contexts, and to identify optimal strategies in such situations.

Method: The authors performed a controlled study on mMARCO, systematically varying query mixing proportions by interpolating monolingual embeddings at the embedding level. They evaluated performance using the BGE-M3 model.

Result: Optimized mixing ratios outperform monolingual endpoints in 88/105 cases. Results showed English dominance, where mixed queries enhance retrieval from non-English indices but pure English queries perform better over indices with English content. English also emerged as the most effective partner for non-English queries.

Conclusion: Language-mixing sensitivity in dense retrievers is structured, predictable, and correlated with factors like English dominance and typological distance. Observed patterns are robust across different model families and scales.

Abstract: While mixed-language querying is ubiquitous in multilingual communities, the sensitivity of dense retrievers to such queries remains poorly understood.
