Building on this holistic tokenizer, we further propose a principled improvement of the editing pipeline: source-target interaction should occur at the latent level inside the tokenizer rather than at the semantic level inside the LLM, substantially improving editing consistency and accelerating convergence. Instantiated at the 7B dense model, HYDRA-X achieves strong performance across image and video understanding and generation tasks, paving the way for future unified-tokenizer UMMs.

</details>


### [201] [DuET: Dual Expert Trajectories for Diffusion Image Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13303)
*Lidia Troeshestova,Alexander Ustyuzhanin,Sergey Kastryulin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DuET introduces a method to temporarily ease constraints on source-image conditioning during image editing, improving edit fidelity and result quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations in existing diffusion editors that struggle to execute substantial edits while maintaining natural outputs during instruction-based editing.

Method: DuET employs a dual trajectory, transitioning temporarily through a text-to-image generation phase and then returning to editing. This allows the editing process to focus on the target distribution while benefiting from the original image's structure.

Result: The method enhances instruction relevance, semantic fidelity, and perceptual quality without changing model weights or adding sampling costs, though at a minor cost to source-image preservation.

Conclusion: DuET offers a predictable trade-off between source preservation and edit fidelity, providing an effective inference approach for diverse image-editing models.

Abstract: Recent diffusion editors perform diverse instruction-based edits while conditioning on the source image at every denoising step. Yet persistent source-image conditioning can limit how fully an edit is executed and how natural the result appears, especially when the target scene diverges substantially from the input. We introduce DuET (Dual Expert Trajectories), a training-free inference method that temporarily relaxes source-image conditioning by transitioning through a text-to-image phase before returning to edit mode, allowing the denoising trajectory to move toward the target distribution while retaining the structural benefits of image-conditioned editing. Without modifying model weights or increasing sampling cost, DuET consistently improves instruction relevance, semantic fidelity, and perceptual quality across diverse models and benchmarks. In some cases, these gains come with a modest reduction in source-image preservation, revealing a predictable trade-off between source preservation and edit fidelity.

</details>


### [202] [ReFree: Towards Realistic Co-Speech Video Generation via Reward-Free RL and Multilevel Speech Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13304)
*Salaheldin Mohamed,M. Hamza Mughal,Rishabh Dabral,Christian Theobalt*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces ReFree-S2V, a new method for speech-driven portrait animation that achieves accurate lip synchronization alongside natural and expressive head and facial motion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current systems struggle to balance precise phoneme-to-lip synchronization with dynamic and natural head motions, resulting in animations that are either overly rigid or poorly synchronized.

Method: The proposed ReFree-S2V framework integrates a multi-level speech representation featuring phonetic and prosodic details injected into transformer blocks via learnable selectors. It also uses a reward-free reinforcement learning approach to ensure natural head movements.

Result: ReFree-S2V significantly outperforms existing methods in both lip-sync accuracy and qualitative evaluations of expressive naturalness, as validated by extensive experiments.

Conclusion: The paper effectively addresses the limitations of previous approaches, offering a system that combines precise articulation with natural expressiveness, advancing the state-of-the-art in speech-driven portrait animation.

Abstract: Speech-driven talking character animation seeks to generate life-like portrait videos that convey natural conversation behavior, aligning facial motion with spoken audio. Although recent advances in video generation have substantially improved realism in video-based animation, achieving both accurate lip articulation and expressive behavior remains challenging. Existing approaches typically trade off precise phoneme-to-lip synchronization against dynamic facial expressions and head motion, yielding animations that are either accurate yet rigid, or expressive but poorly synchronized. We address this challenge by proposing ReFree-S2V, a flow-matching speech-to-portrait animation framework that builds upon a pretrained video generation model to achieve fine-grained speech articulation and high-level expressive cues in speech-driven portrait animation. This model introduces a multi-level speech representation capturing phonetic and prosodic information at both local and global granularities. These representations are selectively injected into transformer blocks via learnable level selectors, enabling both accurate lip synchronization and natural expressive motion. To achieve natural head movements, we further introduce a novel reward-free reinforcement learning scheme into flow-matching training to discourage perceptually implausible motion without relying on handcrafted synchronization metrics or reward models, or the high cost of human preference annotation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ReFree-S2V achieves state-of-the-art performance, significantly outperforming existing methods in both quantitative lip-sync accuracy and qualitative human evaluations of naturalness and expressivity.

</details>


### [203] [MagPlus: Bridging Micro-to-Regular Facial Expressions through Learnable Magnification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13312)
*Sliman Jammal,Andrei Sharf*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces MagPlus, a system for enhancing micro-expression generation quality by magnifying subtle facial movements into regular expression ranges, enabling compatibility with standard facial expression models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Micro-expressions reveal genuine emotions but are hard to model and generate due to their subtlety, short duration, and limited data availability. Existing methods often struggle with quality, robustness, and generalizability.

Method: MagPlus magnifies subtle micro-motions into standard facial expression ranges, allowing compatibility with pretrained expression models. A complementary module, DeMagPlus, reduces enhanced motions back to realistic micro-expression intensities.

Result: Using four facial animation models (FOMM, FSRT, MetaPortrait, EmoPortraits), experiments demonstrate that MagPlus-DeMagPlus improves the generation of realistic micro-expression motions without retraining the existing models.

Conclusion: MagPlus effectively bridges micro-expression analysis and standard facial animation models, enhancing the generation of micro-expressions with higher quality and robustness without requiring specialized training from scratch.

Abstract: Facial micro-expressions are subtle and short-lived facial movements that provide important cues about genuine human emotions. However, modeling and generating them remains difficult because annotated micro-expression data is limited and the underlying facial motions are extremely weak. Existing micro-expression generation methods therefore often suffer from limited quality, weak robustness, and poor generalization. We propose MagPlus, a transferable micro-expression processing pipeline that connects micro-expression analysis with standard facial animation models. Instead of training a dedicated generator from scratch, MagPlus learns to magnify subtle facial motions into the range of regular facial expressions, transforming micro-expressions into signals that are compatible with existing facial expression processing models. The magnified sequence is then used by a standard facial expression model for tasks such as transfer and synthesis. A complementary DeMagPlus module then restores the generated motion back to realistic micro-expression intensity levels while preserving the synthesized dynamics. We evaluate the framework using four facial animation models: FOMM, FSRT, MetaPortrait, and EmoPortraits. None of these models are trained on micro-expression data. Experiments show that MagPlus-DeMagPlus enables pretrained macro-expression models to generate more realistic micro-expression motion without retraining the backbones.

</details>


### [204] [Masked and Predictive Self-Supervised Foundation Models for 3D Brain MRI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13315)
*Esra Ergün,Hersh Chandarana,Dan Sodickson,Gözde Ünal*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper examines two self-supervised learning methods for MRI-based disease detection: Masked Autoencoders (MAE) and Joint Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPA). Novel regularization methods tailored to each approach are proposed and analyzed for their impact on downstream performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address the gap in the application of self-supervised foundation models for MRI-based disease detection, which has not been as extensively explored as segmentation tasks.

Method: Two pretraining paradigms are explored: MAE with a spectral-domain reconstruction loss and JEPA with variance-covariance regularization. MRI data used is heterogeneous, single-contrast, and processed in a contrast-agnostic manner.

Result: The research finds that MAE with spectral-domain supervision performs best for MRI-based disease detection. The relevance of self-supervised objectives to specific task structures is key for downstream performance.

Conclusion: Self-supervised objectives introduce biases that influence their effectiveness in medical imaging, and the alignment between these biases and task characteristics determines their utility.

Abstract: Self-supervised foundation models have shown strong promise in medical imaging. However, existing MRI foundation-model studies have primarily emphasized segmentation and dense prediction tasks, while systematic investigation of self-supervised foundation models for MRI-based disease detection remains limited. In this work, we investigate two major self-supervised pretraining paradigms for MRI-based disease detection: reconstruction-based learning via Masked Autoencoders (MAE) and predictive representation learning via Joint Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPA). We study the role of auxiliary objectives by introducing a novel spectral-domain reconstruction loss for MAE to enhance sensitivity to fine-grained anatomical structure, and by integrating variance--covariance regularization (VCR) within our JEPA framework to encourage decorrelated latent representations. Our models are pretrained on heterogeneous single-contrast MRI volumes in a contrast-agnostic setting, without modality concatenation. Across five downstream disease detection tasks, our results highlight the importance of self-supervised objective design for medical foundation model pretraining, demonstrating that the downstream benefit of each objective is determined by its relevance to the task's structure. Specifically, spectral regularization yields the largest improvements when the downstream discriminative signal is characterized by strong high-frequency anatomical structures, while covariance regularization is most beneficial when discriminative information spans multiple decorrelated feature dimensions. MAE with spectral-domain supervision consistently achieves superior downstream performance for MRI-based disease detection. These findings suggest that self-supervised objectives in medical imaging encode specific biases, and their downstream benefit is fundamentally conditioned on the task's structure.

</details>


### [205] [OR-Action: Multi-Role Video Understanding with Fine-Grained Actions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13332)
*Felix Tristram,Ege Özsoy,Christian Benz,Marcel Walch,Ghazal Ghazaei,Nassir Navab*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces an action-centric benchmark for fine-grained activity understanding in operating rooms (ORs), emphasizing interactions and temporal modeling, and presents a vision-only temporal model that outperforms graph-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to overcome challenges in understanding OR activity, such as clutter, occlusions, and limited sensing, by enabling a workflow-aware and temporally fine-grained modeling approach.

Method: The method involves creating an action-centric benchmark with dense action segments distilled from ground-truth scene graph changes, employing temporal modeling through a vision-only architecture, and introducing a multi-to-single-view alignment strategy for better single-view performance.

Result: The proposed vision-only temporal model significantly outperformed graph-based methods and introduced a feature alignment strategy to improve single-view action recognition.

Conclusion: Current scene graph methods struggle with temporal structure modeling, but the proposed vision-only temporal approach shows strong potential for advancing OR activity understanding without relying on extensive egocentric video.

Abstract: Fine-grained understanding of operating room (OR) activity could enable workflow-aware assistance, yet remains difficult due to clutter, occlusions, and limited sensing. The prevailing approach to model this environment is scene graphs as an interpretable representation of OR interactions. Converting their frame-wise relational predictions into temporally extended, fine-grained actions however, is challenging without explicit temporal modeling. To enable a principled temporal evaluation of current OR understanding methods, we introduce the first action-centric benchmark built on a publicly available ego-exocentric OR dataset by defining a fine-grained, multi-role action taxonomy and generating dense action segments via distillation from ground-truth scene graph state changes. Experiments on this benchmark show that current scene graph prediction methods struggle to model temporal structure, even when adding explicit modeling through Graph Neural Networks. We therefore introduce a vision-only temporal model that outperforms graph-based methods significantly when using all available egocentric video as input. Building on this model we also introduce a novel multi- to single-view feature alignment strategy that improves single-view performance on multi-role action recognition, mitigating the need for extensive egocentric video capture. Benchmark and code will be released upon acceptance.

</details>


### [206] [Dual-Domain Equivariant Generative Adversarial Network for Multimodal CT-PET Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13341)
*Gabriel Steele,Alzahra Altalib,Alessandro Perelli*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces DDE-GAN, a method for improving multimodal CT-PET image synthesis by learning from both spatial and frequency domains and incorporating rotational equivariance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GAN approaches struggle with maintaining structural fidelity due to a lack of geometric consistency during CT-PET image synthesis.

Method: DDE-GAN leverages dual-domain learning (spatial and Fourier) with rotational equivariance embedded into the generator and discriminator losses, using multi-stage loss functions for consistency.

Result: DDE-GAN outperforms baseline models on the HECKTOR 2022 CT-PET dataset, achieving higher synthesis quality and improved anatomical accuracy.

Conclusion: Combining dual-domain learning with geometric equivariance enhances the robustness and accuracy of multimodal CT-PET image synthesis, offering practical benefits for applications like PET completion and data augmentation.

Abstract: We present a Dual-Domain Equivariant Generative Adversarial Network (DDE-GAN) for multimodal CT-PET image synthesis. Traditional GAN-based approaches often operate solely in the spatial domain and ignore geometric consistency, resulting in limited structural fidelity. DDE-GAN addresses these challenges by jointly learning from both spatial and frequency (Fourier) domains, capturing complementary anatomical and spectral information. Furthermore, rotational equivariance embedded in the physics of the CT and PET measurements are integrated into the loss of both the generator and discriminator to ensure consistent responses under rotations, improving anatomical accuracy. A hierarchical dual-domain training strategy enforces intra- and inter-domain consistency through multi-stage loss functions. Evaluated on the HECKTOR 2022 CT-PET dataset, DDE-GAN achieves superior synthesis quality over baseline models for CT-PET image synthesis. The results demonstrate that combining dual-domain learning with geometric equivariance substantially enhances multimodal image synthesis accuracy and robustness, enabling practical applications in PET completion and data augmentation.

</details>


### [207] [JointEdit3D: Feed-Forward 3D Scene Editing in a Unified Latent Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13345)
*Xinnan Zhu,Ruijie Xu,Jiayu Ying,Daoguo Dong,Jiachen Xu,Yuan Xie,Xin Tan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces JointEdit3D, a framework for 3D scene editing that combines appearance and geometry prediction for efficient and consistent edits, supported by a new dataset and benchmark for evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for 3D scene editing face high computational costs, limited 3D awareness, and structural inconsistencies due to reliance on explicit per-scene optimization or complex edit-and-reconstruct pipelines.

Method: The proposed JointEdit3D employs a unified latent space for RGB-geometry reconstruction and generation, performing asymmetric latent inpainting using a single edited RGB reference. It introduces a SceneAnchor Branch to structure source-scene anchoring and leverages specific losses to balance edit quality and unedited content preservation.

Result: JointEdit3D outperforms existing baselines in edited-region quality and 3D structural consistency, while maintaining competitive performance in preserving unedited backgrounds.

Conclusion: JointEdit3D provides a promising feed-forward approach to 3D scene editing by combining appearance synthesis with geometry prediction in a unified latent space. It is complemented by a new dataset and benchmark to facilitate standardized evaluation.

Abstract: Existing 3D scene editing methods typically rely on per-scene optimization over explicit 3D representations or cascaded edit-and-reconstruct pipelines, resulting in high test-time cost, limited 3D awareness, and structural inconsistencies. To couple appearance synthesis and geometry prediction during editing, we build on a unified RGB-geometry reconstruction-generation latent space and adapt it to feed-forward 3D scene editing. The resulting framework, \textbf{JointEdit3D}, performs asymmetric latent inpainting by observing only a single edited RGB reference latent and generating the remaining RGB views and edited geometry latent under source-scene anchoring. JointEdit3D introduces a dedicated SceneAnchor Branch to inject source-scene structure without forcing direct copying, and adopts edit/background-aware losses to balance edited-region fidelity with unedited-content preservation. To address the lack of paired resources for standardized 3D scene editing evaluation, we introduce SceneEdit3D-15K, a dataset with 15K paired editing samples and renderer-provided 3D annotations, together with SceneEdit3D-Bench, a curated 100-sample benchmark. Experiments show that JointEdit3D improves edited-region quality and 3D structural completeness over prior baselines while maintaining competitive background preservation.

</details>


### [208] [Dual-Constrained Diffusion Image Compression for Operational Rate-Distortion-Perception Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13366)
*Sanxin Jiang,Jiro Katto,Heming Sun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces DCIC, a novel image compression framework that balances fidelity and perceptual realism by governing the rate-distortion-perception (RDP) trade-offs using a diffusion-based decoder and dual constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the scarcity of practical frameworks that explicitly realize the rate-distortion-perception (RDP) trade-off surface due to challenges in introducing common randomness at the decoder for image compression.

Method: The authors propose DCIC, which employs a learned codec combined with a diffusion-based decoder with distortion and idempotence constraints. These constraints guide the reverse denoising process via iterative optimization with noise injection, allowing flexible navigation of the RDP trade-off using dual attenuation factors.

Result: The DCIC framework achieves superior results in BD-PSNR and matches dedicated perception-oriented methods in BD-FID for tested datasets and architectures, confirming its effective navigation across the RDP surface.

Conclusion: The proposed DCIC method provides a practical and versatile solution for neural image compression, enabling optimal performance across fidelity-perception trade-offs through its novel integration of diffusion-based decoders and dual constraints.

Abstract: The rate-distortion-perception (RDP) trade-off extends classical rate--distortion theory by imposing a distributional constraint on reconstructions, providing a unified framework for neural image compression that jointly governs fidelity and perceptual realism. While prior work achieves near-optimal rate--perception trade-offs, practical frameworks explicitly realizing the full RDP surface remain scarce, primarily due to the difficulty of introducing common randomness at the decoder. We propose DCIC (Dual-Constrained Diffusion Image Compression), which integrates a learned codec with a diffusion-based decoder governed by joint distortion and idempotence constraints. The distortion constraint bounds reconstruction fidelity relative to the base codec output; the idempotence constraint -- requiring that re-encoding the restored image recovers the base codec reconstruction -- serves as a tractable surrogate for the distributional perception requirement. Together, they steer the reverse denoising process via iterative optimization with consistent noise injection, realizing common randomness without additional rate overhead. At fixed rate, dual attenuation factors $(K_D, K_P)$ jointly navigate the Pareto frontier of the distortion-perception plane, enabling continuously adjustable fidelity-realism trade-offs from a single bitstream. DCIC$_{RD}$ ($K_P{=}0$) and DCIC$_{RP}$ ($K_D{=}0$) arise as boundary curves, with DCIC$_{RDP}$ ($K_D = K_P=1$) realizing the optimal interior operating point. Experiments on CelebA-HQ, CLIC2020, and ImageNet-1K across CNN, Transformer, and hybrid architectures confirm that DCIC$_{RDP}$ achieves superior BD-PSNR over all perceptual codecs, while DCIC$_{RP}$ matches dedicated perception-oriented methods in BD-FID, validating the practical value of full RDP surface navigation.

</details>


### [209] [MoVerse: Real-Time Video World Modeling with Panoramic Gaussian Scaffold](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13376)
*Yang Zhou,Ziheng Wang,Yuqin Lu,Haofeng Liu,Jun Liang,Shengfeng He,Jing Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MoVerse is a real-time video world model that transforms a single narrow-field image into an interactively navigable 3D scene.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to tackle the challenge of creating complete 3D scenes with realistic visuals from a single image, enabling interactive exploration.

Method: MoVerse uses a combination of topology-aware diffusion for panorama expansion, 3D Gaussian scaffolding, and a Gaussian-based video renderer to generate interactive scenes.

Result: The proposed system achieves real-time rendering at 8 FPS on an NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPU, enabling high-fidelity video output that allows user-controlled navigation.

Conclusion: MoVerse bridges explicit 3D representations and generative video models, demonstrating a feasible approach to creating 3D worlds from single images for real-time applications.

Abstract: We present MoVerse, a real-time video world model that creates an interactively navigable scene from a single narrow-field-of-view image. This setting is challenging because the input observes only a small fraction of the environment, while interactive roaming requires a complete surrounding world, persistent geometry, controllable camera motion, and temporally coherent high-fidelity observations. MoVerse addresses this problem by separating world construction from observation rendering. It first expands the input into a gravity-aligned 360$^\circ$ panorama with topology-aware diffusion, closing the missing field of view before 3D reasoning. It then lifts the panorama into a persistent 3D Gaussian scaffold using panoramic geometry-aware residual prediction, yielding a dense and directly renderable spatial memory. Finally, a Gaussian-conditioned video renderer translates scaffold renderings along user-specified camera trajectories into photorealistic video. To make this renderer practical for interaction, we train a bidirectional diffusion teacher for high-quality conditional rendering and distill it into a causal autoregressive student for bounded-latency streaming. This design combines the controllability and long-range consistency of explicit 3D representations with the perceptual quality of generative video models. MoVerse supports real-time scene roaming at 8~FPS on a single NVIDIA RTX~4090 GPU, demonstrating a practical path toward single-image world creation with interactive video output.

</details>


### [210] [Heterogeneous LiDAR Early Fusion and Learned Re-Ranking Strategy for Robust Long-Term Place Recognition in Unstructured Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13503)
*Judith Vilella-Cantos,Juan José Cabrera,Mónica Ballesta,David Valiente,Luis Payá*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces MinkUNeXt-VINE++, a new LiDAR-based place recognition method for challenging environments, achieving significant performance improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance localization in unstructured environments like vineyards by overcoming challenges with repetitive structures and single-sensor limitations.

Method: Combines early fusion of data from two LiDAR sensors with a learned inference-time re-ranking strategy, evaluated on the TEMPO-VINE dataset.

Result: Achieved a 20% improvement in Recall@1 compared to single-sensor methods, and a +30% improvement with re-ranking enabled.

Conclusion: MinkUNeXt-VINE++ outperforms state-of-the-art methods and is publicly available for replication.

Abstract: Robust localization in unstructured environments, such as agricultural fields, is a critical challenge for autonomous systems. LiDAR sensors provide detailed 3D information about the environment and are invariant to lighting conditions. For this reason, LiDAR-based place recognition methods have gained significant attention. In this paper, we propose MinkUNeXt-VINE++, a novel approach that combines early fusion of heterogeneous LiDAR data from two sensors (Livox Mid-360 and Velodyne VLP-16) and a learned re-ranking strategy in inference time. This fusion leverages the strengths of each sensor to provide a more comprehensive representation of the environment. Additionally, the re-ranking approach is particularly important in repetitive environments, such as vineyards, as finding true positives is a major challenge. We evaluated our approach using the TEMPO-VINE dataset, which provides heterogeneous LiDAR data in vineyard environments across different phenological stages. Our results demonstrate that MinkUNeXt-VINE++ significantly improves place recognition performance compared to single-sensor approaches and state-of-the-art methods. MinkUNeXt-VINE++ achieves a 20% improvement in the Recall@1 metric compared to single-sensor approaches, and +30% including re-ranking. The code of our method is publicly available for reproduction.

</details>


### [211] [SmartFont: Dynamic Condition Allocation for Few-Shot Font Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13382)
*Zian Yang,Zixin Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SmartFont is a diffusion-based system for few-shot font generation that combines global content-style modeling with weakly supervised local corrective mechanisms, improving global-local balance, glyph quality, and detail fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the dual challenge of ensuring global structural completeness and fine-grained local style fidelity in few-shot font generation, which previous methods struggle to balance effectively.

Method: The proposed method, SmartFont, incorporates a diffusion-based framework combining global content-style generation and a local branch of corrective experts trained with weak supervision to handle semantic-spatial discrepancies. It also features a denoising-state condition allocation module to adaptively balance global and local features.

Result: SmartFont demonstrates superior performance in balancing global and local aspects, delivering improved font glyph quality and preserving fine-grained details effectively.

Conclusion: SmartFont successfully resolves the limitations of earlier approaches by strategically combining global and local modeling with adaptive condition allocation, offering a robust solution for high-quality and detailed few-shot font generation.

Abstract: Few-shot font generation simultaneously requires global structural completeness and fine-grained local style fidelity. Existing methods usually either rely on global content-style modeling, which is robust but imperfectly disentangled, or emphasize component/local modeling, which captures fine details but relies heavily on local priors and reference coverage. We argue that the key challenge is not merely to learn purer conditions, but to organize complementary yet biased global and local conditions through multi-level allocation during generation. To this end, we propose SmartFont, a diffusion-based few-shot font generation framework that combines global content-style generation with weakly supervised local corrective experts. The local branch performs semantic-spatial allocation by learning expert-wise local concepts and semantically meaningful spatial maps under weak component supervision, enabling fine-grained correction without requiring explicit component-conditioned inference. On top of this, a denoising-state condition allocation module adaptively weights global content, global style, and local corrective feature across timesteps and injection blocks. Extensive experiments show that SmartFont achieves better global-local balance, improves glyph quality and local detail fidelity.

</details>


### [212] [MaskWAM: Unifying Mask Prompting and Prediction for World-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13515)
*Hanyang Yu,Haitao Lin,Jingbo Zhang,Wenyao Zhang,Chenghao Gu,Heng Li,Ping Tan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MaskWAM enhances robotic control by integrating object-centric masks into world-action models, offering robust generalization and better handling of visual noise and language ambiguity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current World Action Models face challenges like referential ambiguity in text input and bias from task-irrelevant backgrounds, necessitating improved semantic grounding and spatial precision.

Method: MaskWAM introduces object-centric masks as both inputs and predictions using Mixture of Transformers (MoT). It uses future mask predictions for semantic clarity and visual prompts, such as target object masks, for spatial anchoring.

Result: MaskWAM achieves superior performance compared to baselines on tasks like LIBERO, RoboTwin, and real-world scenarios, particularly excelling in settings with language ambiguity.

Conclusion: Direct mask conditioning fosters robust language-guided object manipulation, making MaskWAM a powerful solution for enhancing robotic control in complex environments.

Abstract: World Action Models (WAMs) present a promising paradigm for robotic control via video prediction. However, current WAMs suffer from fundamental spatial bottlenecks: standard text inputs introduce referential ambiguity in cluttered scenes, while unstructured RGB predictions lack semantic grounding and remain biased by task-irrelevant backgrounds. To overcome these limitations, we introduce MaskWAM, an object-centric world-action model. By jointly integrating masks as both explicit inputs and predictions via a unified Mixture of Transformers (MoT), MaskWAM unlocks robust policy generalization. This design provides two key benefits: (1) predicting future masks yields object-centric semantic supervision that suppresses visual noise, significantly enhancing even standard text-conditioned WAMs; and (2) coupling this predictive supervision with first-frame visual prompts, such as target object masks, establishes a precise spatial anchor that substantially reduces language ambiguity. Crucially, as WAMs are inherently vision-driven architectures, direct mask conditioning yields substantially stronger guidance than text alone, establishing a precise and robust paradigm for manipulating unseen objects. Evaluations on LIBERO, RoboTwin, and real-world tasks demonstrate that MaskWAM significantly outperforms baselines in both language-clear and language-ambiguous tasks.

</details>


### [213] [Person Identification from Contextual Motion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13410)
*Igor Kviatkovsky,Ehud Rivlin,Ilan Shimshoni*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces an interactive process for identifying individuals based on motion patterns, using a probabilistic generative model. The system uses visual cues and subject responses for identity inference, yielding high recognition accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation revolves around improving person identification techniques for applications such as surveillance and authentication by leveraging motion patterns and introducing an interactive identification approach.

Method: The paper formalizes a sequential interactive process where visual cues are displayed, subjects' motion responses are recorded, and probabilistic models are used to iteratively refine identity inference until sufficient confidence is attained. Mutual information is employed to choose the most informative cues.

Result: High person identification accuracy was achieved through experiments conducted on five public datasets and an additional novel dataset comprising 4,476 recordings from 22 individuals interacting with 15 cues.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates the feasibility of an interactive approach to identity recognition via motion patterns, offering a novel perspective and effective method for improving identification in practical applications.

Abstract: We consider the problem of identifying people based on their motion styles. We present a generative model describing the action instance creation process and derive a probabilistic identity inference scheme for two common person identification scenarios motivated by the surveillance and authentication applications. We introduce a novel, \emph{interactive}, scenario for person identification from motion patterns. To this end, we formalize the identification process in the context of a sequential message exchange session between the subject and the system. The subject's behavior is modeled using a probabilistic generative model inspired by the Human Information Processing (HIP) paradigm. At each stage, the system presents a visual stimulus (a cue) to the subject and records their motion response. The cue is selected so as to maximize the mutual information of the expected response and the subject's identity. Once recorded, the response is used to update the a posteriori probability over possible subjects' identities. The process terminates once a sufficient classification confidence level is reached. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time person identification is addressed in such interactive setting. We report high recognition rates on five publicly available datasets and our own novel dataset consisting of 4,476 recordings of 22 test subjects responding to 15 cues.

</details>


### [214] [VietFashion: Benchmarking Sketch-Text Composed Image Retrieval for Cultural Outfits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13427)
*Hoang-Nguyen Cao,Le-Hoang Bui,Dinh-Khoi Vo,Minh-Triet Tran,Trung-Nghia Le*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VietFashion introduces a benchmark dataset for sketch-text composed image retrieval centered on the Ao Dai, a traditional Vietnamese garment, with over 21,000 generated photorealistic images to aid fine-grained, culturally meaningful fashion retrieval.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address the challenge of retrieving culturally significant garments, which standard AI models struggle to process due to their dependence on subtle structural and symbolic details.

Method: The authors created a dataset called VietFashion, combining 650 sketches expanded to over 21,000 photorealistic images with aligned captions. They use textual prompts from authentic sources, adopt a multi-target retrieval setting, and establish evaluation protocols for cultural and structural garment retrieval.

Result: The experimental results demonstrate significant performance gaps in existing models, particularly in capturing cultural semantics and multi-modal composition during retrieval processes.

Conclusion: VietFashion is positioned as a challenging and valuable benchmark for advancing fine-grained, culturally significant fashion retrieval in AI, and makes its dataset openly available for research.

Abstract: Cultural garments pose a unique challenge for visual retrieval systems, as their identity often depends on subtle structural and symbolic details that are poorly captured by standard AI models. We introduce VietFashion, a new benchmark for sketch-text composed image retrieval centered on the Ao Dai, a traditional Vietnamese garment. VietFashion enables designers and researchers to retrieve culturally meaningful outfits using a combination of hand-drawn sketches, which convey garment structure, and textual descriptions, which encode cultural semantics. The dataset is initialized with 650 sketches and expanded using generative models to produce over 21,000 photorealistic images with aligned captions. Textual prompts that describe detailed outfit attributes, which are extracted from fashion magazines to ensure authenticity and diversity. To better reflect the inherent ambiguity of design intent, VietFashion adopts a multi-target retrieval setting, where a single query may correspond to multiple valid results. We establish standardized evaluation protocols and benchmark state-of-the-art composed image retrieval methods. Experimental results reveal significant performance gaps in modeling fine-grained cultural semantics and multi-modal composition, positioning VietFashion as a challenging benchmark for fine-grained fashion retrieval. The dataset is publicly available at: https://hng0303.github.io/VietFashion.

</details>


### [215] [OmniDirector: General Multi-Shot Camera Cloning without Cross-Paired Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13432)
*Jiwen Liu,Shujuan Li,Zhixue Fang,Xiaohan Li,Yan Zhou,Zijie Meng,Zhimin Zhang,Yawen Luo,Guoxin Zhang,Yu-Shen Liu,Pengfei Wan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces OmniDirector, a framework for cloning complex camera motions using a novel grid-based representation trained on large-scale data, achieving superior control and performance in video generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for cloning camera motion struggle with multi-shot generation and data scarcity, leading to poor performance in complex camera motion scenarios.

Method: The authors propose a novel grid-based camera motion representation and OmniDirector, a framework trained on extensive data, combined with a hierarchical prompt expansion agent to integrate control signals for directing multimodal diffusion transformers.

Result: OmniDirector demonstrates enhanced performance and controllability, excelling in precise and complex camera motion cloning for video generation.

Conclusion: The framework successfully addresses limitations of previous methods, providing a scalable and effective solution for camera motion cloning with applications in video generation.

Abstract: Cloning camera motion from reference videos is an important task in video generation, as videos provide intuitive and precise control. Existing methods either directly use parametric representations that fail to handle multi-shot generation or synthesize cross-paired data, which suffer from data scarcity, resulting in poor performance in complicated camera motion cloning. To address these issues, we introduce a general camera motion representation that encodes cameras as grid motion videos. This camera grid represents the camera parameters visually and supports the integration of diverse trajectories for multi-shot video generation. Building upon this, we propose OmniDirector, a unified framework trained on a million-scale camera grid-video pairs that coordinates characters, actions, and cameras to provide director-level control for multimodal diffusion transformers. Furthermore, we design a novel hierarchical prompt expansion agent that harmoniously integrates different control signals by systematically describing camera motion and visual content through understanding signal relationships. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superior performance and outstanding controllability of our framework. Project page: https://ymlinfeng.github.io/OmniDirector.github.io/

</details>


### [216] [VISA: VLM-Guided Instance Semantic Auditing for 3D Occupancy World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13460)
*Ruiqi Xian,Yuehan Xian,Jing Liang,Xuewei Qi,Dinesh Manocha*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces VISA, a semantic auditing method for 3D occupancy models in autonomous systems, achieving improved accuracy without changing inference methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of common visual language models (VLMs) in reliably improving closed-set occupancy accuracy and managing rare-class errors in semantic 3D occupancy models.

Method: VISA utilizes an offline VLM to audit each physical object instance, extracting class hypotheses, confusions, and reliability. The information is structured and condensed into semantic logits through proposed loss functions, while keeping inference unchanged.

Result: VISA demonstrated improved performance on the nuScenes dataset, increasing mIoU from 19.06 to 20.05 and improving rare-class and object mIoU metrics as well.

Conclusion: VISA shows that VLMs are more effective as semantic auditors for closed-set 3D occupancy systems rather than direct embedding targets, successfully addressing object-level and rare-class errors.

Abstract: Semantic 3D occupancy provides a voxelized world state for autonomous driving and robot decision making, but object and rare-class errors can affect free-space interpretation, collision checking, and temporal state propagation. We show that a common VLM strategy, aligning 3D voxel or object features with crop-caption embeddings, improves text-space similarity without reliably improving closed-set occupancy mIoU. Motivated by this mismatch, we propose VISA, a training-time semantic auditing approach for existing occupancy world models. VISA queries an offline VLM on a representative crop of each physical object instance, obtains a structured audit with class hypotheses, plausible confusions, reliability, attributes, and evidence, and propagates it along the object track. The audit is grounded to matched 3D object voxels and distilled into semantic logits through reliability-weighted taxonomy, attribute-factor, and scene-level audit graph losses, while inference remains unchanged and requires no VLM. On nuScenes, averaged across three runs, VISA improves OccWorld from 19.06 to 20.05 mIoU and GaussianWorld from 21.36 to 21.91 mIoU; on GaussianWorld, object mIoU improves from 18.18 to 19.16 and rare-class mIoU from 15.60 to 16.79. These results suggest that VLMs are better suited to closed-set occupancy as reliability-aware semantic auditors than as generic caption-embedding targets.

</details>


### [217] [Point-Wise Geometry-Aware Transformer for Partial-to-Full Point Cloud Registration in Computer-Assisted Surgery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13488)
*Siyu Zhou,Zhongliang Jiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces GAPR-Net, a novel framework for partial-to-full point cloud registration using transformers and convolution modules. It achieves state-of-the-art performance with high accuracy in experiments on medical data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Point cloud registration is a critical challenge in applications like computer-assisted surgery, where partial-to-full data alignment is necessary despite variations in overlap ratios, densities, and noise.

Method: The authors propose GAPR-Net, which integrates convolution and transformer modules in a coarse-to-fine architecture. It introduces a cross-attention mechanism for blending local and global information between partial and full point clouds, along with a transformation-invariant geometric feature representation.

Result: GAPR-Net achieves a high registration recall of 94.2%, a low RMSE of 1.992 mm, and $R^2$ values of 0.908 for rotation and 0.974 for translation during validation on four types of bone data.

Conclusion: The proposed GAPR-Net method effectively solves the partial-to-full point cloud registration problem with enhanced accuracy, providing a robust foundation for surgical navigation and robotic interventions.

Abstract: Partial-to-full registration remains challenging due to varying overlap ratios, fluctuating point densities, and the presence of noise. While transformers have shown strong potential for point cloud processing, prior methods typically confine them to global context aggregation, overlooking fine-grained local geometry crucial for accurate correspondence. We propose \emph{GAPR-Net}, a learning-based point cloud registration framework with a coarse-to-fine architecture that combines convolution and transformer modules, in which local and global information is fused between the partial and full point clouds using a cross-attention mechanism. To achieve this, a transformation-invariant point-wise geometric feature representation is proposed, which can robustly capture relative geometric features for individual points with respect to their neighboring points. To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed approach, experiments are conducted on four geometrically distinct bones, including the tibia, femur, pelvis, and thoracic cartilage. The overall registration recall reaches 94.2\%, the method results in a low RMSE of 1.992 mm and $R^2$ values of 0.908 and 0.974 for rotation and translation, respectively. The results demonstrate that the proposed method effectively addresses the partial-to-full point cloud registration problem. The proposed method enables highly accurate 3D point cloud registration using partial observation, providing a critical foundation for precise surgical navigation and robotic interventions in computer-assisted surgery. The code will be accessed after the double-blind review process.

</details>


### [218] [Budget-Constrained Step-Level Diffusion Caching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13496)
*Mingkun Lei,Tong Zhao,Liangyu Yuan,Chi Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper presents BudCache, a step-level caching method for diffusion models that optimizes output quality under fixed compute budgets, significantly improving generation quality compared to existing heuristic approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing step-level caching methods for diffusion models rely on error threshold-based heuristics for cache decisions, which results in variable inference latency and suboptimal output quality. This presents limitations in controlling runtime for deployment and optimizing final outputs.

Method: BudCache fixes the compute budget upfront and identifies optimal cache policies that maximize final output quality. It uses an offline search combining Simulated Annealing and deterministic Hill Climbing to efficiently navigate the complex step selection space. Additional cache-aware schedule alignment mitigates trajectory mismatch when compute budgets are very constrained.

Result: Experiments demonstrate that BudCache outperforms heuristic caching baselines in generation quality for the same inference budgets on datasets FLUX.1-dev and Wan2.1.

Conclusion: BudCache is an effective step-level caching solution for diffusion models that significantly enhances output quality under fixed compute constraints. Its novel budget-driven approach and low-overhead execution make it particularly valuable for deployment settings.

Abstract: Step-level caching accelerates diffusion models by exploiting temporal redundancy across denoising steps. Existing methods make per-step cache decisions using threshold-based heuristics, without directly optimizing for final output quality. As a result, their inference latency varies across inputs and is difficult to control at deployment. In this work, we propose BudCache, which inverts this formulation: rather than letting per-step error thresholds dictate the runtime cost, we fix the compute budget in advance and search for the cache policy that best preserves the final output. To tackle the combinatorial complexity of step selection, we combine Simulated Annealing with deterministic Hill Climbing. This offline search identifies high-quality cache policies within minutes and introduces no online search or thresholding overhead during inference. When the compute budget is very tight, we further introduce cache-aware schedule alignment, which adapts the time discretization to the selected cache policy to reduce cache-induced trajectory mismatch. Experiments on FLUX.1-dev and Wan2.1 show that BudCache achieves better generation quality than heuristic caching baselines under the same inference budgets. Code is available at https://github.com/Westlake-AGI-Lab/BudCache

</details>


### [219] [Measurement-Calibrated Multi-Camera Fusion for Vision-Based Indoor Localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13509)
*Mateo Toro Diz,Jonathan Hoss,Noah Klarmann*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper evaluates how explicit error characterization in single-camera localization can optimize multi-camera data fusion for indoor vision-based localization systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Indoor vision-based localization systems face challenges like detection noise, occlusions, and limited camera coverage. These challenges introduce uncertainty, and while multi-camera fusion is used to mitigate these effects, its workings often remain unexamined. The paper aims to address how understanding single-camera errors can enhance fusion strategies.

Method: The authors propose a measurement-calibrated fusion method that explicitly quantifies errors from homography calibration, human detection, and motion tracking. They then integrate this error quantification into a new data fusion approach and assess its impact both component-wise and in full-scale experiments.

Result: The study finds that while measurement-calibrated data fusion only slightly enhances absolute localization accuracy compared to standard fusion techniques, it significantly reduces trajectory variance and improves motion smoothness, ensuring better performance for applications requiring stable motion estimates.

Conclusion: Explicit error characterization is valuable in designing effective multi-camera fusion strategies, particularly for applications that prioritize stability and smooth motion tracking in indoor positioning systems.

Abstract: Indoor vision-based localization systems are affected by detection noise, occlusions, and limited camera coverage, leading to uncertainty at multiple stages of the pipeline. While multi-camera data fusion is widely used to mitigate these issues, it is typically treated as a black-box component and evaluated solely end-to-end, obscuring its mechanistic contributions. To address this gap, this work investigates whether explicitly characterizing single-camera localization errors can be leveraged to calibrate and optimize multi-camera data fusion.
  We introduce a measurement-calibrated fusion approach that integrates component-wise error quantification, specifically isolating homography calibration, human detection, and motion tracking. A component-wise evaluation is conducted to quantify error contributions from homography calibration, human detection, and motion tracking.
  Experimental results show that data fusion improves localization accuracy compared to single-camera baselines. While measurement-calibrated fusion provides only limited improvement in absolute accuracy over standard fusion, it substantially reduces trajectory variance and improves motion smoothness, which are critical for applications requiring stable and continuous motion estimates. These results highlight the value of explicit error characterization when designing data fusion strategies for vision-based indoor positioning systems.

</details>


### [220] [What's Old is New Again: Classical Dimensionality Reduction for Efficient Saliency-Guided Biometric Attack Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13528)
*Samuel Webster,Walter Scheirer*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The authors propose a cost-efficient, scalable approach for saliency-guided training in biometric presentation attack detection using saliency maps derived from PCA and LDA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing saliency-guided training methods suffer from high costs, domain specificity, and limited scalability, thus hindering their widespread adoption.

Method: Saliency maps are generated from raw training data via PCA and LDA, eliminating the need for human annotations or domain-specific knowledge.

Result: The proposed saliency maps improve model performance across five biometric detection tasks, sometimes outperforming baseline and state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: The authors' approach simplifies and enhances saliency-guided training, making it more viable for broader applications beyond biometrics.

Abstract: Saliency-guided training is a paradigm in visual recognition that encourages models to focus on the most relevant image regions during learning. While its application in biometric presentation attack detection (PAD) has shown strong benefits in robustness and generalization, adoption is often limited by the high cost, domain specificity, and limited scalability of existing saliency acquisition methods, such as human annotations over a limited dataset. We present a novel, cost-efficient, and highly-scalable approach to saliency acquisition using maps inspired by classical dimensionality reduction techniques: PCA and LDA. Our proposed methods generate saliency maps directly from raw training data, requiring no human annotation nor domain knowledge. We contextualize the effectiveness of these saliency sources in three saliency-explored domains (iris PAD, synthetic face detection, fingerprint PAD) and demonstrate its scalability in two saliency-novel domains (fingerprint vein PAD and ID card PAD). Across all domains tested, models trained using dimensionality reduction-sourced saliency maps exceed baseline and sometimes SOTA saliency methods without any resource investment or domain-specific tooling. Our findings overcome an important yet unaddressed barrier to saliency-guided training for biometric attack detection and beyond.

</details>


### [221] [Edit the Bits, Diff the Codes: Bitwise Residual Editing for Visual Autoregressive Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13558)
*Shengqiang Zhang,Ruotong Liao,Volker Tresp,Barbara Plank,Hinrich Schütze*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces BitResEdit, a method for text-guided image editing using bitwise-residual visual autoregressive (VAR) generators, achieving improved text alignment and competitive background preservation without additional training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing text-guided VAR editors underutilize the per-bit Bernoulli prediction head and additive multi-scale residual code field, which could improve editing precision and localization.

Method: BitResEdit incorporates BitEdit for source-negative guidance via per-bit log-odds adjustments and ResEdit to apply localized and scale-aware edits while preserving untouched latent features through native code composition.

Result: On PIE-Bench with Infinity-2B, BitResEdit achieves the best text alignment among VAR editors (+1.07 CLIP improvement on the edited region) while maintaining competitive background preservation.

Conclusion: BitResEdit successfully combines decision-time bit guidance with native scale-aware editing, demonstrating its efficacy for localized and precise text-guided image editing.

Abstract: Text-guided image editing with visual autoregressive (VAR) generators requires controlling both what the model samples and where the sampled change is written back into the image code. Existing VAR editors mainly operate on token streams, features, or flat next-token logits, leaving two native structures of bitwise-residual VAR models underused: the per-bit Bernoulli prediction head and the additive multi-scale residual code field from which the image is assembled. We propose BitResEdit, a training-free editor for bitwise-residual VAR generators such as Infinity. BitEdit performs source-negative guidance by tilting the post-CFG per-bit log-odds along a source--target contrast computed on a shared edited prefix, then projects each update into a closed-form Bernoulli-KL trust region around the clean CFG sampler. ResEdit converts the sampled bits into per-scale continuous-code residuals, gates them with a localization mask, and re-injects them through the generator's native sum-of-scales. Together they couple decision-time bit guidance with combination-time code composition, so masked-out latent features are preserved exactly by code arithmetic while localized, scale-aware edits are applied inside the target region. On PIE-Bench with Infinity-2B, BitResEdit attains the strongest text alignment among same-backbone VAR editors, improving CLIP on the edited region by +1.07 over the strongest prior editor while keeping background preservation competitive with it. Ablations show BitEdit and ResEdit play complementary roles in target alignment and background preservation.

</details>


### [222] [Contrast-Informed Augmentation and Domain-Adversarial Training for Adult-to-Neonatal MR Reconstruction Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13562)
*Stephen Moore,Lara Leijser,Richard Frayne,Roberto Souza*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper evaluates methods to improve generalization of MR reconstruction models from adult to neonatal data using augmentation and adversarial training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need to address domain shift when applying deep learning MR reconstruction models trained on adult data to neonatal data.

Method: The study compared three training strategies: adult-only training, mixed training with augmented data, and mixed training with an adversarial objective. Models were trained on T2-weighted MR data and evaluated using metrics like SSIM and PSNR.

Result: Mixed training and domain-adversarial training outperformed adult-only training. Mixed-DAT achieved the best neonatal MR reconstruction results at R=4, and had varying performance at R=8 depending on the evaluation metric.

Conclusion: Contrast-informed augmentation and domain-adversarial training enhance the robustness and generalization to neonatal data in undersampled MR reconstruction tasks.

Abstract: Purpose: To investigate whether contrast-informed data augmentation and domain-adversarial training improve the adult-to-neonatal generalization of the E2E-VarNet. Methods: Three training regimes were investigated: (1) adult-only training with unaugmented adult data, (2) mixed training with paired unaugmented and neonatal-informed augmented adult data, and (3) mixed training with a domain-adversarial objective. Models were trained on retrospectively undersampled multi-coil adult T2-weighted brain MR data and evaluated on neonatal and adult test data at acceleration factors $R=4$ and $R=8$ using quantitative metrics and qualitative evaluation. Feature analyses assessed whether domain-adversarial training altered the latent representations of unaugmented adult, augmented adult, and neonatal test samples. Results: Mixed training (Mixed) and mixed domain-adversarial training (Mixed-DAT) outperformed unaugmented adult-only training (Unaug-Only) when evaluated on neonatal data. At R=4, Mixed-DAT achieved the best performance (SSIM = 0.924 +/- 0.027, PSNR = 33.98 +/- 1.15 dB). At R=8, Mixed-DAT performed best when measured using SSIM (0.848 +/- 0.031 vs. 0.766 +/- 0.037 for Unaug-Only and 0.814 +/- 0.035 for Mixed) and Mixed performed best when measured using PSNR (29.56 +/- 0.83 dB vs. 26.26 +/- 0.78 dB for Unaug-Only and 29.43 +/- 0.83 dB for Mixed-DAT). Qualitative assessment of t-SNE plots suggested that Mixed-DAT increased the overlap among the latent representations of the unaugmented adult, augmented adult, and neonatal test data. Conclusion: Contrast-informed augmentation and domain-adversarial training improved adult-to-neonatal generalization of deep learning-based MR reconstruction. These findings suggest that contrast-informed data augmentation combined with adversarial training may improve robustness to domain shift in undersampled neonatal MR reconstruction.

</details>


### [223] [EvTexture++: Event-Driven Texture Enhancement for Video Super-Resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13580)
*Dachun Kai,Jiayao Lu,Yueyi Zhang,Xiaoyan Sun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces EvTexture++, an event-driven framework focusing on texture enhancement for video super-resolution (VSR) by leveraging high-frequency spatiotemporal details from event-based vision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need to improve texture recovery and temporal consistency in video super-resolution, especially by utilizing the distinct properties of event-based vision like high temporal resolution and dynamic range.

Method: Proposes EvTexture++, which includes a custom texture enhancement branch, iterative texture enhancement module, and temporal texture alignment module to exploit event-based motion cues for better texture recovery and inter-frame alignment.

Result: On five datasets, EvTexture++ delivers state-of-the-art performance, with integration into existing VSR models improving PSNR values by up to 1.55 dB on texture-rich Vid4 dataset.

Conclusion: EvTexture++ is an effective, plug-and-play framework for enhancing texture recovery and temporal consistency in VSR, proving to be a versatile and performance-boosting tool for existing models.

Abstract: Event-based vision has drawn increasing attention owing to its distinctive properties, including ultra-high temporal resolution and extreme dynamic range. Recent works have introduced it to video super-resolution (VSR) to enhance flow estimation and temporal alignment. In contrast, this paper shifts the focus of event signals from motion refinement to texture enhancement in VSR. We propose EvTexture++, the first event-driven framework dedicated to texture enhancement in VSR. It leverages high-frequency spatiotemporal details from events to improve texture recovery. EvTexture++ incorporates a customized texture enhancement branch, along with an iterative texture enhancement module that progressively exploits high-temporal-resolution event information for texture restoration. This enables gradual refinement of texture regions across iterations, yielding more accurate and detailed high-resolution outputs. Besides intra-frame texture recovery, large motions could degrade inter-frame temporal consistency, particularly in texture regions, leading to texture flickering. To mitigate this, we further exploit the continuous-time motion cues of events to enhance temporal consistency, introducing a temporal texture alignment module that estimates event-guided texture-aware flow for precise inter-frame texture alignment. Moreover, EvTexture++ is designed as a plug-and-play tool to flexibly boost the performance of existing VSR models. Experiments on five datasets demonstrate that EvTexture++ achieves state-of-the-art performance. When integrated into recent VSR models, it yields significant improvements, with gains of up to 1.55 dB in PSNR on the texture-rich Vid4 dataset. Code: https://github.com/DachunKai/EvTexture.

</details>


### [224] [Towards Effective Waste Segmentation for Automated Waste Recycling in Cluttered Background](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13587)
*Mamoona Javaid,Mubashir Noman,Abdul Hannan,Shah Nawaz,Mustansar Fiaz,Sajid Ghuffar*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new deep learning-based approach for automated waste segmentation that optimizes both spatial and spectral features, overcoming limitations in existing methods for cluttered scenes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The rise in urban waste production necessitates efficient and automated waste management systems, where current deep learning solutions are inefficient and fail in complex scenarios.

Method: A cascaded network design is introduced, combining spatial and spectral domains for better local and global feature extraction, coupled with an Auxiliary Feature Enhancement Module (AFEM) to improve object boundaries and segmentation.

Result: The proposed method achieves superior performance on ZeroWaste-aug, ZeroWaste-f, and SpectralWaste datasets, demonstrating its effectiveness in cluttered environments.

Conclusion: The paper delivers a computationally efficient and effective model that addresses critical challenges in automated waste recycling, paving the way for practical deep learning applications in waste management.

Abstract: Rapid expansion of urban areas and population growth is causing an immense increase in waste production, which demands the need for efficient and automated waste management. In this scenario, automated waste recycling (AWR) using deep learning methods can assist humans in optimal waste management. Recent deep learning approaches for AWR provide promising waste segmentation performance, however, these methods rely on large backbone networks that are inefficient for AWR systems and suffer from performance deterioration in cluttered scenes. To this end, an optimal waste segmentation network is introduced which effectively utilizes the spatial domain to capture localized structural dependencies and the spectral domain to efficiently extract global contextual relationships. This cascaded design allows the network to progressively leverage both local and global representations across complementary domains to highlight the semantic information necessary for effective segmentation of various waste objects. Furthermore, auxiliary feature enhancement module (AFEM) is introduced to enhance the target objects' boundaries and blob amplification for better segmentation in cluttered scenarios. Extensive experimentation on ZeroWaste-aug, ZeroWaste-f and SpectralWaste datasets reveals the merits of the proposed method.

</details>


### [225] [Revisiting Vehicle Color Recognition in Long-Tailed Surveillance Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13625)
*Vinícius Orrú,Bruno H. Foggiatto,Gabriel E. Lima,David Menotti,Rayson Laroca*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper enhances vehicle color recognition under imbalanced real-world conditions using generative strategies, achieving 94.6% micro accuracy and 79.7% macro accuracy with methodologies and data augmentation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to improve vehicle identification in surveillance systems where license plates are unreadable and real-world color distributions are imbalanced, impacting operational effectiveness.

Method: The study employs synthetic data generation via generative models (RunDiffusion/JuggernautXL and Gemini 2.0 Flash) alongside loss reweighting, learning-rate scheduling, and multiple preprocessing and augmentation strategies.

Result: The best method achieved 94.6% micro accuracy and 79.7% macro accuracy on the UFPR-VeSV dataset, presenting an 8.2% macro accuracy improvement over prior work.

Conclusion: The results highlight both progress and practical limitations in vehicle color recognition under uncontrolled surveillance settings. Remaining errors often stem from visually ambiguous cases for humans. Public resources are made available for further research.

Abstract: Vehicle color recognition is an important cue for vehicle identification in surveillance systems, especially when license plates are illegible due to low resolution, occlusion, motion blur, or poor illumination. However, real-world vehicle color distributions are highly imbalanced, making overall accuracy insufficient to assess performance on rare but operationally relevant colors. This paper presents a comprehensive study of vehicle color recognition under severe class imbalance using UFPR-VeSV, a challenging real-world surveillance dataset. We investigate synthetic minority-class augmentation through two off-the-shelf generative strategies: text-conditioned image generation with RunDiffusion/JuggernautXL and image-conditioned color editing with Gemini 2.0 Flash. The curated synthetic data are combined with modern visual representations, loss reweighting, learning-rate scheduling, color-safe augmentation, foreground-aware preprocessing, and ensemble fusion. The bestperforming approach achieves 94.6% micro accuracy and 79.7% macro accuracy, improving macro accuracy by 8.2 percentage points over recent literature. A manual error analysis further shows that many remaining failures are visually ambiguous even for human annotators, highlighting the practical limits of color-based vehicle identification in unconstrained surveillance imagery. The generated images and source code are publicly available at https://github.com/viniciusorru/vcr-synthetic

</details>


### [226] [Surflo: Consistent 3D Surface Flow Model with Global State](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13644)
*Antoine Guédon,Shu Nakamura,Nicolas Dufour,Jiahui Lei,Ko Nishino,Angjoo Kanazawa*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Surflo is a novel reconstruction method that compresses images into latent tokens, decodes surface points using flow matching, and offers arbitrary-resolution decoding, outperforming existing models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing reconstruction models fail to exploit the geometric redundancy in images, often leading to inefficiencies, fixed outputs, or inconsistencies.

Method: Surflo compresses an arbitrary number of unposed RGB images into K latent tokens, then decodes oriented 3D surface points using flow matching, enhanced with an inference-time guidance term to improve consistency.

Result: Surflo outperforms feed-forward baselines in surface metrics, drastically speeds up inference compared to optimization-based methods, and uniquely combines global latent representation with arbitrary-resolution decoding.

Conclusion: Surflo introduces a more efficient and scalable approach to 3D reconstruction, leveraging flow matching for better resolution flexibility and accuracy, surpassing previous feed-forward and optimization-based methods.

Abstract: Geometry is invariant to viewpoint, which makes any collection of images a redundant encoding of a single 3D state. Existing feed-forward reconstruction models fail to exploit this: per-view methods emit overlapping, unaligned pointmaps that grow linearly with input count, while global-latent methods commit to a fixed, low-resolution output. We introduce Surflo, which compresses a variable number of unposed RGB views into K latent tokens-one global state-and decodes oriented 3D surface points by independently transporting them from noise onto the surface via flow matching. This frees the output from any fixed grid or token budget: the same latent yields from a few thousand to a million points in a single forward pass. To suppress the local inconsistencies inherent to independent per-point decoding, an inference-time guidance term correlates nearby points by injecting a photometric gradient during ODE integration. Surflo matches or surpasses feed-forward baselines on surface metrics, runs an order of magnitude faster than optimization-based methods that require hundreds of views, and is the only feed-forward approach to combine a global latent with arbitrary-resolution decoding.

</details>


### [227] [World Tracing: Generative Pixel-Aligned Geometry Beyond the Visible](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13652)
*Hao Zhang,Mohamed El Banani,Jen-Hao Cheng,Paul Zhang,Yi Hua,Ben Mildenhall,Christoph Lassner,Narendra Ahuja,Gengshan Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces 'World Tracing,' a method that predicts both visible and occluded 3D geometry from input images, improving faithfulness and completeness over existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing image-to-3D methods struggle to balance faithfulness to the input image with the generation of complete 3D geometry, particularly missing alignment for occluded surfaces.

Method: World Tracing predicts pixel-aligned, camera-space 3D points through a generative transformer (WT-DiT), managing visible and occluded surfaces with a novel mixed noise schedule and flow-matching training approach.

Result: World Tracing outperforms existing methods in both visible-surface reconstruction and complete geometry generation while maintaining 2D-to-3D alignment. It enables tasks like 3D editing and view synthesis.

Conclusion: World Tracing effectively addresses the limitations of prior methods by providing accurate, complete, and editable 3D geometry with wide applicability across objects, scenes, and dynamic content.

Abstract: Image-to-3D methods often trade off faithfulness and completeness: depth estimators are anchored to input pixels but stop at the visible surface, while image-to-3D models generate complete shapes that are often misaligned with the input. We introduce World Tracing, a generative pixel-aligned geometry representation that predicts 3D points aligned with observed pixels while completing geometry beyond the visible surface. For each input pixel, World Tracing predicts an ordered stack of camera-space 3D points, where the first layer represents the visible surface and subsequent layers represent front-to-back intersections with occluded surfaces. We instantiate this representation with a world-tracing diffusion transformer, WT-DiT, which treats multiple geometry layers as separate denoising tokens coupled through factorized and global attention. WT-DiT is trained with pixel-space flow matching and a mixed noise schedule that balances visible-surface reconstruction with occluded-geometry generation. World Tracing achieves strong performance on visible-surface reconstruction and complete geometry generation across object, scene, and dynamic benchmarks, outperforming both depth predictors and image-to-3D generators. It also preserves 2D-to-3D correspondence, enabling text-driven 3D scene editing, geometry-conditioned novel-view video synthesis, and training-free integration with textured-mesh generators.

</details>


### [228] [Flex4DHuman: Flexible Multi-view Video Diffusion for 4D Human Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13655)
*Jen-Hao Cheng,Yipeng Wang,Hao Zhang,Gengshan Yang,Jenq-Neng Hwang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Flex4DHuman is a multi-view video diffusion model that enables dynamic 4D content creation from monocular or sparse multi-view videos without explicit geometry priors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenges of transforming sparse monocular or multi-view videos into synchronized dense multi-view videos for scalable 4D content creation.

Method: The method involves a diffusion model with relative camera-pose positional encoding, a three-stage training curriculum, and integration with 4D Gaussian Splatting.

Result: Flex4DHuman achieves state-of-the-art results in synthesizing dynamic 4D Gaussian splats, generalizing to human and animal categories.

Conclusion: The framework offers a step forward in creating scalable 4D content from casual videos, beneficial for simulation, gaming, AR/VR, and video production.

Abstract: We present Flex4DHuman, a multi-view video diffusion model that transforms a monocular or sparse multi-view video of a dynamic subject into synchronized dense multi-view videos using only relative camera-pose conditioning. Unlike prior human-centric methods that rely on skeletons, depth maps, normals, or rendered target-view geometry, Flex4DHuman requires no explicit geometry priors and instead conditions generation through relative camera-pose positional encoding. The generated videos can be directly ingested by downstream reconstruction pipelines to create dynamic 4D Gaussian splats. Built on the Wan 2.1 1.3B text-to-video model, Flex4DHuman preserves the backbone architecture and encodes camera and view information through a five-axis positional encoding that extends spatio-temporal RoPE with view indices and continuous SE(3) relative camera geometry. A three-stage curriculum progressively trains the model for pose following, flexible reference-to-target view generation, and temporal rollout. To support temporal rollout, we train with clean historical target-view tokens. We also add multi-view captions to enable test-time text control. Combined with an off-the-shelf 4D Gaussian Splatting stage, our framework lifts monocular static-camera videos into dynamic 4D Gaussian splats. Experiments on DNA-Rendering and ActorsHQ show that Flex4DHuman surpasses prior state-of-the-art methods, while the same formulation generalizes to animal categories after mixed human-animal training. These capabilities make Flex4DHuman a practical step toward scalable 4D content creation from casual monocular videos for simulation, gaming, AR/VR, and video re-shooting.

</details>


### [229] [SpatialClaw: Rethinking Action Interface for Agentic Spatial Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13673)
*Seokju Cho,Ryo Hachiuma,Abhishek Badki,Hang Su,Byung-Kwan Lee,Chan Hee Song,Sifei Liu,Subhashree Radhakrishnan,Seungryong Kim,Yu-Chiang Frank Wang,Min-Hung Chen*

Main category: cs.CV
