In multi-agent scenes involving humans or morphologically identical robots, the system reliably identifies itself, learns a 3D self-model, and supports downstream tasks including target reaching, collision-aware motion planning, and human-to-robot motion retargeting. Together, these results outline a route toward bodily self-representation in robots that act and coordinate alongside others in shared physical environments. Project page: https://euron-zc.github.io/humanoid-self-model/.

</details>


### [408] [WT-UMI: Tactile-based Whole-Body Manipulation via Force-Supervised Contact-Aware Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13232)
*Jaehwi Jang,Zhaoyuan Gu,Alfred Cueva,Zimeng Chai,Junjie Sheng,Thong Nguyen,Himank Galundia,Yifan Wu,Huishu Xue,Isaac Legene,Ojas Mediratta,Davin Doan,Andrew Collins,Sarah Sadegh,KyoungMok Kim,Rishita Dhalbisoi,Zun Chen,Ye Zhao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces WT-UMI, a wearable tactile interface aiding humanoid manipulation of deformable or shared-load objects by integrating tactile feedback into learning-based force-aware control systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenges in whole-body humanoid manipulation of complex objects due to inadequate attention to contact forces in prior imitation policies and the lack of complementary modalities from human and teleoperation demonstrations.

Method: WT-UMI combines human demonstrations and humanoid teleoperation data using a whole-body tactile interface to provide tactile images, forces, and pose data. A force-conditioned pose correction module converts human inputs into robot-executable targets, and a force-supervised planner generates predictive pose and force trajectories, working alongside an admittance controller.

Result: The system demonstrated improved success rates and reduced tracking errors across five contact-rich tasks involving deformable objects, bulky objects, and collaborative scenarios.

Conclusion: WT-UMI advances humanoid capabilities in handling complex tasks by integrating force-aware planning and tactile feedback, bridging gaps between human and robot demonstrations for more effective imitation policies.

Abstract: Whole-body humanoid manipulation of bulky, deformable, and shared-load objects requires distributed contact sensing and explicit force regulation, yet most imitation policies treat contact force only implicitly. On the other hand, different demonstration sources provide complementary modalities with inherent trade-offs: human demonstrations capture natural contact forces but not robot-executable actions, while teleoperation directly records robot actions but with less natural force regulation. This paper presents \textbf{WT-UMI}, a wearable whole-body tactile interface worn by human operators or mounted on humanoids, providing accurate observations of tactile images, contact forces, and end-effector poses across both human demonstration and humanoid teleoperation modes. We introduce a force-conditioned target-pose correction module that converts measured human poses into contact-aware robot targets by learning corrections from teleoperation data. To leverage the natural force interaction in human data, we propose a force-supervised planner that predicts end-effector pose chunks and contact-force trajectories. The predicted contact force serves as the reference for a tactile-based admittance controller. Across five contact-rich tasks spanning deformable objects, bulky rigid objects, and human--humanoid collaboration, WT-UMI improves success rate and reduces contact-position tracking error over four policy baselines. Our project page is available at https://wt-umi.github.io/WTUMI/.

</details>


### [409] [Humor Style Drives Laughter, Topic Shapes Acceptability: Evaluating Bilingual Personal and Political Robot-Delivered AI Jokes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13256)
*Anna-Maria Velentza,Anne-Gwenn Bosser*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The study explores how humor type, joke content, and language preference influence the perception of robot-delivered humor in group settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Humor is central to human relationships, but its role in human-robot interaction (HRI) is underexplored. Advances in computational humor present new ways to integrate humor into HRI.

Method: Participants in a university classroom evaluated AI-generated jokes delivered by a robot, testing the effects of humor type, joke content, and language preference using a mixed factorial design.

Result: Aggressive and Affiliative humor types were perceived as funnier, while person-related jokes were deemed more appropriate than political ones. Language preference depended on joke content and participants' fluency and humor practices.

Conclusion: Humor perceptions in HRI are shaped by humor type, content, and language preferences, providing insights for designing humor in robotic interactions.

Abstract: Humor plays a central role in human social relationships, and recent advances in computational humor create new opportunities for integrating humor into human-robot interaction (HRI). While large language models (LLMs) can generate diverse forms of humor, it remains unclear how humor style, joke content, and language preference shape perceptions of robot-delivered humor in group settings. In this exploratory study, we employed a mixed factorial design in which participants evaluated AI-generated jokes delivered by a robot in a university classroom. We examined the effects of humor type (Affiliative, Self-Enhancing, Aggressive, Self-Defeating) and joke content (person-related vs. political) on perceived funniness and appropriateness, as well as preferred language. Results show that humor type significantly influences funniness, with Aggressive and Affiliative humor rated higher, while joke content primarily affects appropriateness, with person-related jokes preferred over political ones. Language preference was shaped by both joke content and participants' self-reported fluency and humor practices.

</details>


### [410] [See Selectively, Act Adaptively: Dual-Level Structural Decomposition for Bimanual Robot Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13279)
*Yoon-Ji Choi,Young-Chae Son,Soo-Chul Lim*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces a dual-level structural decomposition framework for bimanual robotic manipulation to handle task-relevant visual information and varying bimanual interaction modes, improving success rates in both simulated and real-world tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies struggle to handle diverse visual inputs and bimanual interaction patterns within a shared representation and action generation framework.

Method: The framework includes a View-Selective Visual Router to prioritize relevant visual cues and an Interaction-Aware Action Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) for coordinated and independent action pathways.

Result: The proposed method achieves a 27.7% higher average success rate in simulation and 43.3% in real-world scenarios compared to a monolithic baseline.

Conclusion: Selective visual processing combined with explicit bimanual interaction decomposition provides a robust solution for improving bimanual robotic manipulation tasks.

Abstract: In bimanual robotic manipulation, task-relevant visual information varies with the task stage and context, while the interaction of the two arms shifts between independent and coordinated modes, making policy learning challenging. However, existing monolithic Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies process diverse visual inputs and interaction patterns through a single shared representation and action generation pathway, often failing to separately account for visual relevance and bimanual interaction structure. To address this issue, we propose a bimanual manipulation VLA framework based on Dual-Level Structural Decomposition. The View-Selective Visual Router dynamically adjusts wrist-view contributions to emphasize relevant visual cues, while the Interaction-Aware Action Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) decomposes action generation into coordinated and arm-wise pathways to adapt to varying bimanual interaction modes. We evaluate the proposed method on six simulated bimanual manipulation tasks in RoboTwin 2.0 and three long-horizon real-world tasks. Our model improves the overall average success rate over a monolithic baseline by 27.7% in simulation and 43.3% in real-world evaluation, while consistently outperforming single-module variants across both settings. These results demonstrate that jointly considering selective visual processing and explicit decomposition of bimanual interaction structures provides an effective inductive bias for robust bimanual manipulation.

</details>


### [411] [EMG-Based Adaptation of Anisotropic Virtual Fixtures for Robot-Assisted Surgical Resection and Dissection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13340)
*Dario Onfiani,Michael Dyck,Luigi Biagiotti,Julian Klodmann*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper develops an adaptive assistance system using EMG signals for robot-assisted laparoscopic surgery, enabling dynamic control over tool constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional Virtual Fixtures in robotic surgery are rigid and cannot adapt to a surgical workflow or a surgeon's intent, creating a need for a flexible and adaptive solution.

Method: The authors introduce an adaptive anisotropic virtual fixture framework with a control interface that uses forearm EMG signals to dynamically modulate constraints during surgery.

Result: Experimental results from a pilot study show improvements in task accuracy, consistency, and reduced cognitive load, effort, and frustration.

Conclusion: The proposed adaptive system enhances surgeon performance and ergonomics, demonstrating its potential for increasing precision and usability in robotic surgical workflows.

Abstract: In this paper, we address the development of an adaptive assistance system for robot-assisted laparoscopic surgery, specifically for delicate tasks such as Resection and Dissection. Even if Virtual Fixtures offer significant advantages for guiding a surgeon's movements, conventional Virtual Fixtures are often defined by fixed geometries, lacking the flexibility to adapt to the surgical workflow or the surgeon's immediate intent. To address these limitations, we propose a novel framework for an adaptive and anisotropic virtual fixture. In addition, we introduce an intuitive control interface that modulates the fixture's geometry in real-time based on the surgeon's intent, inferred from EMG signals. This approach allows the surgeon to dynamically expand or disengage the constraint by contracting their forearm muscles, enabling seamless transitions between precise guided motion and free repositioning of the tool. Experimental results from a pilot user study, based on a standardized surgical training task, demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method. The system showed significant improvements in task accuracy and movement consistency, alongside a reduction in perceived cognitive load, effort, and frustration.

</details>


### [412] [Low cost, easily manufactured, highly flexible strain and touch sensitive fiber for robotics applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13352)
*Christian Diaz Herrera,Srushti Raste,Simin Liu,Miles Modeste,Jiyang,Yin,Katelyn McCall,Yuxing Jared Yao,Roopkamal Chahal,Simon Chidley,Trung Ha,T. David Westmoreland,Sonia Roberts*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces a cost-effective and rapid-manufacture conductive fiber for robots that functions as both resistive strain and capacitive sensors in different applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many existing stretch and touch sensors are prohibitively expensive in terms of materials, manufacturing equipment, or time, necessitating a low-cost, rapidly manufacturable alternative.

Method: The authors characterized a conductive fiber made from inexpensive commercial components (conductive thread, silicone tubing, needle threader) and demonstrated its effectiveness in a series of resistive strain and capacitive sensing applications.

Result: The fiber was successfully used for five applications: resistive sensing for robotic grip and pose estimation, and capacitive sensing for touch and near-field detection, while also showcasing repairability and manufacturing scalability.

Conclusion: The developed fibers offer a cost-effective, versatile, and scalable alternative to conventional sensors, making them highly practical for robotic applications.

Abstract: Existing stretch and touch sensors for robots are generally expensive with respect to at least one of material costs, required manufacturing equipment, or manufacturing time. We present and experimentally characterize a conductive fiber made using only inexpensive commercial off-the-shelf parts (conductive thread at $0.07/ft, silicone tubing at $0.94/ft) and tools (loop-style needle threader at $2), which can be manufactured quickly (20 cm length in 2 minutes.) We demonstrate its use as a resistive strain sensor with three applications: Triggering a grasp in a pneumatically actuated assistive finger, sensing the pose of a pneumatically actuated robotic strap, and estimating the pose of a flexible solid. We also demonstrate that it can be used as a capacitive sensor with two applications: First, as a touch sensor which triggers a commercial robot arm to move, and second, as a near-field sensor enabling the robot arm to follow a moving hand. The capacitive sensors are knitted, showcasing the high flexibility of the fiber. We discuss methods for improving manufacturing scalability and their cost trade-offs. Finally, we demonstrate a method for repairing a cut fiber.

</details>


### [413] [Real-Time Execution with Autoregressive Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13355)
*Sangkyu Lee,Seohyeon Park,Tackgeun You,Avi Caciularu,Idan Szpektor,Hwasup Lim,Youngjae Yu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper demonstrates that autoregressive policies can achieve real-time execution in large-scale Vision-Language-Action models by adjusting tokenization horizons and using constrained decoding, outperforming flow-matching policies in task completion speeds and performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To solve the critical issue of real-time execution in autoregressive policies, which traditionally suffer slower rollout speeds compared to other policies such as diffusion ones.

Method: Adjust tokenization horizon and apply constrained decoding to improve latency bounds, paired with multi-trajectory decoding for enhanced performance.

Result: Autoregressive policies surpassed flow-matching policies in both simulated and real-world environments by achieving faster task completion speeds and better performance.

Conclusion: Autoregressive policies not only support real-time execution but also offer faster convergence, better instruction generalizability, and remain competitive for deployment in Vision-Language-Action systems.

Abstract: Real-time execution, enabled by asynchronous inference that ensures both smooth action trajectories and fast reactivity, is critical for realistic deployments of large-scale Vision-Language-Action models. However, recent work on real-time execution primarily focuses on variants of diffusion policies, even though it is more critical for autoregressive policies given their slower rollout speed in synchronous inference. In contrast, we demonstrate that autoregressive policies can achieve real-time execution by adjusting the tokenization horizon and applying constrained decoding, thereby guaranteeing strict latency bounds that enable multi-trajectory decoding to maximize performance. Across simulated and real-world environments, we find that the autoregressive policy consistently outperforms its equivalent-level flow-matching policy counterpart while achieving significantly improved task completion speeds from synchronous inference. Coupled with the inherent advantages of autoregressive policies, such as faster convergence and better generalizability in instruction-following, these results confirm that autoregressive policies can remain a competitive policy type supporting real-time execution.

</details>


### [414] [GeoHAT: Geometry-Adaptive Hybrid Action Transformer for Mobile Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13394)
*Xiangyu Zhu,Renjun Wu,Luzhou Ge,Jinyan Liu,Xuesong Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces GeoHAT, a framework utilizing geometry-aware perception and task-specific action strategies to significantly improve whole-body mobile manipulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges in whole-body mobile manipulation caused by limited perception and ineffective action strategies, especially when incorporating noisy depth data.

Method: GeoHAT integrates a Fourier spatial encoder for 3D geometry mapping, gated fusion for reliable feature merging, and a hybrid decoder to separate arm and base actions, paired with temporal modeling for coordination.

Result: GeoHAT achieves a 79.3% success rate on the ManiSkill-HAB benchmark, outperforming the strongest baseline by 23.7%, with additional real-world improvements.

Conclusion: GeoHAT effectively enhances spatial understanding and action generation in whole-body manipulation tasks, combining efficient geometry processing and modular control mechanisms.

Abstract: Whole-body mobile manipulation requires coordinating mobile base and manipulator under shifting viewpoints, posing challenges in geometric perception and action generation. Current policies either rely on 2D features or sparse 3D representations that lack dense spatial structure, and typically encode arm and base within one action vector that ignores their distinct control demands. Moreover, existing dense fusion strategies risk corrupting pretrained representations under noisy depth while incurring heavy computational overhead. We present GeoHAT, an end-to-end diffusion-based framework built on a simple principle: geometry should be injected only where reliable and attended to only where needed. GeoHAT employs a lightweight Fourier spatial encoder that maps dense per-pixel 3D coordinates into geometric tokens without an additional 3D vision backbone. These tokens are then selectively injected into vision foundation model features through per-token gated fusion modulated by depth validity, preserving the semantic prior while enriching spatial understanding. For action generation, a Hybrid Whole-Body Action Decoder decomposes arm and base into distinct subspaces and lets each action modality attend to its task-relevant visual context through sparse cross-attention, while causal temporal modeling captures intra-timestep coordination and inter-timestep dependencies. Experiments on the ManiSkill-HAB simulation benchmark demonstrate that GeoHAT achieves a 79.3% mean success rate, surpassing the strongest baseline by 23.7%. Furthermore, real-world experiments on diverse tasks also confirm consistent improvements over all baselines.

</details>


### [415] [GIVE: Grounding Human Gestures in Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13435)
*Pengfei Liu,Gen Li,Junqiao Fan,Boyu Ma,Jindou Jia,Yang Xiao,Jianfei Yang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces GIVE, a novel approach to improving Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models by incorporating gesture understanding to enhance robotic manipulation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLA models overlook gestures crucial in human-robot interaction, causing issues in intent grounding and manipulation when language is ambiguous or vague.

Method: GIVE enhances pre-trained VLA models by introducing gesture understanding via two pathways: a visual pathway with hand skeleton overlays for object grounding, and a semantic pathway for high-level gesture descriptions, without modifying the model's architecture.

Result: In real-world experiments, GIVE achieves a 40% improvement in target object recognition and an 80% increase in task success rate, while generalizing well to new environments and participants.

Conclusion: GIVE effectively improves intent grounding and manipulation reliability in robotic systems by integrating visual and semantic gesture understanding, addressing key limitations of prior VLA models.

Abstract: Human communication is inherently multimodal, where language is often accompanied by non-verbal cues such as gestures to convey intentions. However, current Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models treat robotic manipulation as a pure text-driven task, overlooking the important role of gestures in Human-Robot Interaction (HRI). This often leads to inaccurate intent grounding and unreliable manipulation when language instructions are ambiguous or underspecified. To address this challenge, we propose GIVE (Gesture Intent via Visual-Semantic Enhancement), an effective approach that enhances pre-trained VLA models with human gesture understanding without architectural modifications. Specifically, GIVE incorporates gesture information through two complementary pathways: a visual pathway that overlays hand skeletons and fingertip rays onto robot observations for explicit object grounding, and a semantic pathway that generates high-level descriptions of human gestures and task instructions for robust intent grounding. By jointly leveraging visual and semantic guidance, GIVE enables VLA policies to better associate gestures with manipulation behaviors and adapt to dynamic interaction intents. In real-world HRI experiments, GIVE substantially outperforms the baseline, improving target object recognition accuracy by 40% and overall task success rate by 80%, while demonstrating strong robustness and generalization to unseen spatial layouts and diverse participants.

</details>


### [416] [NavWAM: A Navigation World Action Model for Goal-Conditioned Visual Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13494)
*Daichi Azuma,Taiki Miyanishi,Koya Sakamoto,Shuhei Kurita,Yaonan Zhu,Petr Khrapchenkov,Motoaki Kawanabe,Yusuke Iwasawa,Yutaka Matsuo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: NavWAM bridges the gap between navigation world models and direct robot control by integrating future prediction, action, and value targets into a diffusion-transformer policy for goal-conditioned visual navigation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address the limitations of navigation world models that require external planners for converting predicted futures into closed-loop robot control.

Method: NavWAM is a diffusion-transformer model that incorporates future observations, goal-progress evaluation, and action sequences into a shared latent space for learning closed-loop behavior. It employs simulation pretraining and real-robot adaptation to train the model.

Result: NavWAM outperforms planning-based world models and direct navigation policies in offline benchmarks and real-robot deployments, demonstrating improved visual foresight for robot control without requiring complex external action searches.

Conclusion: NavWAM effectively integrates visual foresight with closed-loop control for visual navigation, reducing the need for external planners and achieving superior performance in goal-directed tasks.

Abstract: Goal-conditioned visual navigation requires a robot to act under partial observability by anticipating how its motion will change the future egocentric view and whether that change brings it closer to the goal. Navigation world models provide such visual foresight, but they remain prediction modules that require an external planner to convert predicted futures into closed-loop control. We propose Navigation World Action Model (NavWAM), a diffusion-transformer policy that turns navigation world-model prediction into executable action by representing future observations, goal-progress values, and action chunks in a shared latent sequence. By learning future prediction jointly with the action and value targets that determine closed-loop behavior, NavWAM makes visual foresight directly usable for robot control. We build NavWAM through simulation pretraining and real-robot adaptation, and evaluate it on image-goal navigation against planning-based world models and a representative direct navigation policy. Across offline benchmarks and closed-loop real-robot deployment, NavWAM improves over planning-based world-model baselines in our evaluations while using the default policy mode without CEM-style action search. Project page: https://dachii-azm.github.io/navwam/

</details>


### [417] [SPARC: Reliable Spatial Annotations from Robot Demonstrations at Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13497)
*Nils Blank,Paul Mattes,Maximilian Xiling Li,Jakub Suliga,Thomas Roth,Moritz Reuss,Pankhuri Vanjani,Rudolf Lioutikov*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SPARC introduces a reliability-aware labeling framework for robot demonstrations, improving annotation accuracy while retaining more useful samples compared to traditional methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Facilitate scalable and reliable automated spatial annotations in robot demonstrations, addressing the trade-off between noisy labels and useful sample retention.

Method: The SPARC framework generates structured spatial annotations from robot demonstrations and provides reliability scores by leveraging spatio-temporal task structures. It also introduces the Interaction-Aware Bench (IA-Bench) to evaluate object localization accuracy.

Result: SPARC outperforms detection-only baselines in localization accuracy, retains three times more samples at high precision, and achieves state-of-the-art results on various benchmarks. It also improves policy performance in real-world cluttered settings.

Conclusion: SPARC enhances spatial annotation reliability, enabling better models and policies without manual intervention, thus benefiting robotics applications significantly.

Abstract: This work introduces Spatial Annotations from Robot Demonstrations with Reliability Calibration (SPARC), a risk-aware framework that automatically labels robot demonstrations with structured spatial annotations and assigns each annotation a reliability score. Structured spatial annotations, such as bounding boxes, object trajectories, and manipulation phase labels, benefit a broad range of robotics applications from training grounded robot policies and embodied foundation models to motion planning and hierarchical task composition. Existing automated pipelines generate such annotations at scale but provide no reliable quality signal: detector confidence is poorly calibrated for annotation correctness, forcing a choice between accepting noisy labels or discarding useful samples. In contrast to existing automated pipelines, SPARC leverages the spatio-temporal structure inherent to robot tasks to generate a reliability signal, reducing noisy labels and retaining more useful samples. We further introduce Interaction-Aware Bench (IA-Bench), a benchmark that measures model accuracy in grounding the locations of interacted objects in robot demonstrations. On 1.7k human-annotated demonstrations spanning diverse embodiments and scenarios, SPARC significantly outperforms detection-only baselines in localization accuracy while retaining three times more samples at high-precision operating points. Our experiments demonstrate that models finetuned on our annotations achieve state-of-the-art results on object-grounding and pointing benchmarks among similarly sized models, while remaining competitive on broader spatial-reasoning suites without manually verified or annotated training data. Furthermore, policies trained on SPARC-generated annotations outperform baselines in cluttered, visually ambiguous real-world scenes. Code, data, and models are available at intuitive-robots.github.io/sparc-labeling.

</details>


### [418] [MCR-Bionic Hand: Anatomical Structural Priors for Dexterous Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13601)
*Haosen Yang,Guowu Wei*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The study introduces the MCR-Bionic Hand, a musculoskeletal biomimetic robotic hand designed to replicate human hand dexterity by embedding physical architectures and structural intelligence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how a human hand’s physical structures contribute to dexterity and apply this knowledge to create a highly functional robotic hand.

Method: The authors developed a biomimetic hand mimicking human hand anatomy, including bones, tendons, ligaments, and muscles, and demonstrated its functionality using geometric mechanical models and contact-rich tasks.

Result: The MCR-Bionic Hand showcases wrist posture induced multi-joint pre-shaping and anatomical mechanisms for coupled finger motion, achieving fine post-contact modulation in tasks like coin rotation and pen transfer.

Conclusion: Anatomical biomimetics, imitating both the structure and function of human hands, helps transfer part of human hand control to robotic systems, improving effective grasp and manipulation.

Abstract: Dexterous robotic hands are usually formulated as high dimensional active control systems governed by degrees of freedom, actuation, and algorithms. Human hand dexterity, however, is partly encoded in the physical architecture of bones, ligaments, tendons, aponeuroses, and intrinsic muscles. This work describes that contribution as two linked forms of structural intelligence: structural prior generation, in which wrist to finger tenodesis, FDS/FDP routing, and the dorsal extensor hood transform low dimensional posture inputs into default grasp configurations and PIP to DIP coordination; and muscle mediated modulation, in which extrinsic muscles, lumbricals, and interossei regulate MCP posture, distal stability, fingertip force paths, and contact states around that default state.
  Based on this framework, MCR-Bionic Hand is developed as a 1:1 musculoskeletal biomimetic hand integrating a two row eight bone wrist, cross wrist tendons, anatomical flexor routing, volar plate and collateral ligament constraints, the dorsal extensor hood, and intrinsic muscle pathways within one body. Functional demonstrations and geometric mechanical models show that wrist posture induces multi joint pre shaping, the extensor hood maps PIP posture to a coupled DIP response, and intrinsic plus pathways modulate distal stability and fingertip action direction after grasp formation. Contact rich tasks, including coin rotation, pen transfer, dorsal coin flipping, and cube manipulation, show that MCR-Bionic links low dimensional state generation with fine post contact modulation. These results suggest that anatomical biomimetics is valuable not for visual similarity, but for identifying human hand structures that perform part of control.

</details>


### [419] [$\texttt{WEAVER}$, Better, Faster, Longer: An Effective World Model for Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13672)
*Arnav Kumar Jain,Yilin Wu,Jesse Farebrother,Gokul Swamy,Andrea Bajcsy*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces WEAVER, a world model for robotics that achieves state-of-the-art results by meeting fidelity, consistency, and efficiency requirements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to overcome challenges in robotic world modeling for tasks like policy evaluation, improvement, and planning using limited real-world interactions.

Method: The WEAVER architecture is a multi-view world model trained with flow-matching loss to predict future latents and reward values while addressing model design, memory, and prediction tasks.

Result: WEAVER achieves strong results in robotic manipulation tasks, policy evaluation (ρ=0.870), improvement (38% real-world success improvement), and test-time planning (14% success boost with 5-10× speedup).

Conclusion: WEAVER satisfies key requirements for successful world modeling, surpassing prior models, especially in dynamic and out-of-distribution scenarios.

Abstract: The potential impacts of world models (WMs, i.e., learned simulators) on robotics are far-reaching -- policy evaluation, policy improvement, and test-time planning -- all with limited real-world interaction. To unlock these downstream capabilities, a WM needs to jointly satisfy three desiderata: $\textit{(i)}$ fidelity (i.e., producing simulated trajectories that correlate with reality), $\textit{(ii)}$ consistency (i.e., producing simulated trajectories that are coherent over long horizons), and $\textit{(iii)}$ efficiency (i.e., producing simulated trajectories quickly). We propose $\texttt{WEAVER}$ (World Estimation Across Views for Embodied Reasoning): a WM architecture that simultaneously achieves all three desiderata, providing state-of-the-art results on robotic manipulation tasks. $\texttt{WEAVER}$ is a multi-view WM trained to predict future latents and reward values via a flow-matching loss. We distill the key design decisions across model architecture, memory, and prediction objectives required to unlock the kinds of long-horizon dynamic manipulation tasks that have confounded prior world modeling approaches. We apply $\texttt{WEAVER}$ in robotic hardware, demonstrating its effectiveness at policy evaluation ($ρ$=0.870 correlation with real-world success rate), policy improvement (real-world success rate improvement of $38\%$ on top of the $π_{0.5}$ robot foundation model), and test-time planning (real-world success rate improvement of $14\%$ with a $5-10\times$ speedup over prior WMs). $\texttt{WEAVER}$ also demonstrates better performance than prior WMs when evaluated on out-of-distribution scenarios. Code, models, and videos at: https://arnavkj1995.github.io/WEAVER/ .

</details>


### [420] [Improving Robotic Generalist Policies via Flow Reversal Steering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13675)
*Andy Tang,William Chen,Andrew Wagenmaker,Chelsea Finn,Sergey Levine*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Flow Reversal Steering (FRS) enhances generalist robot policies by mapping suboptimal human-guided actions to better-performing ones, achieving significant success improvements in diverse tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to enable generalist robotic policies to effectively solve challenging tasks by leveraging their behavioral capabilities, even when direct commands fail or are suboptimal.

Method: The authors propose FRS, a technique that reverses suboptimal actions through a flow policy to infer latent noise and maps such actions to optimal ones. It leverages coarse human or vision-language guidance for better control and enables policy improvement via reinforcement learning.

Result: FRS achieves significant improvements in simulated and real-world tasks, increasing task success rates (up to 95% in some cases) with minimal training time, and enhances RL for tasks it traditionally struggles with.

Conclusion: The proposed FRS method demonstrates that leveraging latent representations of suboptimal actions can substantially improve zero-shot control, real-world manipulation tasks, and RL performance, highlighting its utility in generalist policy advancements.

Abstract: Generalist policies can learn a wide range of skills from diverse robot datasets. In order to solve or improve on challenging news tasks, we need a way to infer and invoke the appropriate actions from the policy's rich behavioral prior, especially when directly commanding the policy fails. We focus on flow matching generalists and propose Flow Reversal Steering (FRS): a method that takes suboptimal but ``reasonable'' actions, finds their latent noises by passing them through the flow policy in reverse, and maps them to nearby generalist action modes. We evaluate FRS across many simulated and real-world manipulation settings. First, FRS can turn coarse semantic guidance from humans or vision-language models (VLMs) into corresponding good robot actions, improving zero-shot control. These gains can be distilled with behavioral cloning by training an auxiliary policy to output noises that the generalist maps to good actions -- showing up to 95% absolute task success rate boosts in under a minute of training. Finally, FRS enables policy improvement by bootstrapping reinforcement learning with semantic knowledge, improving on several tasks that standard RL fails to improve on.

</details>


### [421] [Mana: Dexterous Manipulation of Articulated Tools](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13677)
*Zhao-Heng Yin,Guanya Shi,Pieter Abbeel,C. Karen Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents Mana, a sim-to-real framework for dexterous manipulation of articulated tools via a coarse-to-fine pipeline using animation-inspired methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenges of dexterous manipulation of articulated tools, which involve coordinating internal degrees of freedom and managing contact-rich interactions.

Method: Mana uses a coarse-to-fine pipeline combining procedurally-generated grasp keyframes, motion planning, and reinforcement learning to create manipulation trajectories.

Result: Mana demonstrates zero-shot sim-to-real transfer for grasping and in-hand manipulation across four articulated tools with diverse properties.

Conclusion: Mana provides a scalable and efficient framework for articulated tool manipulation with minimal manual input, leveraging animation-inspired strategies for sim-to-real robotics applications.

Abstract: Articulated tool manipulation remains a major challenge in dexterous robotics due to the need to coordinate internal degrees of freedom and contact-rich interactions. While prior work has largely focused on rigid objects, articulated tool use remains underexplored because of its physical complexity and the difficulty of learning functional grasping and manipulation policies. We present Mana (Manipulation Animator), a general sim-to-real framework that reinterprets dexterous manipulation as an animation problem. Inspired by computer animation, Mana employs a coarse-to-fine pipeline that transforms procedurally-generated grasp keyframes into manipulation trajectories through motion planning and reinforcement learning. The data generation process is largely automatic, requiring only a few mouse clicks to specify functional affordances (<1 minute per tool). Across four articulated tools spanning different scales and joint types, Mana achieves zero-shot sim-to-real transfer for both grasping and in-hand manipulation, demonstrating a scalable approach to dexterous articulated tool use.

</details>


<div id='cs.SE'></div>

# cs.SE [[Back]](#toc)

### [422] [Characterizing Tests in IoT Software: Practices, Challenges and Opportunities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12592)
*Rufeng Chen,Hengcheng Zhu,Wuqi Zhang,Zixu Zhou,Lili Wei*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The study investigates testing practices in open-source IoT software, identifying challenges and proposing mock objects as a solution for improving test effectiveness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The rapid expansion of IoT and the critical nature of its applications necessitate understanding and improving software testing to prevent severe consequences from bugs.

Method: The authors conducted an empirical study evaluating test case effectiveness, challenges in testing IoT software, and mock object usage in open-source IoT projects.

Result: They found that while IoT software has a significant number of tests, their effectiveness is limited due to challenges like complex external dependencies. Mock objects are identified as a useful yet underutilized solution.

Conclusion: The study highlights the importance of enhanced testing frameworks and techniques, like mocking, to improve the quality and reliability of IoT software.

Abstract: The Internet of Things (IoT) is experiencing rapid growth. Smart devices are emerging in smart homes and industrial applications, performing mission-critical tasks. Bugs in IoT software can lead to severe consequences. For example, a buggy smart lock can allow unauthorized access to a private property. Testing is a primary practice to expose software bugs and ensure software quality. However, little is known about how IoT software is tested. To bridge this gap, we conducted the first empirical study on test cases in open-source IoT software. Specifically, we evaluated the effectiveness of test cases in IoT software, explored the challenges inherent in testing IoT software, and analyzed the usage of mock objects. Our results indicate that while IoT software often contains a considerable number of tests, their effectiveness remains limited. We identified the primary challenges in testing IoT software as managing complex interactions with various external dependencies, such as other network-reliant IoT components, file systems, operating systems, and databases. We also observed that the use of mock objects in IoT software closely aligns with our identified testing challenges. This alignment demonstrates the potential of mocking as a solution to enhance test coverage and address the complexities of IoT software testing.

</details>


### [423] [HybridCodeAuthorship: A Benchmark Dataset for Line-Level Code Authorship Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12620)
*Luke Patterson,Li Wang,Adam Faulkner*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: This paper introduces HybridCodeAuthorship, a benchmark dataset for detecting AI-generated code from interleaved human-AI code bases, and evaluates two algorithms on its tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The increasing use of AI code assistants creates hybrid codebases with both AI- and human-authored code. Fine-grained detection of AI-generated code is needed for risk management and productivity assessment.

Method: The authors constructed a Python code benchmark using CodeSearchNet, simulating real-world interleaved codebases, and evaluated two existing AI-generated code detection algorithms on their performance across line- and chunk-level tasks.

Result: Experimental results show that detecting AI-generated code on this benchmark is challenging, with the best-performing algorithm achieving F1 scores of 0.48 for chunk-level and 0.56 for line-level detection.

Conclusion: HybridCodeAuthorship serves as a valuable and realistic benchmark for assessing the nuanced task of AI-generated code detection, revealing room for improvement in detection algorithms.

Abstract: Thanks to the rapid adoption of AI code assistants powered by large language models (LLMs), industry codebases are, increasingly, a hybrid of AI- and human-authored code. For risk management and productivity analysis purposes, it is crucial to enable fine-grained location detection of AI-generated code. To develop algorithms for this task, quality benchmarks are needed to assess performance. However, existing benchmarks tend to comprise academic, LeetCode-style problems and presume a code snippet is either completely human-authored or completely AI-authored, which is not reflective of the diverse intents and styles of industry codebases utilizing AI code assistants. To fill these gaps, we introduce HybridCodeAuthorship, a novel benchmark of Python code files with interleaved human- and AI-authored lines of code to simulate authentic utilization of AI code assistants. In this paper, we first present our dataset construction pipeline, which leverages CodeSearchNet, a massive collection of links to open sourced repositories on GitHub. We then benchmark the performance of two state-of-the-art AI-generated code detection algorithms at both the line- and chunk-level. Experimental results demonstrate that HybridCodeAuthorship is a challenging benchmark with a top-scoring algorithm, AIGCode Detector, obtaining a highest F1 score of 0.48 and 0.56 on chunk-level and line-level code detection tasks, respectively.

</details>


### [424] [Beyond Problem Solving: UOJ-Bench for Evaluating Code Generation, Hacking, and Repair in Competitive Programming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12864)
*Tingqiang Xu,Hangrui Zhou,Tianle Cai,Alex Gu,Kaifeng Lyu*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The study introduces UOJ-Bench, a benchmark to assess the problem-solving and error-identifying ability of Large Language Models (LLMs) in competitive programming. Findings indicate current LLMs struggle with error identification, but test-time scaling can improve results albeit with high computational costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore the under-researched potential of LLMs in supporting human learning and error-identification in competitive programming scenarios.

Method: UOJ-Bench benchmark was created using real-world submissions from Universal Online Judge (UOJ), consisting of tasks for code generation, hacking, and repair. Evaluations were done using UOJ's judging system.

Result: LLMs struggled in identifying errors (failing >50%), though test-time scaling improved success rates above 90%. However, scalability is limited due to computational costs.

Conclusion: While LLMs face challenges in large-scale practical deployment, frontier models exhibit potential by catching errors in full-score submissions, complementing traditional judging systems.

Abstract: Despite strong performance in competitive programming, the role of Large Language Models (LLMs) in supporting human learning in the same setting remains largely unexplored. In this work, we introduce UOJ-Bench, a benchmark designed to evaluate not only the problem-solving ability of LLMs, but also their ability to identify errors in human-written code -- a crucial educational activity traditionally supported by running test cases over online judge systems. UOJ-Bench consists of three distinct tasks: code generation, code hacking, and code repair, all constructed from real-world code submissions on the Universal Online Judge (UOJ) and evaluated through UOJ's native judging infrastructure. Our results show that under one-shot evaluation, even the strongest models fail to identify errors in more than 50% of a set of submissions that have been found to be incorrect by UOJ users. While test-time scaling improves success rates to above 90%, the substantial computational costs incurred from model inference limit its practicality for large-scale deployment. Despite these limitations, we find that the best-performing models under test-time scaling can uncover errors in over 5% of full-score submissions across roughly 30 problems, suggesting that frontier LLMs can already provide complementary signals beyond standard judging systems.

</details>


### [425] [The Rise of AI-Native Software Engineering: Implications for Practice, Education, and the Future Workforce](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12986)
*Mamdouh Alenezi*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: This paper reviews the impact of Generative AI, LLMs, and Agentic AI on software engineering (SE) and offers frameworks, models, and strategies for education and workforce adaptation through analysis of 48 research publications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To analyze and synthesize the disruptive influence of Generative AI, LLMs, and Agentic AI on software engineering practices, education, and workforce, and to guide adaptation strategies.

Method: A systematic review of 48 peer-reviewed publications (2016-2026) across domains like software engineering, machine learning, and computing education. Four research workflow agents were used for discovery, analysis, and synthesizing findings into actionable outcomes.

Result: The paper provides a conceptual framework for AI-native SE, a nine-dimension competency model, a phased university curriculum, workforce transformation strategies, and an eleven-gap research agenda. It notes significant growth in LLM-for-SE studies and context dependency in productivity effects.

Conclusion: The SE field must focus on developing competencies like intent, collaboration, and verification, emphasizing educational and workforce preparation that goes beyond code production, to holistically embrace challenges of the AI-native era.

Abstract: Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI), Large Language Models (LLMs), and emerging Agentic AI constitute the most disruptive transformation in the history of software engineering (SE), reshaping development processes, required competencies, professional roles, and the educational outcomes that universities must deliver. This paper presents a systematic review of 48 verified, influential peer-reviewed publications (2016--2026) drawn from leading venues in software engineering, machine learning, computing education, human--AI collaboration, and software productivity. Studies were discovered, screened, and analyzed through a four-agent research workflow (Literature Discovery, Scientometric Analysis, Curriculum Transformation, and Workforce Impact) and were verified against primary sources. We synthesize the evidence along nine themes and three trajectories -- practice, education, and workforce -- and report a scientometric inflection in which annual LLM-for-SE output grew roughly five-fold after late 2022. From this synthesis we contribute: (i) a conceptual framework for AI-native software engineering organized around \emph{intent}, \emph{collaboration}, and \emph{verification}; (ii) a nine-dimension competency model spanning specification, critical evaluation, agent orchestration, and metacognition; (iii) a four-phase university curriculum roadmap with AI-resilient assessment; (iv) faculty-development and workforce-transformation strategies; and (v) a prioritized agenda of eleven research gaps. The evidence base is internally contradictory on the magnitude and direction of productivity effects, underscoring that benefits are strongly context-dependent and that educating engineers for judgment, verification, and orchestration -- rather than code production alone -- is the central challenge of the AI-native era.

</details>


### [426] [The End of Code Review: Coding Agents Supersede Human Inspection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13175)
*Martin Monperrus*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper argues that the traditional practice of human code review is becoming unnecessary due to the capabilities of large language model (LLM)-based autonomous coding agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to evaluate the viability of coding agents in replacing human code review, reducing costs and improving throughput while maintaining software quality.

Method: The paper bases its argument on two claims: (1) coding agents can achieve all the stated goals of code review more efficiently, and (2) the human-centric model of mandatory review is inadequate and unsustainable in AI-driven workflows.

Result: It highlights that human-centric code review does not scale well with the increased capabilities and output of coding agents.

Conclusion: Coding agents have reached a level of proficiency where traditional human code review is no longer essential in the software development quality pipeline.

Abstract: Code review has been the primary quality gate in software development since Fagan formalised code inspection in 1976. For five decades, having a human examine and comment on a colleague's changes before merge has been a cornerstone practice at organisations of every size. Coding agents are large language model (LLM)-based autonomous systems capable of reading, writing, testing, and repairing software. We argue that coding agents have crossed a threshold of capability at which traditional human code review is no longer a necessary component of a software quality pipeline. Our argument rests on two claims: every stated goal of code review can be served by agents at lower cost and higher throughput; the naive integration in which agents write code and humans remain the mandatory reviewers is a dead end because it neither provides meaningful assurance nor scales with AI-assisted throughput.

</details>


### [427] [ComAct: Reframing Professional Software Manipulation via COM-as-Action Paradigm](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13239)
*Jiaxin Ai,Tao Hu,Xuemeng Yang,Shu Zou,Hairong Zhang,Daocheng Fu,Yu Yang,Hongbin Zhou,Nianchen Deng,Pinlong Cai,Zhongyuan Wang,Botian Shi,Kaipeng Zhang,Licheng Wen*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper presents COM-as-Action, a new paradigm leveraging Component Object Model for reliable professional software interaction, outperforming GUI-based methods in CAD tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address limitations in GUI-based and API-based approaches for professional software manipulation, providing a unified and robust solution for interaction.

Method: Proposes COM-as-Action using Component Object Model for program synthesis, introduces ComCADBench for benchmarking, and develops ComActor for robust CAD software task execution.

Result: COM-based execution outperforms GUI-based methods. ComActor achieves state-of-the-art performance on ComCADBench with strong resilience in complex tasks.

Conclusion: The COM-as-Action paradigm is a significant advancement, showing promise in professional software interaction and outperforming existing methods in accuracy and robustness.

Abstract: Existing computer-use agents remain fundamentally limited in professional software manipulation: GUI-based agents suffer from fragile visual grounding and long-horizon error accumulation, while API-basedapproaches struggle with heterogeneous protocols and inaccessible commercial interfaces. In this work,we identify the Component Object Model (COM) as a unified executable abstraction, proposing COM-as-Action: a new paradigm that reframes professional software interaction as deterministic program synthesisrather than sequential visual control. To validate this paradigm in the most demanding environments, weintroduce ComCADBench, the first benchmark for agents operating real industrial CAD software. Ourexperiments reveal a substantial paradigm gap: frontier proprietary models achieve near-zero successunder GUI-based interaction, whereas COM-based execution yields substantial immediate gains. Tobridge the remaining gap between syntactic correctness and geometric accuracy, we develop ComActor, aself-correcting agent trained through a progressive three-stage framework, alongside ComForge, a scalableplatform for large-scale training in Windows containers. Extensive experiments show that ComActorachieves state-of-the-art performance on ComCADBench, with strong resilience in long-horizon taskswhere baselines collapse, and generalizes to external CAD benchmark.

</details>


### [428] [Mining Architectural Quality Under Agentic AI Adoption: A Causal Study of Java Repositories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13298)
*Oliver Aleksander Larsen,Mahyar T. Moghaddam*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: This paper investigates the impact of AI coding tool adoption on architectural smell density (ASD) in software projects using causal inference techniques, finding no architectural improvement but a reduction in smell density due to codebase growth.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether the adoption of AI coding tools affects software architecture quality, as prior work mostly analyzed code-level outcomes without evidence on architectural implications.

Method: The study analyzed 151 Java repositories, comparing 74 with AI adoption to 77 controls, using difference-in-differences design and advanced imputation estimators to measure changes in ASD over time.

Result: Adoption of AI coding tools did not change total architectural smell counts significantly but led to a 6.7% decline in ASD due to codebase expansion (+12.8%). Robustness checks support these findings.

Conclusion: The observed decline in ASD originates from a denominator effect (codebase growth) rather than actual architectural improvement, stressing the need for careful metric decomposition in causal studies.

Abstract: AI coding tools are now used by a majority of developers, and agentic use of these tools has popularized the practice colloquially called "vibe coding". Yet causal evidence on their effect on software architecture is scarce. Prior causal work has measured code-level outcomes (complexity, static analysis warnings); whether such degradation propagates to architecture-level outcomes remains unknown. We mine 151 open-source Java repositories, 74 with detectable agentic AI adoption (identified via configuration files and Co-Authored-By commit trailers) and 77 propensity-matched controls, across a 13-month per-repository window yielding 1,811 monthly Arcan snapshots. We estimate the causal effect of adoption on architectural smell density (ASD) with a staggered difference-in-differences design and the Borusyak imputation estimator, applying a causal design recently used for code-level metrics to the architecture level. Total smell counts are essentially unchanged (+1.1%, p = 0.82) while lines of code grow +12.8% (p = 0.003); the resulting 6.7% ASD decline (p = 0.004) is therefore a denominator effect rather than an architectural improvement. Per-type estimates and robustness checks (wild cluster bootstrap, Lee bounds, stale-observation sensitivity) corroborate the pattern; pre-trends are flat (Wald p = 0.90), consistent with parallel trends. Density-normalized outcomes can mislead when treatment affects system size: raw counts and explicit decomposition are required for causal mining studies of AI tool adoption. The complete replication package, including the curated 151-repository monthly panel, is publicly available.

</details>


### [429] [Toward Instructions-as-Code: Understanding the Impact of Instruction Files on Agentic Pull Requests](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13449)
*Ali Arabat,Mohammed Sayagh*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper investigates how instruction files for AI agents, like GitHub Copilot, impact the quality and success of their pull requests. Results show mixed outcomes: some projects improve, while others decline. Detailed and structured instruction files correlate with better performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how developer-created instruction files affect the performance of AI agents in generating successful pull requests, and to highlight the need for research in making instruction creation a formalized software engineering practice.

Method: The authors analyzed 15,549 pull requests from 148 projects in the AIDev dataset, comparing project outcomes before and after instruction files were created. They evaluated success based on metrics like merge rate, task complexity, and effort required to merge.

Result: Results indicate mixed effectiveness of instruction files: 27.7% of projects improved merge rates, while 26.35% saw declines. Projects with longer, structured instruction files tended to perform better.

Conclusion: Instruction files don't guarantee improvement, but well-structured and detailed files can enhance AI-agent performance. The study calls for further research on developing instruction files as a dedicated software engineering task.

Abstract: AI-agents (e.g., GitHub Copilot) collaborate as teammates in different software engineering tasks, including code generation proposed through pull requests (Agentic-PRs). For better agent efficiency, developers create instruction files that guide the AI-agents, including how to navigate the project, locate the right components, run tests, respect best practices, and more. In this paper, we investigate the relationship between the creation of these instructions and the performance of AI-agents in creating better pull requests, which have a higher chance of success (i.e., the merge rate), address more complex tasks (e.g., code churn), and require less effort to be merged (e.g., time to merge). To this end, we analyze 15,549 agentic PRs from 148 projects in the AIDev dataset. Using the three dimensions, we compare each project before and after the creation of the instruction files. We find that specifying instructions for AI-agents does not necessarily lead to better results. With the instruction files, 27.7\% of the projects increased their merge rate by at least 20\%, while 26.35\% decreased it. The same observation is seen with the amount of changes (e.g., code churn, number of modified files) and with the efforts to merge an agentic PR (e.g., merge time and number of comments). From a first exploration, we find that projects that managed to increase their merge rate have substantially longer instruction files, which are also well structured into a higher number of sections and sub-sections. Our results motivate the need for research to assist practitioners in framing the development of instruction files as a software engineering activity (aka, \textbf{Instructions-as-Code}).

</details>


### [430] [Understanding the Rejection of Fixes Generated by Agentic Pull Requests -- Insights from the AIDev Dataset](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13468)
*Mahmoud Abujadallah,Ali Arabat,Mohammed Sayagh*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper analyzes AI coding agents' failure modes in generating code fixes for software projects, discovering high rejection rates and identifying reasons behind them to improve efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand why AI-generated code fixes often fail, aiming to better integrate AI coding agents into development processes and reduce wasted resources.

Method: Qualitative study of 306 non-merged pull requests created by AI agents, followed by quantitative analysis of rejection reasons.

Result: Identified 14 reasons for rejecting AI-code fixes, grouped into categories like incorrect implementation, CI pipeline failures, inability to perform, and low priority fixes.

Conclusion: Highlights the need to better guide AI agents on approach constraints, validation processes, and task prioritization to prevent wasted human and computational resources.

Abstract: AI coding agents are increasingly used to generate pull requests (PRs) that propose code fixes in software projects. From a first exploration of the AIDev dataset, we find that 46.41\% of the fixes proposed by the agents Copilot, Devin, Cursor, and Claude are rejected. This represents a significant amount of wasted resources that require human reviews, verifications, and running tests and validations for fixes that are merely discarded. Our goal in this paper is to understand the failure modes of AI-agents, an understanding that is crucial for better integrating AI-agents as efficient teammates. In this paper, we conduct a qualitative study on a representative sample of 306 non-merged pull requests created or co-authored by the agents mentioned earlier, followed by a quantitative analysis of the reasons for rejection. Our qualitative findings identify 14 reasons divided into four high-level categories for rejecting AI-agent fixes. We observe that developers can reject fixes due to fixes whose implementation is incorrect (e.g., incomplete, wrong approach), fixes that do not pass the continuous integration (CI) pipelines and fail tests, fixes for which the agent is unable to perform the implementation (e.g., no code generated, sessions lost), and fixes whose priority is low. Our results shed light on the importance of better guiding the model at these levels: (1) proposing hints about the approach to follow for fixing an issue, (2) outlining constraints or limitations regarding the approaches that should not be taken, and (3) instructing the agent on how to validate the implementation through CI pipelines and without introducing a breaking change. Our results suggest the need for good prioritization of tasks so that generated fixes do not lead to wasted human review efforts or wasted agent resources (e.g., tokens, compute, or allowed number of requests).

</details>


<div id='q-bio.NC'></div>

# q-bio.NC [[Back]](#toc)

### [431] [A quantum-like benchmark for context-sensitive associative memory with adaptive plasticity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12449)
*Yashine H. Goolam Hossen,Lea Gassab,Travis J. A. Craddock*

Main category: q-bio.NC

TL;DR: The paper evaluates associative-memory models under a benchmark testing recall dynamics and adaptive plasticity, showing advantages in multi-objective profiling for quantum-like models under certain conditions, without a universal quantum-like advantage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how memory systems balance plasticity and stability, and to distinguish genuine recall dynamics from structural assistance using a controlled memory-recall benchmark.

Method: The paper employs an order-sensitive adaptive-plasticity benchmark comparing quantum-like associative-memory models with no-phase and Markov-rate controls under identical conditions, focusing on weak structural support.

Result: Adaptive plasticity, particularly homeostatic stabilization, drives recall improvements. While Markov-rate models achieve higher raw recall, quantum-like models preserve better temporal order and stage organization.

Conclusion: Model classes should be evaluated using multi-objective profiles, combining recall levels, temporal organization, and context sensitivity, rather than relying solely on recall scores.

Abstract: Learning and memory require a balance between plasticity and stability: synaptic connections must encode new information without collapsing, saturating, or erasing previously useful structure. Associative-memory models can appear to learn successfully when fixed background connectivity already carries part of the task, making it difficult to distinguish genuine recall dynamics from structural assistance. We test this issue using an order-sensitive adaptive-plasticity benchmark for staged associative recall. The benchmark compares a quantum-like associative-memory model with matched real-valued no-phase and Markov-rate controls under the same task schedule, perturbation profiles, weak-support conditions, and plasticity settings. Here, "quantum-like" refers to the modeling formalism, not to a biological claim about quantum computation. We first screen weak structural support and then fix a conservative operating point for factorial comparisons across model families and plasticity mechanisms. The useful weak-support regime is narrow and non-monotonic. Weak structure alone does not rescue recall in the no-plasticity ablation, whereas most useful recall gains arise from adaptive plasticity, especially homeostatic stabilization. The Markov-rate control often achieves stronger raw recall, but the quantum-like model more consistently preserves order sensitivity and stage-dependent organization. These results do not support a universal quantum-like advantage. Instead, they show that model classes are better distinguished by a multi-objective profile combining recall, temporal organization, and context sensitivity than by any single recall score. The benchmark therefore provides a controlled framework for studying context-sensitive memory dynamics under weak support, regulated plasticity, and matched classical comparison.

</details>


### [432] [Multifractal human signals at the edge of life reveal a heart-brain anti-correlation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12600)
*Yago Emanoel Ramos,Maria Eloá do Ó,Henrique Ferraz de Arruda,Mauro Copelli,G. Camelo-Neto,Pedro V. Carelli*

Main category: q-bio.NC

TL;DR: The study analyzes terminal stage neurophysiological function, revealing divergent patterns in brain and heart activity using multifractal analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the dynamics of human neurophysiological decline and its systemic effects as death approaches, focusing on the decoupling of brain and cardiac activity.

Method: The authors applied Multifractal Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (MF-DFA) to EEG and ECG time series of terminally ill patients, comparing their multifractal spectrum widths.

Result: Neural activity shows a collapse toward reduced complexity, while cardiac signals exhibit anomalous broadening, revealing a negative correlation between their spectral widths.

Conclusion: The study identifies a body-to-brain breakdown characterized by peripheral dysfunction overtaking central regulatory systems, suggesting cross-system disintegration as a marker of the dying process.

Abstract: This study investigates the terminal breakdown of human neurophysiological function through the lens of non-linear dynamics by analyzing the multifractal spectrum. Using Multifractal Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (MF-DFA), we quantify the temporal evolution of complexity in synchronized electroencephalogram (EEG) and electrocardiogram (ECG) time series from patients in the terminal stage. Our results reveal a marked divergence in multifractal spectrum width: while neural activity exhibits a collapse of multifractality toward a more constrained state, cardiac signals undergo anomalous spectral broadening, indicating increased non-linear fluctuations and dynamical instability. A negative correlation between these spectral widths suggests effective functional decoupling and the emergence of anti-correlated dynamics between neural and cardiac systems. Rather than reflecting a uniform physiological decline, this divergence is consistent with a body-to-brain breakdown in which peripheral dysfunction progressively overwhelms central regulatory processes. In a broader context, the observed opposing trends resemble patterns reported in other body-driven adaptive processes, suggesting that inverse dynamics across coupled systems may emerge when constraints originate from peripheral rather than central mechanisms. Ultimately, the dying process appears to represent an extreme form of cross-system disintegration, marked by the collapse of the hierarchical coordination that normally sustains integrated physiological function.

</details>


### [433] [Phase model analysis of the effect of M-current on neural synchrony in hippocampal networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12684)
*Megha Manoj,Sue Ann Campbell*

Main category: q-bio.NC

TL;DR: The paper explores how acetylcholine (ACh) levels influence neural assemblies in the hippocampus, crucial for memory formation, through its effect on neuron synchrony.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the bidirectional role of acetylcholine (ACh) in neural assembly formation and its implications for memory encoding and consolidation.

Method: A network model of pyramidal neurons with modulation of an M-current is used to study synchronization. Analytical reduction to a one-dimensional phase model predicts cluster solutions for different coupling architectures.

Result: Low ACh levels lead to full synchronization of neurons, while high ACh levels produce desynchronization with stable symmetric cluster solutions that signify distinct neural assemblies.

Conclusion: The findings suggest that ACh levels modulate neural assembly dynamics, with low ACh supporting memory consolidation and high ACh enabling memory encoding.

Abstract: Neural assemblies, transiently coordinated groups of neurons, observed in the hippocampus are thought to underlie the formation of episodic memories. Acetylcholine (ACh), a neuromodulator, that is received by the hippocampus, plays a critical role in memory and learning. A well supported hypothesis suggests that high levels of ACh during active exploration and rapid eye movement (REM) sleep promote memory encoding, while low levels during quiet waking and slow-wave sleep (SWS) support memory consolidation. We study this bidirectional role of ACh in neural assembly formation through its effect on the synchrony among neurons. We consider a network model of pyramidal neurons, each equipped with a slow, voltage-dependent, non-inactivating potassium current (M-current), which is downregulated in the presence of ACh. Neural assemblies are represented as cluster solutions to this system. Using a one-dimensional phase model reduction of a pair of weakly coupled pyramidal neurons under different levels of the M-current, we predict the symmetric cluster solutions that may emerge in larger networks equipped with all-to-all globally homogeneous, symmetric distance-dependent and nearest-neighbours coupling architectures. We find that under low ACh conditions, the network can fully synchronize, whereas high levels can desynchronize the network into multiple stable symmetric cluster solutions representing distinct neural assemblies.

</details>


### [434] [Deep Sleep Classification via EEG Signal Criticality: A Passive BCI Approach for Sleep-Improvement Neurofeedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13017)
*Stanisław Narębski,Tomasz Komendziński,Tomasz M. Rutkowski*

Main category: q-bio.NC

TL;DR: This paper investigates automated sleep staging using criticality features from EEG data to identify deep sleep, testing six classifiers with Naive Bayes achieving the best accuracy for neurofeedback applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop advanced passive Brain-Computer Interfaces (pBCIs) capable of identifying deep sleep (N3) states for state-dependent neurofeedback, enabling interventions like enhancing cognitive recovery.

Method: EEG data from 347,232 epochs of 290 older women were analyzed using DFA-derived features, UMAP for visualization, and six classifiers evaluated through 10-fold cross-validation to identify optimal performance.

Result: Naive Bayes achieved the highest balanced accuracy (87.17%) for the classification, outperforming other models including a fully connected deep neural network and random forest.

Conclusion: Probabilistic decoding of EEG criticality with Naive Bayes is a promising, accurate approach for developing neurofeedback mechanisms in pBCIs, especially for deep sleep detection.

Abstract: Automated sleep staging is a fundamental application of passive Brain-Computer Interfaces (pBCI), decoding spontaneous neural states to enable closed-loop interventions independent of user intent. This study evaluates criticality features derived from Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (DFA) for the specific identification of deep sleep (N3).
  We analyzed $347,232$ EEG epochs from $290$ older women using UMAP manifold learning to visualize state transitions. Subsequently, six classifiers were benchmarked via 10-fold cross-validation, using balanced accuracy to determine the optimal "state-sensing" engine for neurofeedback.Naive Bayes achieved the highest mean balanced accuracy ($87.17\% \pm 0.24\%$), significantly outperforming a fully connected deep neural network (FNN: $81.58\%$) and Random Forest ($80.97\%$). Linear models (LDA: $57.21\%$; SVM: $51.01\%$) performed poorly, indicating that DFA-derived criticality features reside on a distinct, non-linear manifold.
  Probabilistic decoding of EEG criticality provides a high-accuracy sensing mechanism for pBCIs. This robust classification pipeline supports the development of state-dependent neurofeedback, such as targeted auditory stimulation, to enhance cognitive recovery.

</details>


### [435] [Including the Cost of Irreducible Uncertainty in the Policy Compression Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13132)
*Álvaro Garrido-Pérez,Pieter Simoens,Amrapali Pednekar,Yara Khaluf*

Main category: q-bio.NC

TL;DR: The paper extends the policy compression framework by incorporating conditional entropy as part of cognitive cost, improving its ability to model biases in human decision-making.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to refine existing models of human decision-making by addressing the gap in how cognitive limitations, specifically conditional entropy, influence biases and reaction times.

Method: The researchers redefine cognitive cost to include policy complexity plus a weighted conditional-entropy term, introducing a new parameter, η, allowing policies to adapt more granularly.

Result: The extended framework suggests that traditional models may underestimate the cognitive cost of action selection and enables a more precise description of decision-making biases.

Conclusion: The extended model improves upon the existing framework but introduces additional challenges in fitting the model to human data, laying the groundwork for future research.

Abstract: AI decision-support systems can benefit from anticipating biases in human decision-making. Many such biases may arise from human cognitive limitations. The policy compression framework models decision-making as a trade-off between reward maximization and the cognitive cost of encoding state-dependent action policies, formalized as the mutual information between states and actions (policy complexity). We argue that this account is incomplete because it treats conditional entropy--the irreducible uncertainty about which action should be selected given a state--as costless, even though empirical evidence suggests that it modulates reaction times. We therefore extend the framework by defining cognitive cost as the sum of policy complexity and a weighted conditional-entropy term, governed by a new parameter, $η$. The resulting optimal policy retains the standard exponential form but becomes sharper as $η$ increases, allowing policy precision to vary more independently of reward sensitivity. This modification implies that the standard policy compression framework may underestimate the cognitive cost of action selection, and it has the potential to better account for biases in human decision-making. At the same time, it introduces additional complexity for fitting the model to human data, which future work will need to address.

</details>


<div id='stat.ML'></div>

# stat.ML [[Back]](#toc)

### [436] [Identifiability Without Gaussianity: Symbolic World Models and Near-Infinite Temporal Consistency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12471)
*Seth Dobrin,Łukasz Chmiel*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper introduces a new architecture called Physics-Grounded Symbolic Architecture (PGSA) that overcomes limitations of Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPAs) for identifying true latent variables and ensuring temporal consistency in dynamic systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to overcome a fundamental limitation of JEPAs, which fail to achieve linear identifiability and temporal consistency in non-Gaussian systems, creating a gap in accurately modeling physical world dynamics.

Method: The authors propose the PGSA, analyze its properties mathematically, and provide formal proofs for its ability to achieve exact linear identifiability and near-infinite temporal consistency under all physical regimes.

Result: The PGSA achieves exact linear identifiability regardless of latent variable distributions, maintains temporal consistency for an unbounded number of steps under numerical precision, and outperforms traditional statistical world models in non-Gaussian systems.

Conclusion: Symbolic grounding in the causal generator of world dynamics, as implemented in PGSA, is the sufficient and only condition for achieving near-infinite temporal consistency in non-Gaussian regimes.
