Abstract: Klindt, LeCun, and Balestriero (arXiv:2605.26379) proved that Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPAs) achieve linear identifiability, the linear recovery of the world's true latent variables, if and only if the world's latent dynamics follow a Gaussian, stationary process. This Gaussian boundary implies a fundamental limit on temporal consistency: for any non-Gaussian physical system, the representation error of a statistical World Model grows monotonically with time. We prove that this limit is an artifact of the statistical alignment mechanism, not a property of World Models in general. We introduce the Physics-Grounded Symbolic Architecture (PGSA) and prove three results: (1) a PGSA achieves exact linear identifiability for all physical regimes, regardless of the latent distribution; (2) the per-step error of a PGSA is bounded by numerical precision alone; and (3) as a direct consequence, a PGSA maintains temporal consistency for an unbounded number of transitions, a property we term near-infinite temporal consistency. We further prove that statistical World Models cannot achieve this property for any non-Gaussian system, regardless of model capacity or the volume of training data. The algebraic cores of four of the theorems are formalized in Lean 4 with Mathlib4 v4.31.0 (zero sorry placeholders); the Klindt et al. converse is taken as an external premise. The contrast establishes that symbolic grounding in the causal generator of the world's dynamics is the sufficient condition and, in non-Gaussian regimes, the only condition for near-infinite temporal consistency.

</details>


### [437] [Epistemic Uncertainty Is Not the Reducible Kind](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12646)
*Robin Young*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper critiques the standard definitions and measures for epistemic uncertainty, demonstrating their inconsistencies, and introduces a revised framework with three distinct categories of uncertainty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address inconsistencies in the standard definition and measurement of epistemic uncertainty, particularly the issue of reducibility and its misalignment with practical outcomes in uncertainty estimation.

Method: Theoretical analysis, mathematical proofs, explicit constructions, and empirical experiments (including finite-sample falsification tests and seed-swept experiments) are used to analyze and test the inconsistencies in current measures and definitions of epistemic uncertainty.

Result: The study establishes that epistemic uncertainty as traditionally defined contains irreducible components, demonstrates misalignment between ensemble disagreement and epistemic estimates, and offers a new breakdown of uncertainty into three categories: aleatoric, sample-reducible epistemic, and mechanism-reducible epistemic.

Conclusion: This work identifies fundamental flaws in the current framework for epistemic uncertainty and provides a new theoretical identity and classification system, revealing implications for how we model and interpret uncertainty in predictive systems.

Abstract: The standard taxonomy of predictive uncertainty defines epistemic uncertainty as the part removable by collecting more data, while the standard measure identifies it with a mutual-information term. We prove the definition and the measure are extensionally inconsistent. On an explicit construction, the measure assigns all uncertainty to the epistemic class, yet no quantity of training data reduces it. Reducibility is instead a property of the pair (uncertainty, acquisition class), and the dichotomy resolves into three parts: aleatoric, sample-reducible epistemic, and mechanism-reducible epistemic uncertainty. An exact identity for the value of an observation shows that in-distribution data never reduces mechanism-irreducible uncertainty and generically increases it. Ensemble disagreement, the deployed epistemic estimate, tracks the training procedure rather than the epistemic term. It collapses to zero beneath a positive truth under consistent training, and equals hyperparameter-scaled initialization noise under interpolation. A finite-sample falsification test and seed-swept experiments confirm the theory.

</details>


### [438] [Prediction-Powered Causal Inference by Automatic Debiased Machine Learning and Semi-Supervised Riesz Regression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12892)
*Masahiro Kato*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper investigates a semi-supervised method for more efficient estimation of causal and structural parameters using both labeled and unlabeled data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce the asymptotic variance in estimating causal and structural parameters, leveraging additional unlabeled data alongside labeled data.

Method: The authors derive an efficient influence function and efficiency bounds, and integrate these into a debiased machine learning framework to create new estimators, namely EE-DML-PPCI and TMLE-DML-PPCI.

Result: The proposed methods achieve smaller asymptotic variances that match the efficiency bounds derived, outperforming labeled-only estimation approaches.

Conclusion: By incorporating auxiliary regressors, these methods improve estimation efficiency, providing practical tools for semi-supervised causal inference.

Abstract: This study investigates semiparametric efficient estimation of causal and structural parameters in a semi-supervised setting. In our setting, unlabeled auxiliary regressors are available in addition to labeled observations consisting of outcomes and regressors. Our goal is to construct estimators of causal and structural parameters whose asymptotic variances are smaller than those of estimators constructed using only labeled data. We refer to this framework as prediction-powered causal inference (PPCI). We first derive the efficient influence function and the efficiency bound, which imply that the use of auxiliary regressors can attain a smaller asymptotic variance than the efficiency bound attainable from labeled observations alone. Then, by combining the efficient influence function with the debiased machine learning (DML) framework, we propose methods that we call DML-PPCI. If we construct an estimating-equation estimator, we refer to the method as EE-DML-PPCI; if we construct a targeted-learning estimator, we refer to the method as TMLE-DML-PPCI. The asymptotic variances of both estimators match our derived efficiency bound. In the construction of the estimators, estimation of the efficient influence function plays an important role. In our study, the efficient influence function is also a Neyman orthogonal score, which depends on the Riesz representer and the regression function. For Riesz representer estimation, we develop semi-supervised generalized Riesz regression with convergence rate guarantees.

</details>


### [439] [Robust State-Conditional Feature-Weighted Jump Models for Temporal Clustering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13146)
*Federico P. Cortese,Alessio Farcomeni*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces a robust feature-weighted jump model for time-dependent clustering with smooth transitions and state-specific feature relevance, validated via simulations and real-world datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address the need for a robust and smooth time-dependent clustering approach that can weigh features differently across states and cope with outliers.

Method: The proposed model integrates a penalty for smooth transitions, a Tukey's biweight loss function for robustness, and a parameter for feature-weight variability.

Result: The method accurately recovers cluster sequences, identifies relevant features, and outperforms competing models, especially when outliers are present.

Conclusion: The approach is effective and reliable for time-dependent clustering and has real-world utility demonstrated through conflict-related and macroeconomic applications.

Abstract: We propose a robust feature-weighted jump model for time-dependent clustering. A penalty is used to encourage smoothness of transitions over time, while robustness is achieved through the use of a Tukey's biweight loss function. An additional parameter controls the variability of feature weights across states, allowing the model to assign state-specific relevance to each feature. We illustrate in simulation how the method accurately recovers the true cluster sequence and reliably identifies relevant features, outperforming competing approaches, particularly in the presence of outliers. We conclude with two empirical applications, one on the number of conflict-related homicides in Kosovo in the period 1998-2000, and another on macroeconomic performance of twelve European countries in the period 1949-2024.

</details>


### [440] [ProtoX-AD: Self-Explainable Time Series Anomaly Detection and Characterization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13277)
*Aitor Sánchez-Ferrera,Elisabeth Wetzer,Kristoffer Wickstrøm,Michael Kampffmeyer,Robert Jenssen*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: ProtoX-AD is a self-explainable framework for detecting anomalies in time series data, offering both accuracy and prototype-based insights.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite the effectiveness of self-supervised classification methods in time series anomaly detection, current models suffer from a lack of explainability about flagged anomalies.

Method: ProtoX-AD uses transformation-aware latent representations and interpretable prototypes to enhance both anomaly detection and explanation capabilities.

Result: ProtoX-AD performs comparably to black-box methods in anomaly detection but provides more consistent and meaningful explanations.

Conclusion: ProtoX-AD bridges the gap between performance and explainability in time series anomaly detection. Its explainable design enables systematic exploration of transformations' impact on detection.

Abstract: Recent advances in time series anomaly detection (TSAD) have highlighted the effectiveness of self-supervised classification-based approaches. These methods apply transformations to normal training samples, training a classifier to recognize transformation-specific patterns that help identify anomalies through increased classification errors. Despite their strong performance, a significant challenge is their lack of explainability, as they provide limited insight into the characteristics of flagged anomalies. To address this limitation, we propose ProtoX-AD, a prototype-based self-explainable framework for self-supervised TSAD. ProtoX-AD learns transformation-aware latent representations alongside interpretable prototypes, enabling both accurate anomaly detection and the identification of distinct anomalous profiles through prototype-based explanations. Additionally, it allows for systematic analysis of how transformation design impacts detection performance and explainability. Experimental results on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that ProtoX-AD achieves detection performance comparable to its black-box counterparts while offering more consistent and semantically meaningful explanations than existing explainable baselines. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/Aitorzan3/ProtoX-AD.

</details>


### [441] [Simultaneous Latent Budget Trees for Stratified Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13295)
*Simultaneous Latent Budget Trees for Stratified Classification Cristian Buoncompagni,Stefano Pellegrino,Giulia Vannucci,Roberta Siciliano*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper proposes Simultaneous Latent Budget Trees (SLBT), a probabilistic framework for classification trees accounting for stratification factors like time or demographics, which is applied to understand gender-related differences in ALS disease progression.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of standard tree growth procedures in properly accounting for stratification factors and to provide a more interpretable, explainable tree-based classification framework in fields requiring nuanced analysis, such as healthcare.

Method: Introduced a model-based split rule where child nodes are latent components of a simultaneous mixture model. Parameters are estimated using least squares with a neural network perspective, and tools for visualization and tree selection are developed.

Result: SLBT offers an interpretable classification tree framework that accounts for stratification factors and offers metrics for handling unbalanced responses. It successfully applied to analyze gender differences in ALS progression.

Conclusion: The SLBT framework advances interpretable classification trees by incorporating stratification factors and robust explanatory tools, proving useful for applications like healthcare research. A corresponding SLBT library is made available.

Abstract: In the era of Explainable Artificial Intelligence, there is a renewed focus on single trees for their ease of interpretation. This paper introduces Simultaneous Latent Budget Trees, a probabilistic machine learning framework for classification trees in the presence of a stratification factor such as a temporal, spatial, or demographic variable, acting as a control variable or potential confounder. Standard tree growth procedures are not designed to optimize a conditional split rule. A model-based split rule is proposed in which child nodes are interpreted as latent components of a simultaneous mixture model, such as the Simultaneous Latent Budget Model and its constrained versions, fitted to the parent node. Mixing parameters drive the observations, differently for each group, to the child nodes whereas latent budgets parameters update the response classes profile of each level of the control variable. Parameters are estimated by least squares considering a neural network perspective of the model. An informative tree structure can be interactively visualized with interpretation aids on the node and the paths, including visual pruning and decision tree selection procedure. Suitable measures are proposed to handle an unbalanced response class distribution. The proposed methodology is applied to investigate gender-related differences in disease progression of Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis. The SLBT library with the various tree-based algorithms is available in the linked GitHub repository.

</details>


### [442] [Majority-of-Three is Optimal](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13614)
*Divit Rawal,Nikita Zhivotovskiy*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper proves the optimality of majority vote among three classifiers in the PAC setting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to simplify the algorithmic structure and probabilistic analysis of voting learners.

Method: They provide a short proof focusing on three independent consistent classifiers in a realizable PAC setting.

Result: The majority vote of three classifiers is proven to be an optimal learner in the PAC framework.

Conclusion: A simplified and optimal solution for voting learners has been established, improving past algorithms and analyses.

Abstract: We give a short proof that the majority vote of three independent consistent classifiers is an optimal learner in the realizable PAC setting. This proves optimality for the simplest voting scheme, while simplifying both the algorithmic structure and the probabilistic analysis of previous voting learners, including the algorithm of S. Hanneke and the analysis of bagging by K. Green Larsen.

</details>


<div id='physics.comp-ph'></div>

# physics.comp-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [443] [REMAL: Residual Equilibrium Manifold Active Learning for Surrogate-Based Multidisciplinary Design Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13245)
*Kail Yuan,Ashwin Renganathan*

Main category: physics.comp-ph

TL;DR: The paper introduces REMAL, a surrogate modeling framework addressing the high computational cost of solving equilibrium states in multidisciplinary coupled system design through Gaussian process models and active learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation stems from the high computational cost of solving mutual consistency of coupling variables at each design point in multidisciplinary design, especially in tasks like optimization, uncertainty quantification, or digital twin updating.

Method: REMAL uses a multitask Gaussian process to model the joint residual manifold of coupled systems, along with an entropy-based active learning strategy to refine its predictions. Equilibrium states are computed via a nonlinear least-squares optimization using the surrogate.

Result: REMAL was tested on several engineering benchmarks, showing consistent cost-effectiveness by reducing the need for iterative fixed-point evaluations.

Conclusion: REMAL offers a theoretically sound and practical approach for efficient equilibrium state computation in coupled systems, demonstrating accuracy and reduced computational costs across diverse benchmarks.

Abstract: Multidisciplinary design analysis of coupled engineering systems requires the computation of equilibrium states in which all disciplinary coupling variables are mutually consistent. Conventional fixed-point iteration resolves this consistency problem separately at each design point, which can become expensive when disciplinary evaluations are costly and many analyses are required in outer-loop tasks such as multidisciplinary design optimization, uncertainty quantification, or digital twin updating. This paper introduces REMAL, a residual manifold surrogate modeling framework for coupled systems. Instead of approximating each discipline independently or directly learning converged coupling variables, the proposed method learns a surrogate model of the joint residual manifold via multitask Gaussian process models. An entropy-based active learning strategy selects additional residual evaluations near uncertain zero-contour regions, and equilibrium states for new design inputs are recovered by solving a nonlinear least squares optimization problem using only the trained surrogate. The method is evaluated on four engineering coupled system benchmarks: a satellite model, an aerostructural model, a finite-element gas-turbine heat-transfer and economics model, and a modified turbine model with added feedback coupling. Across these cases, REMAL consistently demonstrates the cost effectiveness when repeated evaluations of the fixed point across the design space are necessary. Theoretically, we show that, under mild assumptions, REMAL's predictive fixed point error is bounded.

</details>


### [444] [Feature-preserving Latent-EnKF for Data Assimilation of Flows with Shocks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12559)
*Hemanth Chandravamsi,Hangchuan Hu,Ponkrshnan Thiagarajan,Tamer A. Zaki*

Main category: physics.comp-ph

TL;DR: The paper addresses issues in the ensemble Kalman filter (EnKF) when handling solutions featuring discontinuities, such as shocks, by introducing a latent-space-based method to preserve sharp features and avoid spurious oscillations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The EnKF struggles with discontinuities like shocks in compressible flows because the associated multimodal statistics violate the Gaussian assumption, leading to errors in analysis states.

Method: The authors propose a latent-EnKF that updates in a latent space where sharp features like shocks are represented smoothly. The latent state is then decoded back into physical space using a shared decoder.

Result: Numerical experiments on shock-related problems showed that the method accurately preserves sharp features (shocks and discontinuities) and avoids spurious oscillations, even with sparse, noisy observations.

Conclusion: The feature-preserving latent-EnKF improves the ability to handle shocks and discontinuities in data assimilation tasks, addressing limitations of traditional EnKF methods effectively.

Abstract: The ensemble Kalman filter (EnKF) is widely adopted for sequential data assimilation, but fails for solutions with discontinuities, such as shocks in compressible flows. Uncertainty in shock location induces multimodal ensemble statistics that violate the Gaussian assumptions underlying the EnKF, producing large-scale spurious oscillations in the analysis state. We introduce a feature-preserving latent-EnKF that performs the ensemble update in a learned low-dimensional latent space, where shock and flow features admit a smooth manifold representation, thereby preserving sharp features during EnKF analysis. The updated latent state is mapped back to physical state through a shared decoder for all ensemble members. The algorithm eliminates the member-specific ordered training and positivity flooring used in prior approaches. Numerical experiments on a Sod shock tube and Mach 2 shock interaction with a 2D cylinder, using sparse and noisy observations, show accurate feature recovery of shocks and contact discontinuities without spurious oscillations.

</details>


<div id='q-bio.QM'></div>

# q-bio.QM [[Back]](#toc)

### [445] [OCOO-T : A Simple and Scalable Virtual Cell Model for Transcriptional Perturbation Response Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12838)
*Danning Jiang,Zheming An,Yalong Zhao,Lipeng Lai*

Main category: q-bio.QM

TL;DR: The paper presents OCOO-T, a minimalist Transformer-based model for predicting single-cell transcriptional responses, offering scalability and simplicity while achieving state-of-the-art performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of predicting transcriptional responses to various perturbations in single-cell biology while reducing architectural complexity in existing methods.

Method: The authors propose OCOO-T, a Transformer-based architecture using continuous-time denoising and adaptive layer normalization to predict transcriptional responses, integrating perturbation and cell-type specificity directly.

Result: OCOO-T demonstrates state-of-the-art performance across diverse datasets and cell types, effectively scaling to large transcriptional profiles using a patching and depatching mechanism.

Conclusion: OCOO-T provides an efficient and scalable alternative for single-cell omics simulation, leveraging the simplicity and effectiveness of a Transformer stack with denoising processes.

Abstract: Predicting single-cell transcriptional responses to genetic, chemical and cytokine perturbations is a fundamental challenge in computational biology and AI Virtual Cell (AIVC) modeling, with direct implications for drug discovery and the elucidation of gene regulatory networks. Existing approaches often rely on auxiliary cell-state encoders, hierarchical variational autoencoders, dedicated Transformer encoder-decoder modules, or gene-interaction priors to compress high-dimensional expression profiles into latent representations. While effective, these designs increase architectural complexity and may limit scalability and generalizability. This paper introduces OCOO-T, a minimalist flow-matching-based AIVC model for transcriptional perturbation response prediction. OCOO-T utilizes a vanilla Transformer stack that operates directly on continuous gene expression profiles and formulates perturbation response prediction as a continuous-time denoising process. Perturbation embeddings, dosage information, and cell-line/cell-type specificity are integrated through adaptive layer normalization and in-context tokens. Comprehensive evaluations on Tahoe100M, Replogle, and PBMC benchmarks demonstrate that OCOO-T achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse perturbations and cell types while effectively scaling to long transcriptional profiles through patching and depatching of cellular contexts. By leveraging the simplicity of Transformer-based denoising for single-cell omics, OCOO-T provides an effective and scalable framework for in-silico cellular simulation.

</details>


<div id='cs.DS'></div>

# cs.DS [[Back]](#toc)

### [446] [A unified complexity bound for logconcave sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12694)
*Yunbum Kook,Santosh S. Vempala*

Main category: cs.DS

TL;DR: The paper provides an improved and nearly optimal approach for sampling logconcave distributions using In-and-Out algorithms with exponential lifting, achieving faster convergence rates in both constrained and well-conditioned settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address the challenge of efficiently sampling from logconcave distributions, which is crucial in various applications like optimization and probabilistic inference.

Method: They use the In-and-Out algorithm combined with exponential lifting and introduce an enhanced analysis leveraging improved bounds on the Poincaré constant.

Result: The approach demonstrates nearly tight convergence rates for sampling from logconcave distributions, including constrained (e.g., Gaussian on convex bodies) and well-conditioned scenarios.

Conclusion: The paper establishes a unified framework with nearly optimal bounds for sampling logconcave distributions that significantly improve upon prior methods in terms of convergence rates.

Abstract: We give a simple, unified, and nearly tight bound for sampling arbitrary logconcave distributions from a warm start using the In-and-Out algorithm along with exponential lifting. The main new ingredient in the analysis is an improved bound on the Poincaré constant of a lifted distribution. As a consequence, the resulting convergence rate is nearly tight for both constrained settings (e.g., Gaussian restricted to a convex body) and well-conditioned settings (e.g., strongly logconcave and smooth densities).

</details>


### [447] [Diffusion-Network Alignment: An Efficient Algorithm and Explicit Probability Bounds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12879)
*Ziao Wang,Lei Ying*

Main category: cs.DS

TL;DR: This paper introduces and solves the problem of aligning rooted diffusion trees from communication traces to online or offline social networks using local neighborhood tree correlation tests.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenge of aligning networks with information asymmetry, such as diffusion trees and social networks, which are partially observed.

Method: Proposes an efficient algorithm based on tree correlation tests to match vertices between rooted diffusion trees and networks, analyzing its performance in sparse graph regimes.

Result: The algorithm aligns all vertex pairs correctly with high probability and provides depth-dependent probability lower bounds for correct matches.

Conclusion: The approach is effective in addressing diffusion-network alignment problems and provides performance guarantees on vertex matching accuracy, especially closer to the tree root.

Abstract: This paper studies a variation of the classic network alignment problem, named diffusion-network alignment. The goal is to align the vertices of a rooted diffusion tree to the vertices of a network, where the diffusion tree could be from a communication trace or contact tracing, and the network could be an online or offline social network. Different from the classic network alignment where both networks are fully observed, this model captures the information asymmetry of two networks. To solve this problem, this paper presents an efficient algorithm based on tree correlation tests to extract alignment information from local neighborhoods. We analyze the performance of the algorithm in the sparse graph regime and show that with high probability, all matched pairs are correct. Furthermore, for each vertex on the diffusion tree, this paper establishes an explicit lower bound on the probability that the vertex is correctly matched. These lower bounds are depth-dependent and increase as vertices get closer to the root.

</details>


### [448] [Learning-Augmented Approximation for Unrelated-Machines Makespan Scheduling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13133)
*Kaito Baba,Evripidis Bampis,Giorgos Mitropoulos*

Main category: cs.DS

TL;DR: This paper extends Antoniadis et al.'s prediction-based framework to the makespan minimization scheduling problem (R||Cmax) and achieves a smooth trade-off between prediction accuracy and worst-case performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to explore whether Antoniadis et al.’s framework, which approximates NP-hard selection problems using predictions, can be generalized to problems like scheduling, which are outside the class of selection problems.

Method: The authors developed a learning-augmented algorithm for makespan minimization (R||Cmax), which uses predictions of heavy job assignments. The algorithm provides a (1+ε)-approximation with accurate predictions and degrades to a 2-approximation in the worst case when prediction errors increase.

Result: The solution achieves polynomial-time performance with a graceful degradation from a near-optimal solution to a robust 2-approximation in cases of high prediction error.

Conclusion: The approach demonstrates the feasibility and effectiveness of generalizing prediction-based frameworks to scheduling problems, validated by empirical experiments.

Abstract: Recently, Antoniadis et al. (ICLR 2025) proposed a framework for incorporating predictions to approximate NP-hard selection problems. Despite its simplicity, this approach tightly matches theoretical lower bounds, making its generalization highly compelling. We address an open question raised in the work of Antoniadis et al., concerning the extension of this approach to other important problems outside the class of selection problems, such as scheduling. We develop a learning-augmented algorithm for the makespan minimization problem on unrelated machines, denoted by $R\|C_{\max}$. By using predictions of heavy job assignments, we achieve a polynomial-time $(1+\varepsilon)$-approximation for accurate predictions that smoothly degrades to a worst-case 2-approximation as the error increases. We conclude our work with an empirical analysis of our method.

</details>


<div id='physics.optics'></div>

# physics.optics [[Back]](#toc)

### [449] [Optical Implementation of Equilibrium Propagation Using Spatial Photonic Ising Machines](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13454)
*Dimitri Vanden Abeele,Daniele Veraldi,Davide Pierangeli,Claudio Conti,Serge Massar*

Main category: physics.optics

TL;DR: The paper demonstrates the hybrid optical-digital implementation of Equilibrium Propagation using Spatial Photonic Ising Machine, experimenting with datasets for energy-efficient machine learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore an energy-efficient physical implementation of Equilibrium Propagation for energy-based networks.

Method: The study uses a Spatial Photonic Ising Machine (SPIM) and optically encodes neuron states through gauge transformation, leveraging digital inference via finite difference schemes. Datasets like Wine and MNIST are used for benchmarks.

Result: The experimental system successfully applied SPIM on the Wine dataset and numerical evaluations on MNIST demonstrated the potential for more complex applications.

Conclusion: This approach shows a promising path toward energy-efficient computing frameworks leveraging optical systems for machine learning applications.

Abstract: Equilibrium Propagation offers a compelling alternative to traditional machine learning for training energy-based networks. Here we demonstrate a hybrid optical-digital implementation of EP using a Spatial Photonic Ising Machine (SPIM). The SPIM exploits the gauge transformation method to optically encode both continuous neuron states and rank-1 binary trainable patterns as phase modulations via a spatial light modulator, with inference realized using a finite difference scheme. The experimental system is evaluated on the Wine classification dataset. The potential of this approach, including the use of continuous couplings and structured coupling matrices, is evaluated numerically on the more complex MNIST dataset. Our work provides a concrete pathway toward energy-efficient physical implementations of Equilibrium Propagation.

</details>


<div id='eess.IV'></div>

# eess.IV [[Back]](#toc)

### [450] [Acquisition state behaves as a structured, measurable variable governing lung-nodule AI: kernel-driven measurement instability and noise-driven detection fragility, invisible to DICOM metadata](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12824)
*Daniel Soliman*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: The paper highlights the necessity of monitoring acquisition variables in medical imaging AI, revealing their influence on model reliability and proposing a methodology for acquisition-aware validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing guidelines for AI governance in medical imaging emphasize output monitoring but overlook acquisition-level variations, which may affect AI reliability.

Method: The authors used a LUNA16-trained MONAI RetinaNet lung-nodule detector to evaluate AI performance under controlled perturbations in medical image acquisition, testing variables such as reconstruction kernels.

Result: They found that acquisition variations significantly impact AI performance along distinct axes, although acquisition state identification is achievable with pixel fingerprinting techniques.

Conclusion: Incorporating acquisition-aware validation into AI acceptance testing and monitoring processes is essential for robust AI governance in medical imaging.

Abstract: AI governance for medical imaging is formalizing: the 2026 ACR-SIIM Practice Parameter recommends local acceptance testing and ongoing drift monitoring, and the ACR Assess-AI registry monitors AI outputs using DICOM metadata for context. We argue that a necessary, currently unmonitored layer sits beneath output metrics: whether incoming studies remain within the acquisition envelope a model was validated on. Using a LUNA16-trained MONAI RetinaNet lung-nodule detector, we test whether acquisition state behaves as a structured, measurable variable. On real paired CT differing only in reconstruction kernel (NLST B30f vs B80f), kernel alone shifted AI-measured diameter and flipped a Fleischner size category in 5.2% (8 of 155) of nodules at fixed patient and acquisition, while detection confidence was unchanged (Wilcoxon p=0.22). Under controlled LIDC-IDRI perturbations the effects dissociated by axis: the noise axis degraded detection confidence (p=5.9e-32, concentrated in nodules under 6 mm) but not measurement, while the frequency/kernel axis corrupted measurement (p=8.6e-13) but not detection. A 4-feature pixel fingerprint recovered reconstruction identity (patient-level AUC about 0.95 on real CT, 0.995 on a QIBA phantom) where the ConvolutionKernel DICOM tag was uninformative (identical labels across reconstructions). The kernel axis transported across four manufacturers (leave-one-vendor-out AUC 0.94-0.98, matching the within-vendor ceiling). Acquisition state thus maps to distinct AI failure modes, frequency content to measurement reliability and noise to detection sensitivity, and is not recoverable from metadata. Acquisition-aware, input-side validation is the missing layer for the acceptance-testing and drift-monitoring requirements now entering imaging-AI accreditation.

</details>


<div id='cs.HC'></div>

# cs.HC [[Back]](#toc)

### [451] [Exploring How Agent Voice Accents Shape Human-AI Collaboration in K-12 Group Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12805)
*Prerna Ravi,Carúmey Stevens,Ben Hurt,Brandon Hanks,Grace Lin,Emma Anderson*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The study examines how the accents (British, Indian, and African American) of GenAI conversational agents influence teachers' perceptions, trust, and group collaboration dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore how sociolinguistic design features such as accents in conversational AI influence agent perception, dynamics, and trust in group settings, particularly within collaborative learning.

Method: A between-subjects mixed-methods study with 33 teachers, analyzing the effects of GenAI voice agents with different accents on group collaboration, perceptions, and roles through surveys, interaction analysis, and artifacts.

Result: The British-accented agent was treated as a tool with utility-based engagement, while the Indian and African American-accented agents were more anthropomorphized and seen as peers, influencing trust, engagement, and reliance.

Conclusion: Accents in AI conversational agents significantly shape group interaction dynamics, mental models, and trust, with implications for designing culturally inclusive AI to support collaborative learning.

Abstract: Collaboration is widely recognized as a cornerstone of 21st-century education, yet teachers still encounter persistent challenges in fostering productive peer interaction. LLM conversational peer agents introduce new possibilities for mediating in-person group work, raising questions about how persona design, particularly their voice characteristics, shapes learners' perceptions, trust, and interactional dynamics. While prior work has examined agent accent effects in one-to-one settings, little is known about how these effects manifest in groups. We conducted a between-subjects mixed-methods study with 33 teachers examining how a GenAI voice agent with different accents (British, Indian, and African American) influenced collaboration and agent perception. Across surveys, group interaction analyses, and artifacts, we find that accent shaped participants' mental models and the roles the agent assumed in group interaction. The British-accented agent was largely treated as a tool and engaged in detached, utility-based ways, whereas Indian- and African American-accented agents were more readily anthropomorphized and integrated as peers. These role expectations influenced trust, engagement, and reliance over time. This work advances understanding of how GenAI's sociolinguistic design features shape group dynamics in CSCL, with implications for designing culturally inclusive AI partners in group learning.

</details>


### [452] [Ride, Track, and Recover: Pilot Randomized Trial of a Wearable Digital Self-Management Intervention During a Veteran Endurance-Cycling Program](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13529)
*Alan Ta,Nilsu Salgin,Caleb Armstrong,Kala Phillips Reindel,Farzan Sasangohar*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The study used smartwatch sensing and digital tools to address PTSD symptoms in veterans, finding that digital interventions improved symptom stabilization compared to physical activity alone or control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to explore efficient ways to monitor and manage PTSD symptoms in veterans, especially hyperarousal and associated conditions, outside traditional clinical settings.

Method: Veterans were grouped into three cohorts—digital intervention with physical activity, physical activity only, and at-home control. Smartwatches monitored hyperarousal events, and weekly surveys assessed symptoms. Generalized additive mixed models analyzed symptom trajectories.

Result: The digital intervention group showed stabilization of hyperarousal and better symptom maintenance compared to the other groups. Symptom severity influenced the perceived accuracy of the wearable detections.

Conclusion: Digital self-management tools, paired with wearable technology, have the potential to improve PTSD symptom stabilization and highlight the need for personalized and user-centered designs for mental health systems.

Abstract: Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) in veterans is characterized by persistent hyperarousal and comorbid anxiety and depressive symptoms that are difficult to monitor and manage outside clinical settings. Thirteen veterans participating in a Project Hero cycling event in Texas were randomized by computer-generated sequence in a naturalistic setting to two arms: (1) digital intervention plus physical activity, or (2) physical activity only, plus a third at-home monitoring control cohort consisting of 7 veterans selected from the broader Project Hero veteran community. Continuous smartwatch sensing combined heart rate and accelerometer features to detect hyperarousal events, which were confirmed in real time by participants. Weekly self-report measures of anxiety, depression, and PTSD severity were collected. Generalized additive mixed models characterized nonlinear trajectories over time. Baseline-normalized hyperarousal trajectories differed significantly across conditions, with the digital intervention group (n=7) showing structured stabilization compared to late-study escalation in the physical-only group (n=3). Both cycling groups exhibited acute symptom improvements during the endurance event; however, the digital intervention group demonstrated a higher overall maintenance of gains. The at-home control group (n=4) showed gradual symptom declines. Perceived precision of ML detections varied substantially across individuals and was positively associated with symptom severity, with higher-severity participants confirming a greater proportion of detected events. These results suggest that coupling wearable detection with digital self-management tools may support stabilization of hyperarousal and symptom improvement while emphasizing the importance of personalization and human-centered design in wearable mental health systems.

</details>


### [453] [Mod-Guide: An LLM-based Content Moderation Feedback System to Address Insensitive Speech toward Indigenous Ethnic and Religious Minority Communities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13397)
*Dipto Das,Achhiya Sultana,Ankit Singh Chauhan,Saadia Binte Alam,Mohammad Shidujaman,Shion Guha,Sunandan Chakraborty,Syed Ishtiaque Ahmed*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: This paper explores how language models can better recognize culturally insensitive speech impacting minority communities in Bangladesh and proposes an improved moderation tool called Mod-Guide.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of ensuring that large language models effectively recognize and moderate culturally insensitive speech, focusing on underrepresented minority perspectives.

Method: The authors co-created a culturally grounded corpus with minority communities in Bangladesh and employed retrieval augmented generation (RAG) to integrate this data into moderation pipelines, improving sensitivity to minority viewpoints.

Result: The Mod-Guide tool, enhanced with RAG, demonstrated improved context accuracy and sensitivity to minority perspectives, as validated through mixed-method evaluations.

Conclusion: By embedding restorative justice and hermeneutical inclusion into content moderation frameworks, the work highlights the importance of centering minority lived experiences for socially aware AI systems.

Abstract: Language operates as a mechanism of both marginalization and resistance, especially for minority communities navigating insensitive and harmful speech online. As content moderation increasingly depends on large language models (LLMs), concerns arise about whether these systems can recognize culturally insensitive speech-language that disregards or marginalizes the cultural and religious perspectives of historically underrepresented communities, often through implicit erasure, misrepresentation, or normative framing, rather than overt hostility. Focusing on Bangladesh's Hindu and Chakma communities -- the country's largest religious and Indigenous ethnic minorities, respectively -- this paper investigates the epistemic limits of LLM-based moderation systems and explores methods for incorporating minority perspectives. We co-created a culturally grounded corpus of insensitive speech with community members and integrated their narratives into moderation pipelines using retrieval augmented generation (RAG). Our tool, Mod-Guide, improves LLM sensitivity to minority viewpoints by leveraging contextual cues derived from lived experience. Through mixed-method evaluations involving both minority and majority participants, we demonstrate that RAG-enhanced moderation responses are more contextually accurate and perceived differently across ethnic lines. This work advances research in human-computer interaction, AI ethics, and social computing by foregrounding restorative justice and hermeneutical inclusion in the design of content moderation systems.

</details>


<div id='eess.SY'></div>

# eess.SY [[Back]](#toc)

### [454] [MPC for underactuated spacecraft control with a Lyapunov supervised physics-informed neural network correction layer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13113)
*Amirhossein Ayanmanesh Motlaghmofrad,Carlo Cena,Mauro Martini,Marcello Chiaberge*

Main category: eess.SY

TL;DR: The paper introduces a hierarchical control architecture to address challenges in underactuated spacecraft attitude control, using NMPC, a physics-informed neural network (PINN), and a Lyapunov-based safety mechanism. The approach improves accuracy and robustness in simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation stems from the limitations of conventional controllers in stabilizing underactuated spacecraft due to controllability challenges, environmental disturbances, and uncertainties such as inertia variations and unmodeled dynamics.

Method: A three-layer hierarchical system: (i) Nonlinear Model Predictive Controller (NMPC) for planning and stability under constraints, (ii) a Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) to estimate disturbance torques, and (iii) a Lyapunov-based safety layer to supervise and maintain system stability.

Result: Simulation results demonstrate improved tracking performance and reduced steady-state attitude errors with robust behavior under uncertain conditions. The supervisory layer ensures reliability by managing learning-based augmentations.

Conclusion: The proposed architecture effectively combines model-based and learning-based approaches to enhance attitude control in underactuated spacecraft while ensuring robustness and safety under various uncertainties.

Abstract: Underactuated spacecraft faces controllability limitations and heightened sensitivity to environmental disturbances, complicating attitude maneuvering and stabilization. Due to the lack of control authority along the underactuated axis, conventional controllers cannot directly stabilize all attitude components and therefore require reference planning strategies. Furthermore, MPC approaches remain sensitive to inertia uncertainty and unmodeled dynamic couplings, resulting in degraded tracking performance under mismatch. To address these issues, we consider a hierarchical architecture integrating three layers: (i) a nonlinear model predictive controller (NMPC) for constraint and underactuation-aware maneuver planning and nominal closed-loop stability under actuator limits; (ii) a physics-informed neural network (PINN) trained offline on simulation data to estimate residual disturbance torques, with loss terms that enforce consistency with rigid-body rotational dynamics; (iii) a Lyapunov-based supervisory safety mechanism that evaluates the learned correction online and bounds or suppresses its influence to preserve the stability properties of the baseline controller. The architecture is evaluated in a high-fidelity simulation environment modelling reaction wheel dynamics, actuator saturation, and environmental disturbances. Monte Carlo studies show statistically significant reductions in steady-state attitude error relative to standalone NMPC while maintaining robust behavior under uncertainty. The supervisory layer ensures graceful degradation to purely model-based control when the learning-based augmentation is unreliable.

</details>


### [455] [Agentic MPC for Semantic Control System Resynthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12774)
*Yuya Miyaoka,Masaki Inoue*

Main category: eess.SY

TL;DR: This paper introduces an agentic MPC framework that integrates large language model-based agents for context-aware control synthesis, assessed in autonomous driving scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional MPC systems inadequately handle high-level contextual information like social norms or user intent.

Method: Integrating heterogeneous inputs (e.g., natural language, observations) into an MPC framework via large language models.

Result: Showcased effectiveness in autonomous driving, enhancing the system’s ability to align with personal preferences and social situations.

Conclusion: Integrating large language models enables MPC systems to dynamically incorporate contextual and adaptive control capabilities.

Abstract: While MPC effectively handles structured, diverse, and low-level specifications, it lacks the capability to dynamically incorporate high-level contextual information such as social norms, user intent, or natural language instructions. To address this limitation, this manuscript introduces an agentic MPC framework that enables context-aware, semantically adaptive control synthesis by integrating with large language model-based agents. The agent interprets heterogeneous inputs, including natural language messages, environmental observations, and external knowledge, to resynthesize the control specifications. The effectiveness of the framework is demonstrated in an autonomous driving scenario, where the system aligns with personal preferences or responds to social situations such as emergency vehicle yielding.

</details>


### [456] [Aerial Wildfire Suppression Planning with a Hybrid CNN-Cellular Automata Fire Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13633)
*Ion Matei,Maksym Zhenirovskyy,Takuya Kurihana,Rohit Vupala,Anthony Wong*

Main category: eess.SY

TL;DR: This research develops a model for planning aerial wildfire suppression effectively using neural-cellular automaton simulations and uncertainty analysis to optimize intervention strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance the effectiveness of aerial wildfire suppression strategies by integrating predictive fire behavior models with robust optimization techniques under operational and environmental uncertainties.

Method: The authors developed a hybrid neural-cellular automaton model for wildfire spread prediction and combined it with a gradient-based optimization framework for determining aerial drop actions. They incorporated suppression effects for both water and retardant resources and evaluated strategies under both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties using Monte Carlo sampling and spatially correlated error analysis.

Result: The framework produced coherent and effective aerial suppression schedules, demonstrated through a case study on the 2020 Bear Fire. It successfully evaluated the uncertainty and reduced the total fire-affected area in simulations.

Conclusion: The presented approach enhances decision-making in aerial wildfire suppression by improving predictive accuracy, designing effective interventions, and accounting for uncertainties in environment and operations.

Abstract: Aerial wildfire suppression requires not only predicting fire spread, but also designing effective intervention strategies under operational and environmental uncertainty. We present a modeling and optimization framework for aerial wildfire suppression that combines a hybrid neural-cellular automaton wildfire model with gradient-based design of targeted aerial drops. The wildfire model predicts spatially varying spread behavior from terrain, fuel, and wind data, while the intervention module determines binary drop actions with continuous-valued location and orientation parameters mapped to the simulation grid. Water and retardant are represented with distinct suppression effects, corresponding to immediate reduction of active burning and persistent reduction of future spread. To evaluate the robustness of the resulting suppression plans, we quantify both aleatoric uncertainty through Monte Carlo sampling of daily fire-state realizations and epistemic uncertainty through spatially correlated prediction-error perturbations. A case study based on the 2020 Bear Fire shows that the framework can generate coherent aerial suppression schedules for reducing total fire-affected area and can support uncertainty-aware analysis of wildfire intervention strategies.

</details>


<div id='hep-ex'></div>

# hep-ex [[Back]](#toc)

### [457] [AgentRivet: an automated system for producing Rivet routines from journal publications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13535)
*Antonio J. Costa,Caterina Doglioni,Christian Gütschow,Andrew D. Pilkington,Sukanya Sinha*

Main category: hep-ex

TL;DR: The paper develops AgentRivet, an automated workflow based on Large Language Models to generate missing Rivet routines for collider experiments, addressing incomplete analysis preservation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Incomplete analysis coverage, with only 39% of collider measurement routines documented, limits model validation and development.

Method: AgentRivet, a workflow using Large Language Models, extracts physics analysis data from papers and generates Rivet routines with built-in quality control steps.

Result: AgentRivet produced accurate and functional Rivet routines, though challenges arose due to ambiguous definitions and complex observables in publications.

Conclusion: AgentRivet is a promising tool for automating Rivet routine generation, but further refinement is needed to handle physics complexities and ambiguities.

Abstract: Particle physics collider experiments provide Rivet routines as part of the analysis preservation strategy for model-independent measurements. Rivet is a C++ toolkit that allow new theoretical models to be compared to the measurements, thus aiding the development and tuning of Monte Carlo event generators as well as searches for physics beyond the Standard Model. However, analysis coverage is known to be incomplete, with only 39% of measurements having documented and publicly available Rivet routines. In this article, we design and implement an automated workflow based on Large Language Models with the goal of providing the missing routines. This multi-step workflow, referred to as AgentRivet, extracts the physics analysis information from published papers and writes the missing Rivet routines, with intermediate code- and physics- reviews as part of an autonomous quality control. We report the results obtained using commercial Large Language Models, provided by OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google, for two recent measurements from the ATLAS and CMS experiments. We find that AgentRivet produces competent Rivet routines with few syntax errors. The physics fidelity of the routines is reasonable and follows the explanations given in the relevant publications. Nevertheless, physics-implementation issues do arise and are investigated using the artefacts produced by AgentRivet. The majority of physics implementation issues arise from subtle-but-ambiguous definitions in the given publication, although some models struggle to implement complex observables even when clear definitions are given.

</details>


<div id='cond-mat.dis-nn'></div>

# cond-mat.dis-nn [[Back]](#toc)

### [458] [A solvable model for unsupervised federated learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13045)
*Giovanni Catania,Aurélien Decelle,Gianluca Manzan,Beatriz Seoane,Daniele Tantari*

Main category: cond-mat.dis-nn

TL;DR: A theoretical framework examines federated learning using a teacher-student structure, showing that interaction among students improves learning under varied noise and data conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how interaction among learners affects federated learning in noisy generative data settings and to derive optimal learning conditions.

Method: Theoretical analysis using equilibrium disordered system tools and numerical simulations, involving a teacher-student model under different data realizations and noise levels.

Result: Interactions among students lead to better performance: noisy students need fewer samples, while lower-noise students achieve stronger results. Optimal Bayesian conditions for recovery were derived.

Conclusion: Distributed interactions enhance learning in generative frameworks, with clear links to equilibrium sampling in structured Boltzmann Machines.

Abstract: We introduce a theoretical framework for analyzing federated learning in a generative setting through a teacher-multiple interacting students scenario, in which each student receives a distinct realization of the data, either through a different noise corruption or by accessing a different subset, possibly of varying size. Using theoretical tools in equilibrium disordered system, we analytically show that interactions among students systematically enhance learning performance: highly noisy students require fewer samples to recover the underlying pattern, while low-noise students achieve a larger overlap with the ground-truth signal. We derive the optimal Bayesian conditions for teacher recovery as functions of the sample complexity, noise level, and interaction strength, and validate these predictions through numerical simulations. The resulting dynamics can be mapped onto equilibrium sampling in a Restricted Boltzmann Machine with a structured hidden layer, providing a principled theoretical understanding of how interactions improve distributed generative modeling.

</details>


<div id='cs.SI'></div>

# cs.SI [[Back]](#toc)

### [459] [To Share or Not to Share: Orchestrating Trustworthy Data in Global Value Chains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12788)
*Han-Teng Liao,Chang-Yi Kao*

Main category: cs.SI

TL;DR: This paper proposes a reference architecture for semiconductor value chains to ensure compliance and sustainability under the EU CBAM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper is motivated by the structural tensions in the global semiconductor value chain caused by CBAM requirements and the need for tools to reconcile data sovereignty and environmental compliance.

Method: Utilization of the International Data Spaces (IDSA) framework to manage environmental telemetry, paired with methodologies for transforming vulnerabilities into systemic benefits and roadmaps for compliance.

Result: The study outlines the adoption of regulatory mechanisms like Digital Product Passports and the integration of Agentic AI to streamline compliance and sustainability in semiconductor-petrochemical ecosystems.

Conclusion: The proposed framework demonstrates how sovereign data exchange and AI can ensure that global industrial clusters achieve compliance with CBAM while fostering sustainability and transparency.

Abstract: As the EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) approaches, the global semiconductor value chain faces growing structural tensions between regulatory transparency and data sovereignty. This article proposes a RegTech reference architecture using the International Data Spaces (IDSA) framework to orchestrate trustworthy environmental telemetry across the semiconductor-petrochemical nexus. The framework distinguishes the mandatory CBAM requirements from voluntary Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi) frameworks, while addressing the additive complexities of the Safe-and-Sustainable-by-Design (SSbD) framework. Moving beyond standard linear technology stacks, we introduce a prospective roadmapping methodology that transforms upstream physical vulnerabilities into circular, negative feedback loops. Focusing on the Taipei and Penang technology corridor, the article details how sovereign data exchange enables Digital Product Passports (DPPs) to drive Global Business Services (GBSs) capability demands. Finally, we discuss the integration of Agentic AI for autonomous compliance and FinTech green financing, providing a scalable blueprint for global industrial clusters to achieve sovereign, sustainable, and transparent value chains.

</details>


### [460] [Graph Reduction in Multirelational Networks: A Spreading-Oriented Reduction Benchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12581)
*Mateusz Stolarski,Michał Czuba,Piotr Bielak,Piotr Bródka*

Main category: cs.SI

TL;DR: The paper introduces SORB, a framework for studying how graph reduction affects influence maximization models on various networks and tasks, revealing that reduction impacts predictive performance differently based on network type and task.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study is motivated by the challenges of analyzing large, incomplete, noisy, and evolving networks, particularly in how graph reduction affects influence maximization accuracy—a topic that has been underexplored.

Method: They propose and implement the Spreading-Oriented Reduction Benchmark (SORB), a robust framework for systematically evaluating influence maximization models, incorporating graph reduction into the analysis pipeline.

Result: The results demonstrate that the effect of graph reduction varies depending on the type of network (single-layer or multilayer) and the task, with reduction having distinct implications for seed set quality and ranking degradation.

Conclusion: Reduction-aware, multi-task evaluation is crucial for understanding spreading processes in complex networks, with outcomes strongly influenced by network structure and task specifics.

Abstract: Real-world networks are inherently incomplete, noisy, and dynamically evolving, making it difficult to capture all actors and their relationships. Their scale often renders direct analysis computationally demanding. While influence maximisation (IM) has been widely studied, the role of graph reduction as a preprocessing step, and its impact on IM accuracy, remains underexplored. In this work, we introduce the Spreading-Oriented Reduction Benchmark (SORB), an open-source, standardised framework for systematically evaluating IM models across diverse task settings. SORB provides an extensible pipeline operating on a representative collection of real-world networks, including single- and multilayer structures, and accounts for graph reduction directly into the evaluation process. This design shifts the focus from analysing IM algorithms in isolation to quantifying how graph reduction alters predictive performance. Using SORB, we study the effects of sparsification and coarsening across multiple IM scenarios. Our results show that the impact of reduction is strongly dependent on both the network type (single-layer vs. multirelational) and the downstream task ($Gain@k$ vs. $\mathrm{AUC}_{\mathrm{cutoff}}$): sparsification preserves seed set quality on single-layer networks, whereas flattened multilayer networks exhibit systematic ranking degradation regardless of reduction strategy. These findings highlight the importance of reduction-aware, multi-task evaluation when studying spreading processes in complex networks.

</details>


<div id='stat.AP'></div>

# stat.AP [[Back]](#toc)

### [461] [Estimating Individualized Treatment Effects in Acute Ischemic Stroke with Causal Transformation Models (TRAM-DAG): A Multi-Centre Observational Study with External RCT Validation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12623)
*Oliver Dürr,Lisa Herzog,Pascal Bühler,Susanne Wegener,Beate Sick*

Main category: stat.AP

TL;DR: The study explores using causal transformation models (TRAM-DAG) to estimate individualized treatment effects (ITE) in stroke care by leveraging both observational data and clinical trial validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to enhance acute ischemic stroke care by moving from average treatment effects (ATE) to individualized treatment effects (ITE), helping clinicians identify which patients benefit most from mechanical thrombectomy.

Method: The researchers use causal transformation models (TRAM-DAG) on observational data from a stroke patient dataset. They train the model on a subset that aligns with clinical trial criteria and validate it by comparing its ITE estimates to results from the MR CLEAN trial.

Result: The ITE estimates generated by TRAM-DAG align with the average treatment effects of the MR CLEAN trial and successfully rank patients by their observed outcomes, demonstrating the model's validity for personalized decision-making.

Conclusion: TRAM-DAG models can effectively estimate ITEs, providing a crucial tool for personalized medicine in stroke care. This approach bridges observational data and clinical trial findings for better treatment decisions.

Abstract: Personalized medicine in acute ischemic stroke requires moving beyond average treatment effects (ATE) to individualized treatment effect (ITE) estimates to support treatment decisions. In acute ischemic stroke, mechanical thrombectomy has been shown to be more effective on average than lysis in randomized controlled trials (RCTs), such as the MR CLEAN study. We aim to identify which individual patients benefit most from mechanical thrombectomy compared to lysis. The outcome of interest is the modified Rankin Scale (mRS) at three months, an ordinal measure of functional disability (0: no symptoms, 6: death). We demonstrate that causal transformation models on directed acyclic graphs (TRAM-DAG) can be used for ITE estimation after being fitted on observational MAGIC multi-center stroke patient data. To ensure comparability with the MR CLEAN population, which we use for validation, we train the TRAM-DAG on a MAGIC sub-population with NIHSS at admission >= 6, corresponding to one inclusion criterion of MR CLEAN. The fitted model is then used to estimate ITEs for stroke patients in the MR CLEAN population. While these ITE estimates cannot be confirmed experimentally, we show that their average is consistent with the trial's reported ATE. Furthermore, the ITE estimates correctly rank trial patients by their observed frequency of a good outcome (mRS at three months <= 2). These findings support the use of causal models like TRAM-DAG for personalized decision-making in stroke care and highlight their ability to bridge the gap between observational evidence and clinical trials.

</details>


<div id='math.OC'></div>

# math.OC [[Back]](#toc)

### [462] [A Communication Complexity Lower Bound for Nonuniformly Convex Consensus Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12675)
*Demyan Yarmoshik,Maxim Klimenko*

Main category: math.OC

TL;DR: The paper studies the communication complexity of decentralized convex optimization over dynamic networks, establishing new lower bounds using spectral graph theory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate the communication cost required for decentralized optimization where nodes in a time-varying network collaboratively minimize a global objective with limited information exchange.

Method: The authors derive a new lower bound for communication rounds using mathematical analysis and embed time-rotating star gadgets into expander graphs while preserving spectral properties.

Result: They prove a lower bound of $Ω(χ_{\mathcal G} \sqrt{κ_g}\,\log(n/χ_{\mathcal G})\log(1/\varepsilon))$, revealing a gap in round complexity between uniform and nonuniform regimes.

Conclusion: This work establishes fundamental limits on communication rounds for decentralized optimization, highlighting the role of spectral properties of the network in determining performance.

Abstract: We study the communication complexity of convex decentralized optimization over time-varying networks, where $n$ nodes hold private functions and must agree on the global minimizer using only synchronous exchanges with neighbors. The cost is the number of communication rounds to reach accuracy $\varepsilon$ -- a measure akin to round complexity in the LOCAL model, but constrained by nodes sharing only oracle responses. We prove a new lower bound of $Ω\!\left(χ_{\mathcal G} \sqrt{κ_g}\,\log\frac{n}{χ_{\mathcal G}}\log\frac1\varepsilon\right)$ communication rounds, where $χ_{\mathcal G}$ is the condition number of the network Laplacians and $κ_g$ that of the global objective, showing the round complexity attainable under uniform regularity cannot be matched in the nonuniform regime. The construction rests on spectral graph theory: we embed time-rotating star gadgets into the edges of an expander and patch them to preserve spectral connectivity.

</details>


### [463] [Distribution-Agnostic Robust Trajectory Optimization via Chance-Constrained Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13605)
*Yashdeep Chaudhary,Roberto Armellin,Harry Holt,Marco Sagliano*

Main category: math.OC

TL;DR: The paper introduces a robust trajectory-optimization framework using chance-constrained reinforcement learning, evaluated on two trajectory design problems involving space and rocket dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a distribution-agnostic framework for robust trajectory optimization that can handle uncertainties in initial conditions and process noise, applicable across various trajectory design problems without redesign.

Method: A two-step process: offline computation of a deterministic nominal trajectory followed by reinforcement learning to robustify it with structured affine closed-loop correction laws. Probabilistic feasibility is enforced through empirical rollout-based quantiles and covariance-feasibility penalties.

Result: The framework was tested on a 3D Earth-Mars transfer and a stochastic atmospheric pinpoint rocket landing. It demonstrated competitive fuel costs while preserving probabilistic feasibility, and proved adaptable across different trajectory planning problems.

Conclusion: This framework is robust, scalable, and portable across diverse trajectory-optimization problems, enabling effective stochastic control under uncertainty.

Abstract: This paper presents a distribution-agnostic robust trajectory-optimization framework based on chance-constrained reinforcement learning. The uncertainty is represented here through initial conditions and process noise, with the only requirement being that it can be sampled. A deterministic nominal trajectory is first computed offline, and reinforcement learning is then used only to robustify that baseline through a structured affine closed-loop correction law comprising a feedforward control adjustment and time-varying feedback gains. Probabilistic feasibility is enforced empirically through rollout-based upper-tail quantiles, while terminal dispersion is regulated through covariance-feasibility penalties. The framework is assessed on two materially different trajectory design problems. The flagship case study is a three-dimensional multi-impulse Earth-Mars transfer, where the learned policy is benchmarked against a recent robust trajectory-optimization reference under Gaussian uncertainty and then evaluated under bounded uniform uncertainty and under process disturbances not seen during training. The second case study is a stochastic atmospheric pinpoint rocket landing problem, used to assess portability to a short-horizon continuous-thrust setting with drag, mass depletion, and glide-slope constraints. The results show that the proposed framework can remain competitive in upper-tail fuel cost while preserving probabilistic feasibility, and that the same robustification scaffold can be carried across heterogeneous spacecraft trajectory planning problems without redesign of its core stochastic-control structure.

</details>


<div id='cs.ET'></div>

# cs.ET [[Back]](#toc)

### [464] [Modern analog computing for solving differential and matrix equations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13179)
*Zhong Sun,Piergiulio Mannocci,Manuel Le Gallo,Abu Sebastian*

Main category: cs.ET

TL;DR: Modern analog computing, driven by applications like AI and scientific computing, focuses on three computational primitives: differential equations, matrix equations, and matrix-vector multiplications. It explores advances in analog CMOS circuits and resistive memory technologies, with resistive memory arrays showing notable promise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need for efficient computation in data-intensive applications like AI and scientific computing drives renewed interest in analog computing, particularly with modern circuit and memory advancements.

Method: The study identifies three key computational tasks (differential equations, matrix equations, and matrix-vector multiplication) and assesses their implementation through analog CMOS circuits and resistive memory arrays, analyzing strengths, weaknesses, and practical applications.

Result: Resistive memory arrays emerge as efficient hardware for analog computing tasks, with notable progress in solving differential and matrix equations. Issues like precision, scalability, and computational complexity are discussed alongside solutions.

Conclusion: Analog computing, leveraging advances in circuits and memory technology, holds strong potential to address next-generation computational challenges, with unique advantages and certain hurdles to overcome.

Abstract: In recent years, driven by the computational demands of data-intensive applications such as artificial intelligence and scientific computing, analog computing has gained renewed interest. Given the diversity of computational tasks and recent advancements in analog CMOS circuits and resistive memory technologies, we refer to the evolving landscape as modern analog computing. In this context, we identify three core computational primitives: solving differential equations, solving matrix equations, and performing matrix-vector multiplications, and we explore the connections among them. We also examine various hardware implementations of these analog computing operators, including those built with discrete components, integrated circuits, and resistive memory devices. Among these, resistive memory arrays emerge as particularly promising due to their implementation efficiency. The paper then surveys recent progress in leveraging modern analog computing to solve differential and matrix equations using both advanced analog CMOS circuits and resistive memory arrays. Finally, we discuss the applications of these circuits, the precision and scalability issues and their potential solutions, the relationship with in-memory computing, and the unique computational complexity of analog computing. This paper provides a unified perspective on analog computing, highlighting its strengths, current developments, and challenges, and positioning it as a pivotal enabler of next-generation computational frontiers.

</details>


<div id='cs.SD'></div>

# cs.SD [[Back]](#toc)

### [465] [AudioX-Turbo: A Unified Framework for Efficient Anything-to-Audio Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12555)
*Zeyue Tian,Lei Ke,Zhaoyang Liu,Ruibin Yuan,Liumeng Xue,Yujiu Yang,Weijia Chen,Xu Tan,Qifeng Chen,Wei Xue,Yike Guo*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: The paper introduces AudioX-Turbo, a framework for flexible multimodal audio generation, achieving high-quality results with significantly reduced computational cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges in multimodal audio generation—such as frameworks for unified modeling, lack of quality training data, and inefficient multi-step diffusion sampling.

Method: The proposed framework, AudioX-Turbo, employs a teacher-student paradigm where a teacher model (AudioX-Base) uses multimodal diffusion and adaptive fusion modules for synthesis, and the student model is distilled for efficiency. A new dataset, IF-caps-Pro, supports this training.

Result: AudioX-Turbo delivers superior performance in text-to-audio and text-to-music generation, with only 4 sampling steps, requiring 25x fewer evaluations than traditional methods.

Conclusion: AudioX-Turbo efficiently handles multimodal audio generation, achieving high quality and flexibility while reducing computational demands. It shows significant promise for multimodal audio tasks.

Abstract: Audio and music generation based on flexible multimodal control signals is a widely applicable topic, with the following key challenges: 1) a unified multimodal modeling framework, 2) large-scale, high-quality training data, and 3) the prohibitive inference cost of multi-step diffusion sampling. As such, we propose AudioX-Turbo, a unified and efficient framework for anything-to-audio generation that integrates varied multimodal conditions (i.e., text, video, and audio signals) in this work. AudioX-Turbo follows a teacher-student paradigm. The teacher AudioX-Base is built on a Multimodal Diffusion Transformer with a Multimodal Adaptive Fusion module that aligns diverse multimodal inputs for high-fidelity synthesis, and is then distilled into the few-step student AudioX-Turbo via Distribution Matching Distillation adapted to flow matching, complemented by a diffusion-based discriminator for high-quality few-step generation. To support the training of AudioX-Turbo, we construct a large-scale, high-quality dataset, IF-caps-Pro, comprising approximately 9.2M samples curated through a two-stage data collection and annotation pipeline. We benchmark AudioX-Turbo across a wide range of tasks, finding that our model achieves superior performance, especially on text-to-audio and text-to-music generation, while operating at only 4 sampling steps and requiring approximately 25x fewer function evaluations (NFE) than multi-step baselines. These results demonstrate that our method is capable of audio generation under flexible multimodal control, showing efficient and powerful instruction-following capabilities. The code and datasets will be available at https://zeyuet.github.io/AudioX-Turbo/.

</details>


### [466] [BASENet: Band-Adapted Speech Enhancement Network with Cross-Band Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12662)
*Damien Martins Gomes,François Capman*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: The paper introduces BASENet, a speech enhancement model prioritizing frequency-adapted architecture to align with human auditory perception.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the limitations of uniform capacity in speech enhancement models, as they do not align with the human ear's non-uniform spectral resolution.

Method: BASENet partitions the spectrum into Bark-scale bands, assigning scaled-capacity encoders to each band. It incorporates a cross-band attention module for harmonic dependencies and builds on inverted residual blocks with convolutional recurrent networks.

Result: BASENet achieves 3.55 PESQ and ~96% STOI using minimal parameters (0.83M) and computation (7.3 G~MACs). A causal variant with 3.44 PESQ surpasses many other non-causal models.

Conclusion: BASENet demonstrates effective and efficient speech enhancement while accommodating auditory perceptual differences, making it viable for real-time, resource-limited scenarios.

Abstract: Speech enhancement models typically apply uniform capacity across all frequencies, disregarding the non-uniform spectral resolution of human hearing. We propose BASENet, a frequency-adapted architecture that partitions the spectrum into Bark-scale bands and assigns each a scaled-capacity encoder derived from critical-band density, automatically granting deeper branches to perceptually dense low frequencies and lighter ones to high frequencies.
